name: Home
title: Home
description: Landing page to start chalet searches, access the store, manage existing bookings, view favorites, and see homepage promotions.

core_features: Load default chalet search values (check-in, check-out, adults, children) using getChaletSearchDefaults and prefill the quick search form.
Provide a quick chalet search form that lets users set dates and guest counts, optionally adjust basic filters, and navigate to chalets.html with those values as URL parameters.
Load homepage promotional content (featured chalets, featured store bundles, seasonal offers) using getHomepagePromotions and display each set in its own section.

Provide navigation shortcuts to the store: one generic 'Store' entry tile (store.html) and tiles for primary store sections (Groceries & Essentials, Winter Gear, Fireplace & Comfort, Gift Cards) that navigate to store.html with the appropriate categoryId URL parameter.
Provide shortcuts to manage an existing booking (navigate to manage_booking.html) and to view favorite chalets (navigate to favorites.html).
Handle loading and error states for getChaletSearchDefaults and getHomepagePromotions so the page can show or hide dependent components appropriately.
user_workflows:
Start a chalet search from Home:
1) User lands on Home; onPageLoad triggers getChaletSearchDefaults.
2) When defaults are returned, the quick search form fields (check-in, check-out, adults, children) are prefilled.
3) The user adjusts dates and guest counts as needed and optionally sets basic filters (e.g., pet friendly, near ski lifts, minimum bedrooms).
4) The user submits the form; the component validates dates and counts.
5) If validation passes, the page navigates to chalets.html with URL parameters checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, and any selected filter parameters appended.

Use the store from Home:
1) User sees the store navigation tiles section.
2) If the user clicks the generic Store tile, the page navigates to store.html with no additional parameters.
3) If the user clicks the Groceries & Essentials tile, the page navigates to store.html with categoryId set to grocery_essentials.
4) If the user clicks the Winter Gear tile, the page navigates to store.html with categoryId set to clothing_accessories.
5) If the user clicks the Fireplace & Comfort tile, the page navigates to store.html with categoryId set to fireplace_comfort.
6) If the user clicks the Gift Cards tile, the page navigates to store.html with categoryId set to gift_cards.

Manage an existing booking from Home:
1) User locates the booking shortcuts component.
2) The user clicks the Manage booking shortcut.
3) The browser navigates to manage_booking.html, where the user can identify and modify a booking.

View favorite chalets from Home:
1) User locates the booking shortcuts component.
2) The user clicks the View favorites shortcut.
3) The browser navigates to favorites.html, where favorited chalets are handled.

View homepage promotions:
1) On page load, the homepage-init-controller triggers getHomepagePromotions.
2) While data is loading, the promotional sections can display a loading state.
3) Once data is returned, the featured-chalets-list, featured-store-bundles-list, and seasonal-offers-list components render the respective arrays.
4) If getHomepagePromotions fails, each dependent list component shows an error state and may display a retry action that re-invokes getHomepagePromotions via the controller.
interactions: User edits quick search fields (check-in, check-out, adults, children): onChangeField events update the in-memory search state used to construct the chalets.html URL on form submission.

User toggles basic chalet filters (e.g., pet-friendly, within a distance to ski lifts, minimum bedrooms): onChangeFilter events update the filter portion of the search state used when building the chalets.html URL.
User submits the quick search form: onSubmit validates input and, on success, navigates the browser to chalets.html with the appropriate URL parameters.
User clicks the generic Store tile: onClickStoreAll navigates to store.html without extra URL parameters.
User clicks the Groceries & Essentials tile: onClickStoreGroceries navigates to store.html with ?categoryId=grocery_essentials.
User clicks the Winter Gear tile: onClickStoreWinterGear navigates to store.html with ?categoryId=clothing_accessories.
User clicks the Fireplace & Comfort tile: onClickStoreFireplaceComfort navigates to store.html with ?categoryId=fireplace_comfort.
User clicks the Gift Cards tile: onClickStoreGiftCards navigates to store.html with ?categoryId=gift_cards.
User clicks the Manage booking shortcut: onClickManageBooking navigates to manage_booking.html.
User clicks the View favorites shortcut: onClickViewFavorites navigates to favorites.html.
Page load triggers data fetching: onPageLoad calls getChaletSearchDefaults and getHomepagePromotions; their responses update shared state consumed by other components.
If getChaletSearchDefaults fails, the quick search form shows an error or empty state, and onClickRetrySearchDefaults can be used to call getChaletSearchDefaults again.
If getHomepagePromotions fails, the promotional list components show an error state with an onClickRetryPromotions interaction that re-invokes getHomepagePromotions via the controller.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Home page itself does not depend on incoming URL parameters; it initializes its state on page load using the getChaletSearchDefaults and getHomepagePromotions interfaces. The quick search form maintains an internal search state consisting of check-in date, check-out date, adults, children, and basic filters (e.g., pet-friendly flag, maximum ski-lift distance, minimum bedroom count). When the quick search form is submitted, this state is serialized into URL query parameters for chalets.html (e.g., checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, and optional filter parameters such as petFriendly, skiLiftMaxDistanceKm, minBedrooms). These parameters are not stored locally on Home; they are only present in the outgoing URL. Store navigation uses URL parameters to determine which store category to show: clicks on category tiles navigate to store.html with categoryId set to the relevant category key. The Manage booking and View favorites shortcuts navigate to their respective pages without additional URL parameters from Home. Loading and error flags for getChaletSearchDefaults and getHomepagePromotions are kept in the homepage-init-controller's in-memory state; they do not affect the URL. No user state is persisted via URL on the Home page itself beyond what is passed along when navigating to other pages.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading for the Home page and exposes shared state (search defaults, promotions, loading/error flags) to child components.
Displays and manages the chalet quick search form, prefilled with defaults from getChaletSearchDefaults, and navigates to chalets.html with the user's input as URL parameters.

Provides basic filter controls for the quick chalet search (such as pet-friendly, proximity to ski lifts, and minimum bedrooms) and updates the search state used when constructing the chalets.html URL.
Provides direct navigation buttons or links to manage an existing booking and to view favorite chalets.
Displays functional tiles for entering the store in general and for jumping directly to key store categories, each navigating to store.html with appropriate URL parameters.
Shows the list of featured chalets returned by getHomepagePromotions, including chalet name, image, starting price, rating, and review count, with loading and error states.
Displays featured store bundles from getHomepagePromotions, including bundle title, description, approximate total price, and included product type keys, with loading and error handling.
Displays seasonal offers returned by getHomepagePromotions, showing each offer's title and description, with support for loading and error states.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid (Bootstrap-style), with content constrained in a centered max-width container and nav elements arranged in columns (logo, nav links, cart) within the same grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strictly vertical, top-to-bottom document flow, mirroring the mockup: a full-width blue contact strip at the very top, then a full-width white navigation bar beneath it. Below the nav, all homepage components sit inside a single centered content column. Within this column, sections are stacked in a logical journey: first the chalet quick search form as the primary hero element, followed immediately by quick filters and booking shortcuts for utility, then store navigation tiles as a bridge into the e-store, and finally vertically stacked featured lists (seasonal offers, featured chalets, featured store bundles). No sidebars are used; all interactive and informational components align to the same central column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strictly vertical, top-to-bottom document flow, mirroring the mockup: a full-width blue contact strip at the very top, then a full-width white navigation bar beneath it. Below the nav, all homepage components sit inside a single centered content column. Within this column, sections are stacked in a logical journey: first the chalet quick search form as the primary hero element, followed immediately by quick filters and booking shortcuts for utility, then store navigation tiles as a bridge into the e-store, and finally vertically stacked featured lists (seasonal offers, featured chalets, featured store bundles). No sidebars are used; all interactive and informational components align to the same central column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top 10–15% of the viewport height is reserved for the combined contact strip and main navigation, consistent with the mockup. The remaining vertical space is dedicated to the main content column, which on desktop spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width (e.g., an 8–10 column span in a 12-column grid) and is centered, leaving 15–20% margin on each side. Key hero elements like the quick search form get slightly more vertical padding (32–48px above and below) to feel prominent, while subsequent sections use 24–32px gaps between them and 16–24px internal padding. On tablet and mobile, the content column expands closer to 100% width with consistent internal padding so the structure remains linear and uncluttered.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density follows a readable, document-like rhythm: each major section (search, filters, shortcuts, tiles, listings) is visually separated with 24–32px vertical spacing, while elements within a section (form fields, cards, list items) have 16–24px spacing. Line heights are generous to support readability of any text labels or card descriptions. There is no tight clustering of controls; even interactive groups like filters and shortcuts are arranged with breathing room, echoing the mockup’s legal-text readability and avoiding visual crowding.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts at the blue contact bar and immediately continues through the main navigation (logo left, nav center, cart right), establishing the corporate-brand context. Below this, attention is pulled to the centered quick search form that acts as the homepage hero. From there, the eye travels downward along the left-aligned content within the central column: first to the quick filters (fine-tuning searches), then to booking shortcuts (managing existing relationships), then to store navigation tiles (exploring the e-store), and finally to the sequential featured lists (seasonal offers, then featured chalets, then featured store bundles). Each section’s heading or label acts as a visual anchor, guiding users down the page in a predictable, linear reading flow.

layout.overall_description: Below the full-width blue contact bar and the clean white navigation bar, the homepage content sits in a single centered column that feels like a well-structured document but with interactive blocks instead of paragraphs. The first thing inside this column is a prominent, card-like chalet quick search form, centered and spanning about two-thirds of the desktop viewport, with ample white space above and below so it feels like the main hero. Directly beneath it, aligned to the same width and center, a compact band of quick filters lets guests refine their search without overwhelming the layout. Just below, a clearly separated row of booking shortcuts provides links to manage bookings, view favorites, and access account-related actions, maintaining a tidy horizontal alignment within the column. Slightly further down the page, a set of store navigation tiles appears as a neat, grid-like group of flat, clickable blocks, still confined to the central column and surrounded by generous white margins. Beneath these, the page transitions into a sequence of featured lists that read like structured sections of a document: first a seasonal offers list that surfaces current promotions, then a featured chalets list, and finally a featured store bundles list. Each list is introduced by a left-aligned heading with spacious margins, and each card or item within the lists spans the width of the content column with consistent vertical spacing. The result is a calm, modern, and highly readable layout where all components line up vertically, with no sidebars, and the user’s gaze moves naturally from top to bottom, guided by spacing and headings rather than heavy visual decoration.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The homepage-init-controller has no visible UI; it functions as a logical or technical wrapper for the page. Conceptually, place it at the top of the main content area, immediately below the main navigation bar and above all other homepage components in the DOM order. It occupies no real visual space and does not disrupt the vertical layout; instead, it ensures that the subsequent components (starting with the chalet quick search form) are initialized and arranged within the single centered content column. From a spatial perspective, treat it as a zero-height element anchored to the top of the main content container.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller; participates only in document flow and initialization logic, not in the visual layout. Does not affect spacing or positioning of other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The chalet-quick-search-form is the primary hero block in the main content column. It is placed directly below the navigation bar with a generous top margin of roughly 48–64px to clearly separate it from the header area. On desktop, it sits centered horizontally within the page, spanning about 60–70% of the viewport width (e.g., 8–10 columns in a 12-column grid) with equal left and right margins forming comfortable white space. The form is presented as a flat, white card with subtle resort branding (thin blue accent lines or labels), and internal padding of 24px on all sides, making the fields feel spacious. Form inputs are stacked vertically or in 2-column rows (e.g., dates side by side) but always respecting the text-focused, uncluttered aesthetic. A clear, full-width primary action button at the bottom aligns left within the card but visually reads as spanning the core of the form. Below the form, there is 32px of vertical space before the next component, maintaining the document-like section spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the central column, participating in normal document flow. On tablet and mobile, it expands toward 90–100% of the available width inside the content padding, remaining centered with consistent vertical margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The chalet-quick-filters component is positioned immediately below the chalet-quick-search-form, aligned to the same centered content column and width. It begins after a vertical gap of roughly 24–32px to distinguish it as a separate but closely related section. On desktop, the filters appear as a slim horizontal band of pill-style or flat buttons across the column, left-aligned with the form above, and allowed to wrap to a second line if there are many options. The band’s overall width matches the search form’s card footprint (about 60–70% of viewport width), but the background remains mostly white with minimal bordering to keep it lightweight and secondary in presence. There is approximately 16px of vertical padding inside this band and around 8–16px horizontal spacing between individual filter controls. A small left-aligned label such as “Quick filters” can sit above or inside the band, using the same typographic treatment as subheadings in the mockup. Below the filters, a 24–32px vertical gap separates this section from the booking shortcuts.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline with document flow. On smaller screens, filter controls stack or wrap gracefully while staying within the same central column, preserving ample vertical spacing between rows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-shortcuts component sits beneath the chalet-quick-filters within the same central column, separated by about 24–32px of vertical space. It is presented as a concise horizontal row of 3–4 shortcut links or buttons (e.g., “My bookings,” “Favorites,” “Account,” “Support”) that help returning users quickly access key areas. On desktop, each shortcut appears as a flat tile or text-plus-icon button occupying equal width segments inside the same 60–70% content width, aligned in a single row where possible. The group is left-aligned within the column but visually centered as a whole; internal horizontal spacing between tiles is around 16–24px, and vertical padding within each tile is about 16px. If there is a section label (such as “Quick access”), it appears left-aligned above the shortcuts with a 8–16px gap, similar in style to numbered subsection headings in the mockup but lighter in weight. Below this shortcuts row, maintain a 32px spacing before the store navigation tiles to keep the document flow clear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central column. On tablet and mobile, the shortcuts stack into two columns or a single column, preserving equal spacing between items and consistent alignment with the main content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The store-navigation-tiles component is positioned below the booking-shortcuts with a vertical gap of roughly 32px, continuing the linear flow inside the central column. It introduces the e-store visually through a small grid of flat tiles, each representing a store category or key destination (e.g., gear, bundles, gift cards). On desktop, the tile group still respects the main column width (about 60–70% of the viewport) and is centered; within this column, tiles are arranged in a 2- or 3-column grid depending on available width, with each tile occupying equal space and maintaining 16–24px gaps both horizontally and vertically. The section begins with a left-aligned heading such as “Explore the store,” styled similarly to other section headings and separated from the tile grid by 8–16px. Tiles themselves use white backgrounds with subtle blue accents and have internal padding of around 16–24px, ensuring they feel tap-able but not heavy. A vertical space of 32–40px below the last tile separates this section from the seasonal offers list that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, responsive grid inside the same central column. On smaller screens, the grid collapses to a 1- or 2-column layout, preserving the same vertical spacing and alignment with other sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The seasonal-offers-list appears directly below the store-navigation-tiles, separated by about 32–40px of vertical whitespace to signal a new, content-focused section. It uses the same centered content column width as previous components and reads like a structured document section: a bold, left-aligned heading introduces the section at the top of the column, followed by a short descriptive line (optional), and then a vertical list of offer items. Each seasonal offer is presented as a horizontally full-width card inside the column, with white background, subtle outline or shadow, and 16–24px internal padding. On desktop, offers can display a simple two-part layout within each card (e.g., text on the left, small image or badge on the right) but the card itself does not extend beyond the main column. The vertical spacing between offers is around 16–24px, maintaining the mockup’s text-block rhythm. The entire section reads as the first of several featured content blocks, so it is clearly delineated from what comes after with a consistent bottom margin of 32–40px before the featured chalets list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section within the central column, fully participating in normal vertical scrolling. On smaller screens, cards stack with all content in a single column inside each card, keeping text left-aligned and preserving generous vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-chalets-list sits immediately below the seasonal-offers-list, separated by a clear section gap of about 32–40px, and remains confined to the same centered column. It is treated as a major content anchor akin to a key numbered section in the mockup: a prominent left-aligned heading such as “Featured chalets” appears at the top of the section, with increased font weight and a 16–24px gap before the first chalet item. Each chalet is presented as a full-width card or row within the column, with balanced white space and a layout that might show a thumbnail image on the left and textual details on the right, but the overall bounding width adheres to the 60–70% main content width. Cards feature 16–24px internal padding and 16–24px vertical gaps between them, reflecting the document-like paragraph spacing. A maximum of a small number of featured items (e.g., 3–6) keeps the section from feeling crowded. The section as a whole is visually more prominent than the store-related content, anchoring the core accommodation offering, and is followed by 32–40px of vertical space before the featured store bundles section begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the document flow, with cards stacking vertically. On mobile, each chalet card becomes a single-column layout, placing image above text if needed, but still honoring the same spacing rhythm and central alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-store-bundles-list is placed below the featured-chalets-list, separated by a consistent 32–40px vertical gap, and continues the linear, document-like sequence of featured sections. The content remains within the same centered column, matching the width and alignment of previous sections. A left-aligned heading like “Featured bundles” introduces the section, styled similarly but slightly less visually dominant than the main chalet heading if desired. Below the heading, a vertical stack of bundle cards or rows fills the column. Each bundle card has a white background, subtle border or shadow, and 16–24px internal padding, mirroring the styling of the seasonal offers and chalet cards for visual cohesion. The cards may use a compact layout with text-heavy descriptions and smaller imagery, emphasizing clarity over visual spectacle. Vertical spacing of 16–24px between bundles and 24–32px at the bottom of the section maintains the readable rhythm, allowing the homepage to end in a calm, uncluttered way if there is no further content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically stacked within the central content column. On smaller screens, each bundle card expands to nearly full width inside the page padding, maintaining consistent margins and spacing.

name: Chalet Search & Results
title: Chalet Search & Results
description: Search for available chalets by dates and guests, refine with filters, sort the results, manage favorites, and open chalet detail pages.

core_features: Read incoming URL parameters for check-in, check-out, adults, and children to initialize the search context.
If URL parameters are missing, use getChaletSearchDefaults to pre-populate the date and guest fields, then sync them into the URL.
Load filter configuration (price ranges, amenities, bedrooms, distance to ski lifts) via getChaletFilterOptions and render dynamic filter controls.
Load sort options via getChaletSortOptions and allow users to pick a sort order (e.g., by price or rating).
Execute availability searches with searchAvailableChalets using the current dates, guests, filters, and sortBy, and render a chalet results list.

Display each chalet result with key attributes from searchAvailableChalets (name, effective price per night, total stay price, rating, review count, distance to lifts, bedroom count, amenity indicators, favorite status).
Allow users to favorite or unfavorite a chalet via toggleFavoriteChalet and immediately reflect the updated is_favorited state in the list.
On chalet card click, navigate to chalet_detail.html with the selected chalet id and current search context (checkIn, checkOut, adults, children) encoded as URL parameters.
Keep the page state (dates, guests, filters, sortBy, and optional pagination) fully represented in the URL so searches can be shared or reloaded without additional client storage.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Initial load without explicit search state
1. User opens chalets.html from the header “Find a chalet” link without URL parameters.
2. The page calls getChaletSearchDefaults.
3. The search form is initialized using the returned default_check_in, default_check_out, default_adults, and default_children.
4. The page writes these values into the URL parameters (checkIn, checkOut, adults, children) without reloading the page.
5. The page calls getChaletFilterOptions and getChaletSortOptions to render filter controls and available sort options.
6. The page assembles an initial filters object (e.g., empty or based on preset defaults from getChaletFilterOptions) and a default sortBy value, then calls searchAvailableChalets.
7. The returned chalets are rendered in the results list; is_favorited flags from the response are shown on each chalet card.
8. The user can now refine filters, change dates/guests, or change sorting, and the URL is updated accordingly before re-running searchAvailableChalets.

Workflow: Load a previously shared search via URL
1. User opens chalets.html with URL parameters such as checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, and optional filter/sort parameters (for example, maximum prices, selected amenities, bedroom options, distance limit, sortBy).
2. The page reads URL parameters and uses them to initialize the search form, filter controls, and selected sort option.
3. The page still calls getChaletSearchDefaults only as a fallback for any missing core search parameters, updating URL only for those missing values.
4. The page calls getChaletFilterOptions and getChaletSortOptions.
5. Filter controls are marked as selected based on the URL parameters (for example, matching amenity codes or bedroom thresholds).
6. The page builds a filters object from the URL parameters and calls searchAvailableChalets with checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, filters, and sortBy from the URL.
7. The user sees results consistent with the encoded URL state and can further modify the search; any change updates the URL and triggers a new search.

Workflow: Change dates and guests and re-run a search
1. User edits the check-in/check-out fields and/or the adults/children fields in the search form.
2. On form submission, the page validates that the new values are coherent (for example, checkOut is after checkIn, and guest counts are non-negative).
3. The page updates the URL parameters (checkIn, checkOut, adults, children) to the new values, preserving any existing filter and sort parameters.
4. The page constructs an updated filters object from the current filter controls and a sortBy value from the selected sort option.
5. The page calls searchAvailableChalets with the updated parameters.
6. The results list re-renders to show chalets available for the newly selected dates and guest configuration.

Workflow: Apply or adjust filters
1. User opens or interacts with the filter controls (price sliders or inputs, amenity checkboxes, bedroom options, and ski-lift distance controls).
2. When the user changes a filter (for example, adjusts a maximum nightly price or toggles a hot tub amenity), the filter component updates the internal filters object.
3. The filter component updates the URL to reflect the new filter state (for example, parameters for max nightly price, max total price, selected amenities, selected bedroom threshold, selected distance limit), without reloading the page.
4. The page calls searchAvailableChalets using the current checkIn, checkOut, adults, children from the URL and the updated filters object, keeping the existing sortBy.
5. The results list re-renders with chalets matching the updated criteria.
6. If the user clears filters, the filter component removes the corresponding URL parameters and triggers a new search with the reduced filters object.

Workflow: Change sort order
1. User opens the sort dropdown or control that was populated from getChaletSortOptions.
2. User selects a new sort option (one of the keys returned by getChaletSortOptions).
3. The sort component updates the sortBy parameter in the URL.
4. The page calls searchAvailableChalets with the existing dates, guests, and filters, and the new sortBy value.
5. The results list re-renders in the new order, while maintaining all other search constraints.

Workflow: Toggle a favorite chalet
1. User clicks the favorite icon/button on a chalet card in the results list.
2. The chalet card triggers toggleFavoriteChalet with the chalet_id for that result.
3. When the interface returns, the component updates that chalet’s is_favorited state using the returned is_favorited value, and may also update any global favorites count indicator using favorites_count.
4. The visual state of the favorite control on that card is updated to reflect the new status without re-running searchAvailableChalets.
5. If searchAvailableChalets is later called again, the returned is_favorited values from the backend will be used to re-sync the UI.

Workflow: Open a chalet detail page from results
1. User clicks anywhere on a chalet card outside of the favorite control (for example, the name, image, or a dedicated details link).
2. The results list component reads the chalet_id from that result item.
3. It also reads the current checkIn, checkOut, adults, and children from the URL (or from its internal state that mirrors the URL).
4. The page navigates the browser to chalet_detail.html with URL parameters id set to chalet_id and checkIn, checkOut, adults, children set to the current search values.
5. The detail page can then use these parameters to show availability and pricing specific to the selected stay.
interactions:
Interaction: Search form input changes
- User action: Typing or selecting new values in check-in, check-out, adults, or children fields.
- System reaction: The search form updates its internal state but does not immediately call searchAvailableChalets until the user submits the form (to avoid excessive calls). Optionally, a debounced auto-search could be triggered based on configuration; in either case, URL parameters are updated before calling searchAvailableChalets.

Interaction: Search form submission
- User action: Clicks a search button or presses Enter while focus is in the form.
- System reaction: The page validates the input, updates URL parameters for checkIn, checkOut, adults, and children, constructs or updates a filters object using the current filter controls, then calls searchAvailableChalets. Results list updates from the returned data.

Interaction: Filter control change
- User action: Adjusts price limits, toggles amenity selections, selects a bedroom count option, or adjusts distance-to-lifts settings.
- System reaction: The filter component updates its internal representation of filters, encodes these into URL parameters, then triggers searchAvailableChalets with unchanged dates, guests, and sortBy. Results list updates accordingly.

Interaction: Sort selection
- User action: Changes the selected sort option in the sort dropdown populated via getChaletSortOptions.
- System reaction: The selected sort key is written into the URL as sortBy, and searchAvailableChalets is called again with the same dates, guests, and filters. The order of the results list re-renders based on the new sortBy.

Interaction: Chalet card click
- User action: Clicks on a chalet card (excluding the favorite icon).
- System reaction: The component reads the chalet_id for that card and navigates to chalet_detail.html using a URL that includes id, checkIn, checkOut, adults, and children taken from the current page state/URL. No API call is required before navigation.

Interaction: Favorite toggle
- User action: Clicks the favorite icon/button on a chalet card.
- System reaction: The card component calls toggleFavoriteChalet with the chaletId of that card. It then updates the card’s is_favorited display state based on the interface response and optionally updates any favorites counter in the UI. No changes to filters or sorting occur.

Interaction: URL-driven state updates (back/forward navigation)
- User action: Uses browser back/forward buttons or manually edits URL parameters.
- System reaction: The page listens to URL changes, re-parses checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, filters, and sortBy from the URL, updates the search form and filter/sort controls to match, then calls searchAvailableChalets to synchronize the results with the new URL state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses the URL as the single source of truth for search state. On load and whenever the URL changes, the page parses parameters including checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, and additional parameters representing filters and sortBy. If any of the core search parameters (checkIn, checkOut, adults, children) are missing on initial load, the page calls getChaletSearchDefaults to obtain defaults, initializes the form with those values, and then writes them into the URL without reloading. Filter-related parameters (such as maximum nightly price, maximum total price, selected amenity codes, bedroom options, and a distance limit to ski lifts) are encoded into the URL as individual query parameters or a structured set that the page converts into the filters object passed to searchAvailableChalets. The sortBy parameter in the URL stores the currently selected sort option key returned by getChaletSortOptions. Optional pagination parameters can also be encoded within this URL or inside the filters object so that searchAvailableChalets can return the appropriate result subset; this page will always derive pagination state from the URL when present. At any user interaction that changes dates, guest counts, filters, sort order, or pagination, the page first updates the URL parameters to reflect the new state and then calls searchAvailableChalets with parameters derived from the updated URL and UI controls. No client-side storage such as localStorage is used for persisting search state; reloading or sharing the URL reproduces the same search because all necessary parameters are encoded in the query string.

components.functionality: Provides inputs for check-in date, check-out date, number of adults, and number of children, initializes them from URL or getChaletSearchDefaults, and triggers availability searches via searchAvailableChalets when submitted.

Displays dynamic filter controls for maximum nightly price, maximum total stay price, selectable amenities, bedroom options, and distance-to-ski-lifts presets. It builds and maintains a filters object and updates URL parameters and the current search whenever a filter changes.
Shows available sort options returned by getChaletSortOptions, maintains the selected sortBy key (also mirrored in URL), and triggers re-searches when the sort changes.

Summarizes the current search (showing the active date range, guest counts, and a description of active filters), and optionally shows the number of chalets returned by the last searchAvailableChalets call.

Renders the list of chalet results returned from searchAvailableChalets, passing each item’s attributes to chalet-result-card components. It also handles empty states when searchAvailableChalets returns no items.

For a single chalet result, shows the chalet name, short name (if available), price_per_night_for_dates, total_price_for_stay, rating, review_count, distance_to_ski_lifts_km, bedroom_count, amenity indicators, primary image, and favorite state. It routes clicks to chalet_detail.html and delegates favorite actions to the favorite-toggle-control.

Provides a clickable control (for example, an icon or button) to favorite or unfavorite a given chalet; calls toggleFavoriteChalet and updates the is_favorited state of the associated chalet result based on the response.

Allows the user to move between pages of results when searchAvailableChalets is configured to support pagination via the filters parameter. It reads and updates pagination-related values from/to the URL and triggers updated searches.

Centralizes reading and writing of URL parameters for checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, filter parameters, sortBy, and pagination. It listens for browser navigation events, updates components when the URL changes, and ensures all search-changing interactions update the URL before invoking searchAvailableChalets.

Manages loading and error states for calls to getChaletSearchDefaults, getChaletFilterOptions, getChaletSortOptions, searchAvailableChalets, and toggleFavoriteChalet, so the UI can show appropriate feedback while keeping functionality intact.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid (Bootstrap-style), with content constrained in a centered max-width container and nav elements arranged in columns (logo, nav links, cart) within the same grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the stacked, top-to-bottom hierarchy from the mockup: global contact strip, then main navigation bar, then a constrained central content area. Within the content area, the chalet search page shifts from a pure single-column document into a calm two-column layout: a narrow filter column on the left and a primary vertical content column on the right. The sequence within the primary column is: search criteria bar at the very top, then a compact summary/sort region, followed by the results list, with pagination at the bottom. State-related components (favorites, URL sync, error/loading) integrate into these visible pieces without adding visual clutter.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the stacked, top-to-bottom hierarchy from the mockup: global contact strip, then main navigation bar, then a constrained central content area. Within the content area, the chalet search page shifts from a pure single-column document into a calm two-column layout: a narrow filter column on the left and a primary vertical content column on the right. The sequence within the primary column is: search criteria bar at the very top, then a compact summary/sort region, followed by the results list, with pagination at the bottom. State-related components (favorites, URL sync, error/loading) integrate into these visible pieces without adding visual clutter.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The same centered container is used, occupying about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop with 15–20% whitespace margins left and right. The left filter sidebar consumes roughly 25–30% of this container width, and the right results column takes the remaining 70–75%, reinforcing the right column as the main reading area. Vertically, the search bar gets generous breathing room below the nav (24–32px). Each horizontal band (search, summary/sort, filters, results, pagination) follows the mockup’s spacing: 24–32px between major sections and around 16px between related elements within a section.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content remains moderately spacious and text-focused. Form fields, filter options, and chalet cards are not crammed; they use a comfortable 16–24px internal padding. Line height for text in summaries and cards is slightly relaxed to support scanning. Filters are grouped into clear blocks with 16–24px vertical spacing between groups, mirroring the mockup’s legal text sections. There are no competing side widgets, ads, or large decorative elements; the interface is kept visually quiet and functional.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow inherits the mockup’s top-down emphasis: eye moves from the blue contact strip to the white nav bar, then directly to the prominently placed search criteria bar. From there, the user’s attention is led down to the summary and sorting controls, then into a vertical scan down the right-hand results column. The filter sidebar forms a stable vertical anchor on the left but is visually secondary to the right column. Within each result card, the chalet title and image dominate, with the favorite toggle subtly in the top-right. Pagination closes the flow at the bottom, clearly signaling the end of the current result set.

layout.overall_description: Below the slim blue contact bar and the white main navigation, the chalet search page sits in a centered, white content container that spans roughly two-thirds of the screen width on desktop, framed by generous white margins. At the top of this container, a single, wide search criteria bar stretches nearly the full container width, creating a strong, functional header for the page’s content. Below it, the layout splits into two columns: a narrower, calm filter panel on the left and a taller, primary results column on the right. The results column begins with a concise summary of the current search and sorting options, then flows into a vertical stack of full-width chalet cards, each separated by comfortable whitespace. The left filter column runs parallel, aligned from just under the search bar down toward the lower portion of the page, but without visually dominating the screen. Error messages and loading states appear inline within the results area, while the URL/state logic remains invisible. The page ends with centered pagination controls beneath the last card, clearly marking the bottom of the content. The overall impression is clean, modern, and quietly branded: mostly white, accented with flat blue on key controls and headings, with a structured, document-like vertical rhythm that makes scanning and reading effortless.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search criteria bar sits directly below the main navigation bar as the first element in the main content container. It is horizontally centered and spans roughly 80–90% of the container width, leaving a slim margin of white space on both sides to prevent it from feeling wall-to-wall. Vertically, there is about 24–32px of white space between the bottom of the nav and the top edge of this bar, and another 24px before the content splits into two columns (filters and results). The bar itself has a low-profile, rectangular form with a lightly elevated or subtly bordered white background against the overall page white, helping it register as a distinct interactive section without feeling heavy. Inside, form fields (dates, guests, location) are arranged in a left-to-right row with equal height, following the mockup’s clean horizontal alignment. On wide screens, the action button (e.g., 'Search') is aligned to the far right of the bar. On narrower screens, the bar remains centered and may wrap into two rows, but it always maintains generous vertical padding (16–20px) inside, matching the mockup’s comfortable text area spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the page flow, attached to the top of the main content. On desktop, it defines the visual start of the search experience; on smaller screens, it remains full-width within the container and may stack fields, but it never becomes fixed or floating.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter sidebar begins just below the search criteria bar, aligned to the left edge of the main content container. It occupies roughly 25–30% of the container width on desktop, forming a vertical column that runs down the page alongside the results. The top of the sidebar is separated from the search bar by about 24px of vertical space, maintaining the mockup’s section spacing. The sidebar background remains white, with a subtle border or faint shadow to differentiate it from the page background without adding visual noise. Inside, filters are grouped into stacked sections (e.g., 'Price', 'Amenities', 'Rating'), each section header set in a slightly bolder style and separated from the next group by 16–24px of vertical space, echoing the numbered headings and paragraphs pattern from the mockup. The left margin of the filter labels aligns with the left margin of the results column’s text, creating a shared vertical rail. On mobile/tablet widths, this sidebar collapses above the results into a full-width, collapsible panel or a "Filters" button that expands inline, ensuring the layout returns to a single-column, document-like flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and large tablets, the sidebar behaves as a sticky column: its top edge locks a short distance (e.g., 16–24px) below the fixed nav when scrolling, so filters remain visible while the user scrolls the results. On small screens, it becomes a non-sticky, collapsible block stacked above the results to preserve vertical reading flow and avoid cramped horizontal layouts.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort bar is positioned at the top of the right-hand results column, directly to the right of the filter sidebar and beneath the search criteria bar and results summary. On desktop, it shares a single horizontal band with the results summary bar: the summary content is left-aligned, and the sort controls are right-aligned within the same row, together spanning the full width of the results column. This band sits about 16px below the search bar, forming a compact but clearly distinct control strip. The sort bar’s controls (e.g., dropdown for "Sort by" and order toggles) are minimal, with 8–12px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding inside, matching the navigation-style spacing. The background is white, using subtle flat blue accents only on active states or labels to keep the area refined and understated. On smaller screens, the sort controls wrap beneath the summary text but remain right-aligned within the column, maintaining a clean separation and hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the flow of the results column. It scrolls away with the content rather than sticking, emphasizing a simple, document-like scroll behavior consistent with the mockup, while keeping the search bar as the main top control.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results summary bar shares the same horizontal band as the sort bar at the top of the results column. It sits in the left portion of this band, roughly occupying the left 50–60% of the results column width. The summary text (e.g., "32 chalets found" and a concise restatement of dates/guests) is left-aligned, using a slightly stronger weight for the count and normal weight for the contextual details, similar to how section headings and intro sentences are handled in the mockup. There is about 16px of vertical padding within the band, with the baseline of the summary text aligning visually with the controls in the sort bar. The entire summary/sort band is separated from the first result card by about 24px, so the summary feels like a header for the result set. On mobile, the summary sits on the first line, full width, with the sort controls dropping below while preserving a clear top-down reading order.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the top of the results column. It scrolls away with the results list and does not stick, keeping the scroll behavior simple and predictable.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The chalet results list occupies the entire width of the right-hand column beneath the summary/sort band. It is a vertical stack of chalet result cards, each card spanning roughly 100% of the results column width minus consistent inner padding (16–24px). There is 24px of vertical space between consecutive cards, mirroring the spacing between subsections in the mockup’s legal text. The left edge of each card’s text aligns with the left edge of the summary text above, reinforcing a clean reading line. The list extends downward and becomes the primary scrollable content on the page; the filter sidebar visually travels alongside it on desktop. On tablet and mobile, once the layout drops to a single column, the list cards become full-width within the central container, preserving the same top-to-bottom reading pattern without side-by-side columns.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical stack in the main scroll flow. There is no infinite scrolling by default; the list is clearly bounded and paired with pagination at the bottom to align with a clear, document-like end state.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each chalet result card functions like a structured, mini-document block within the results list. It sits on a white background with a subtle border or light shadow and 16–24px of internal padding on all sides, matching the inferred card spacing in the mockup. The card stretches almost the full width of the results column, leaving only a small gutter to ensure it doesn’t visually touch the column edges. Within each card, a thumbnail image (or carousel preview) is positioned on the left or top (depending on viewport): on desktop, the image occupies roughly 30–35% of the card width on the left, with the chalet’s name, location, rating, price, and key attributes laid out in a neat vertical stack to the right. On narrower screens, the image sits above the text, returning to a simple top-down layout. The chalet name appears as the most prominent textual element, left-aligned and given extra spacing (12–16px) below it before secondary details. The favorite toggle control is anchored in the top-right corner of the card, slightly inset so it does not touch the card’s edge. The overall card design is clean and text-centric, with flat blue accents reserved for key interactive elements (links, price highlight, or primary action) to keep the design aligned with the clean corporate style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element within the results list. On hover (desktop), the card may exhibit a subtle elevation or border color change, but it does not move or float; it remains firmly in the vertical reading flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorite toggle control appears within each chalet result card as a small, discrete icon/button positioned in the top-right corner of the card content area. It is visually separated from the card edges by about 8–12px of internal padding and aligned vertically with the chalet name’s baseline or slightly above it, making it easy to spot without overpowering the card. The control is typically an outlined heart or bookmark icon that fills with flat brand blue when active, matching the minimal, flat accent style of the mockup. Its size is modest (roughly 24–32px touch target) so it is easily clickable but not dominant. It floats visually over the image/text layout using relative positioning within the card, but still respects the card’s padding and never breaks the clean rectangular shape of the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored within each card; it scrolls along with the card content. On desktop it remains at the top-right of the card; on mobile it may shift slightly but stays in the upper-right area of the card for consistency. It does not become sticky or global; it’s always local to its card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls are placed at the bottom of the results column, directly beneath the last chalet card with about 24–32px of vertical space separating them from the list. They are horizontally centered within the results column, reinforcing their role as the terminus of the current result set. The controls are presented as a clean, linear sequence of page numbers, with "Previous" and "Next" positioned to the left and right of the numbers or as simple chevrons, using flat blue for the active page and subtle borders or underlines for inactive states. The entire pagination strip fits comfortably within a single line on desktop, with around 8–12px of padding around each clickable element, consistent with navigation link spacing in the mockup. On smaller screens, the pagination adapts to show fewer explicit page numbers, potentially collapsing into previous/next plus current page, but it remains centered and occupies at most 2–3 compact rows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, defining the bottom of the primary content. It scrolls into view after the user reaches the end of the current results; it does not stick to the viewport edges, preserving the simple document-style scroll experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state sync manager operates entirely behind the scenes and does not present a visible UI component. Its effects are reflected indirectly in the layout: as users adjust the search criteria bar, change filters in the sidebar, modify sort options, or click through pagination, the URL updates gracefully in the browser’s address bar without page reload. This ensures that the current visual state of the page—active filters, sort order, current page of results, and dates/guests—is always shareable and restorable through the URL. The visual layout remains clean and uncluttered, with no additional bars, badges, or overlays introduced for this logic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; it has no spatial footprint in the layout. It modifies browser history and URL parameters in response to interactions with existing visible components while preserving the existing visual hierarchy and scroll behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error and loading handler occupies the same logical space as the chalet results list within the right-hand column. When the page is loading results (for an initial search, filter change, sort change, or pagination), a subtle full-width loading state appears where the list would be: either a set of skeleton cards matching the shape and spacing of chalet cards, or a single centered spinner with a short status message. This loading region starts directly below the summary/sort band and extends downward, preserving the familiar vertical rhythm. When an error occurs (e.g., network issue, no results found), a clean, centered message block appears in place of the list, maintaining the same horizontal alignment as the cards. The message uses concise, left-aligned text with a bold heading and a brief explanation beneath, similar in style to a numbered heading followed by a paragraph in the mockup. Optional action buttons (e.g., "Retry", "Clear filters") are placed below the message, center-aligned. The filter sidebar and search bar remain visible and unchanged, signaling that the user can adjust criteria even if the list failed to load.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contextual and inline with the results list area. It appears and disappears based on loading/error states without shifting the position of the surrounding search bar, filters, summary, or pagination. It does not overlay the entire viewport; instead, it replaces just the list region, maintaining the page’s calm, document-like flow.

name: Chalet Detail
title: Chalet Detail
description: Page for viewing detailed information about a specific chalet, configuring stay dates and guests, seeing pricing, and starting a booking or toggling favorites.

core_features: Load and display detailed chalet information (description, images, location, amenities, capacity, distance to ski lifts) using getChaletDetails.
Show stay pricing for the current date and guest selection (nightly and total, with taxes and fees) using getChaletDetails.
Allow users to adjust check-in/check-out dates and guest counts in place and refresh pricing accordingly.
Display overall chalet rating and review count to support comparison with other chalets.
Provide a prominent 'Book now' action that navigates to booking.html with the current chalet and stay configuration in URL parameters.
Let users add or remove the chalet from their favorites via a heart icon using toggleFavoriteChalet, and reflect the current favorite state.
user_workflows: View chalet details: User opens chalet_detail.html with an id parameter; the page calls getChaletDetails with any provided checkIn, checkOut, adults, and children parameters and displays chalet info, rating, capacity, amenities, images, and distance to ski lifts.

Refine stay configuration: User adjusts check-in and check-out dates and guest counts in the stay configuration form; the page validates input, updates the URL parameters, calls getChaletDetails again with the new values, and updates the pricing summary.

Start booking: After reviewing details and pricing, the user clicks 'Book now'; the page navigates to booking.html with chaletId set to the current chalet id and checkIn, checkOut, adults, and children set to the current active stay configuration.

Toggle favorite: User clicks the favorite (heart) icon; the page calls toggleFavoriteChalet with the current chaletId and updates the heart icon state and any favorite count indicator based on the response.

Return with pre-filled search context: User arrives from chalets.html with id, checkIn, checkOut, adults, and children in the URL; the page uses these parameters directly in the initial getChaletDetails call and shows pricing and guest counts already configured.
interactions: On initial page load, the page reads URL parameters (id, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children). If id is present, it calls getChaletDetails; if id is missing or invalid, it shows an error state instead of chalet content.

When the user changes check-in or check-out dates in the stay configuration form, the component validates the date range, updates internal activeCheckIn/activeCheckOut state, synchronizes the URL parameters, and calls getChaletDetails with the new values.
When the user changes the number of adults or children, the component updates activeAdults/activeChildren state, synchronizes the URL parameters, and calls getChaletDetails with the new guest counts.

When getChaletDetails returns, the page binds the chalet object to display components (header, description, amenities, media gallery, capacity, distance to ski lifts) and the pricing object to the pricing summary component.

When the user clicks the favorite icon, the page calls toggleFavoriteChalet with the current chaletId, and on success updates the heart icon state and any displayed total favorites count using the returned is_favorited and favorites_count values.

When the user clicks 'Book now', the page reads the current chalet id and activeCheckIn, activeCheckOut, activeAdults, and activeChildren state and navigates to booking.html with these as URL parameters.

If getChaletDetails returns pricing without check-in/check-out values (e.g., no dates provided), the page displays a prompt to select dates, and full price breakdown is only shown after valid dates are set.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state for this page is driven by URL parameters and the responses from getChaletDetails and toggleFavoriteChalet. On load, the page reads id, checkIn, checkOut, adults, and children from the URL. The id parameter is required to identify the chalet; if absent, the page shows an error instead of making API calls. The checkIn, checkOut, adults, and children parameters are optional and, when present, are passed directly into getChaletDetails as chaletId, checkIn, checkOut, adults, and children. The response from getChaletDetails defines the chalet object and pricing object, as well as initial favorite state via is_favorited. The page maintains in-memory state variables activeCheckIn, activeCheckOut, activeAdults, and activeChildren that are initialized from one of: (1) URL parameters, if present and valid; (2) the pricing section of getChaletDetails, if it includes check_in, check_out, adults, and children; or (3) empty/undefined values if no context is provided. Whenever the user changes dates or guest counts, the new values update these active* variables and immediately update the URL parameters using the same names (checkIn, checkOut, adults, children) so that reloading or sharing the URL preserves the configuration. After updating the URL, the page calls getChaletDetails again with the updated parameters to refresh pricing and any stay-dependent information. The favorite status is derived solely from the APIs: on initial load from getChaletDetails.is_favorited and on subsequent toggles from toggleFavoriteChalet.is_favorited; no favorite data is persisted outside of these API responses, and no localStorage is used. The 'Book now' navigation uses the current chalet id (from URL/id and chalet.id) together with activeCheckIn, activeCheckOut, activeAdults, and activeChildren to construct a booking.html URL that passes chaletId, checkIn, checkOut, adults, and children as parameters so the booking page can reconstitute the selected stay context.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by parsing URL parameters, validating the presence of a chalet id, calling getChaletDetails with any provided stay parameters, and distributing the returned chalet, pricing, and is_favorited data to child components.
data_binding: FavoriteChalet (via is_favorited flag, not direct model)
event_handlers: onInit (parse URL params id, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children; set activeCheckIn, activeCheckOut, activeAdults, activeChildren; call getChaletDetails)
onParamsChange (when URL parameters change due to user interactions, re-call getChaletDetails with updated values)
onDataLoaded (distribute chalet and pricing objects and is_favorited state to other components)
onError (handle cases where id is missing or getChaletDetails fails; show error state component instead of main content)

components.functionality: Displays key chalet information at the top of the page, including name, short name if available, location details, bedroom count, capacity, distance to ski lifts, rating, review count, and the current favorite heart icon. Provides quick actions such as toggling favorite and a visible 'Book now' button tied to the current stay configuration.
event_handlers: onFavoriteClick (call toggleFavoriteChalet with chalet.id; on success, update is_favorited and any displayed favorites count)

onBookNowClick (read current chalet.id and activeCheckIn, activeCheckOut, activeAdults, activeChildren from page state and navigate to booking.html with URL params: chaletId, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children)

components.functionality: Shows the chalet images returned by getChaletDetails, allowing the user to browse photos of the property. Handles cases where images are absent by adjusting display accordingly.
event_handlers: onImageSelect (set currently displayed main image based on user selection)

components.functionality: Lets the user configure their stay by selecting check-in and check-out dates and specifying numbers of adults and children. Keeps these values synchronized with the URL parameters and triggers pricing refreshes via getChaletDetails.
data_binding: pricing.check_in (for initial date values when provided by API)
event_handlers: onInit (initialize form fields from activeCheckIn, activeCheckOut, activeAdults, activeChildren or from pricing.check_in/check_out/adults/children if available)
onCheckInChange (validate date, update activeCheckIn state, update checkIn URL parameter, and trigger getChaletDetails with chaletId and updated stay parameters)
onCheckOutChange (validate date, update activeCheckOut state, update checkOut URL parameter, and trigger getChaletDetails)
onAdultsChange (enforce max_adults and max_guests constraints if present in Chalet, update activeAdults, update adults URL parameter, and trigger getChaletDetails)
onChildrenChange (enforce max_children and max_guests constraints if present in Chalet, update activeChildren, update children URL parameter, and trigger getChaletDetails)
onValidationError (display validation messages when dates are invalid or guest counts exceed chalet capacity without submitting the form)

components.functionality: Displays the current pricing information for the selected stay configuration, including base nightly rate, number of nights, room charges total, taxes, fees, and total amount with currency. Also provides a 'Book now' action aligned with the currently configured stay.
event_handlers: onPricingDataChange (update displayed breakdown whenever new pricing data is received from getChaletDetails)

onBookNowClick (same behavior as in chalet-summary-header: navigate to booking.html with chaletId and active stay parameters; this may be wired to the same navigation handler to keep behavior consistent)
onMissingDates (if pricing.check_in or pricing.check_out is absent, display a prompt or disabled state encouraging the user to select dates in stay-configuration-form before booking)

components.functionality: Presents the amenities available for the chalet, highlighting specific features such as hot tub, sauna, and pet-friendly where present. Uses the generic amenities codes and is_pet_friendly flag.
event_handlers: onAmenitiesDataChange (re-render the amenities list when chalet data is updated)

components.functionality: Shows the chalet description and practical stay information such as default check-in and check-out times when available.
event_handlers: onDescriptionDataChange (update displayed text when new chalet data is loaded)

components.functionality: Displays a summary of guest satisfaction using the chalet rating and review count. Provides context for how well-reviewed the chalet is, even though individual review details are not loaded on this page.
event_handlers: onRatingDataChange (update rating display when chalet data changes)

components.functionality: Centralizes logic for toggling the chalet's favorite status. Coordinates between UI components (e.g., header, possibly other indicators) and the toggleFavoriteChalet interface.
event_handlers: onToggleFavorite (invoked from any component with a favorite button; calls toggleFavoriteChalet with chaletId and updates shared is_favorited and favorites_count state on success)
onToggleError (handles API errors by reverting UI state and optionally showing an error message)

components.functionality: Ensures consistent navigation to booking.html with the current chalet and stay configuration parameters. It centralizes link creation so that any 'Book now' button uses the same parameter logic.
data_binding: activeCheckIn (derived from URL or form state)
event_handlers: onBookNowRequest (construct booking.html URL with chaletId=Chalet.id, checkIn=activeCheckIn, checkOut=activeCheckOut, adults=activeAdults, children=activeChildren, and perform navigation)
onMissingRequiredData (if Chalet.id is absent or no valid dates are set, prevent navigation and inform the user that configuration is incomplete)

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid (Bootstrap-style), with content constrained in a centered max-width container and nav elements arranged in columns (logo, nav links, cart) within the same grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear, top-to-bottom document flow inside a single centered main content column. Above this column, retain the stacked global structure from the mockup: a slim full-width blue contact bar at the very top, then a full-width white navigation bar with logo left, nav links center, and cart right. The chalet detail content begins immediately below the nav as a clean, uninterrupted column. Within that column, the chalet title is treated like a page title (visually strong, centered), followed by left-aligned functional and descriptive content sections stacked vertically: summary header, media gallery, booking configuration (form + pricing), amenities, description, and reviews.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear, top-to-bottom document flow inside a single centered main content column. Above this column, retain the stacked global structure from the mockup: a slim full-width blue contact bar at the very top, then a full-width white navigation bar with logo left, nav links center, and cart right. The chalet detail content begins immediately below the nav as a clean, uninterrupted column. Within that column, the chalet title is treated like a page title (visually strong, centered), followed by left-aligned functional and descriptive content sections stacked vertically: summary header, media gallery, booking configuration (form + pricing), amenities, description, and reviews.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve the top 10–15% of the viewport for the stacked blue contact strip and white nav bar, matching the mockup. Below that, the chalet detail area sits in a centered column that occupies about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, leaving 15–20% whitespace margins on both left and right. Major sections (gallery, booking section, amenities, description, reviews) each behave like blocks inside this column with clear vertical separation (24–32px). Any card-like panels (e.g., pricing) sit inside this main column rather than forming independent sidebars.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately spacious, document-style density tailored for reading. Use generous line height for all text and consistent paragraph spacing (~16px). Major section headings have larger top/bottom margins (~24–32px) to create clear anchors. Interactive areas like the stay configuration form and pricing panel are compact but not cramped, using internal padding of 16–24px. Overall, avoid visual clutter: no visual sidebars, no overly dense grids—just well-separated, clearly defined vertical sections.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye from the bright blue top bar down to the logo and nav, then into the centered chalet title, which serves as the main entry point for this page. From there, the vertical flow proceeds downward: summary/header metadata, then the media gallery as a strong visual anchor, followed immediately by the booking configuration and pricing section as the primary action area. Below this, secondary reading content (amenities, description, reviews) continues in a sequential document-style flow. The favorite toggle and booking navigation controller act as subtle but visible action affordances that do not disrupt the main vertical reading path.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a slim, full-width blue contact bar spanning the very top, followed directly by a clean white navigation bar with the resort logo on the left, navigation links centered, and a cart icon on the right. Beneath this global header stack, the chalet detail content sits in a single, centered column that occupies roughly two-thirds of the screen width on desktop, framed by generous white space on both sides. At the top of this column, the chalet name is prominently centered as a large page title with comfortable spacing above and below, while supporting actions like the favorite toggle sit subtly aligned to the right edge. Directly underneath, concise summary information leads into a wide media gallery that fills the width of the content column and becomes the primary visual element on the page. Below the gallery, a booking section presents the stay configuration form and pricing summary as coordinated panels, visually grouped but still fitting within the same central column. Further down, the amenities list, detailed description, and reviews summary are stacked as clearly separated sections with bold headings and well-spaced paragraphs, echoing a legal-document reading rhythm but adapted to hospitality content. A discreet booking navigation controller provides quick access to booking actions, likely at the bottom edge of the viewport or near the booking section, all while preserving the calm, airy, and text-focused aesthetic of the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The chalet-detail-loader appears as a full-coverage overlay on top of the main content column area, starting just below the white navigation bar and extending down to the bottom of the viewport. It is horizontally centered to match the main content container and can either dim the background or display a centered spinner and status text. The loader content (spinner + text) is vertically centered within this overlay, occupying a compact area (roughly 15–20% of viewport height and 20–30% of viewport width) to keep the rest of the layout context visible but subdued. When active, it prevents interaction with the components beneath but does not obscure the top blue contact strip or main nav bar, which remain visible above it for continuity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay relative to the viewport, anchored below the global header; it covers the interactive area of the page until data is loaded, then fades out to reveal the underlying stacked content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The chalet-summary-header is the first component within the main content column, positioned immediately below the stacked contact strip and navigation bar, with a vertical top margin of about 32px to create breathing room from the header. It spans the full width of the centered content column (roughly 60–70% of the viewport), aligned centrally in the viewport with 15–20% whitespace margins left and right. The main chalet title is horizontally centered within this block, using a large font and a generous bottom margin (~16–24px) to mimic the prominent page title from the mockup. Supporting details—such as location, rating, guest capacity, or short tagline—are left-aligned and placed directly beneath the title, occupying the same column width. The favorite toggle controller is visually associated with this header, typically in the top-right region of the header block, aligned to the right edge of the content column but kept on a single line with secondary metadata or slightly offset downward, maintaining a clean, uncluttered feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scroll flow; it remains at the top of the content column and scrolls away as the user moves down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The chalet-media-gallery sits directly beneath the chalet-summary-header, separated by a vertical gap of about 24–32px. It stretches the full width of the main content column, aligning perfectly with the edges of the header above. On desktop, the gallery area has a landscape orientation, typically occupying a height of roughly 35–45% of the viewport, ensuring it feels substantial but not overwhelming. The primary image or carousel dominates the top of the gallery block, with any thumbnails or navigation controls arranged either beneath the main image or along its lower edge, keeping everything within the same column width. Whitespace above and below the gallery is consistent with other major sections, reinforcing the document-like vertical rhythm and ensuring that the gallery reads as a distinct, self-contained section within the linear flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the vertical flow; no fixed or floating behavior. On smaller screens, it scales to full-width of the screen while still respecting vertical spacing between sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The stay-configuration-form is positioned immediately below the media gallery, offset by a vertical gap of about 24px. It is part of a booking-focused segment of the page: on desktop, this form occupies the left side of a two-column layout within the main content column, taking roughly 55–60% of the content column’s width, while still remaining within the overall central column (no true page sidebar). The form fields are left-aligned, stacked vertically with 16px spacing between inputs, and wrapped in a subtle card-like container with 16–24px internal padding and a light border or shadow to distinguish it from the white background. On tablet and mobile, this form spans the full width of the content column and stacks above the pricing-summary-panel, preserving the single-column, document-like feel. There is comfortable spacing (24–32px) above the form and 16–24px below it before the pricing panel or subsequent sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Responsive: aligned within the main content column; side-by-side with the pricing panel on wider screens, stacked vertically on narrower screens. It scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pricing-summary-panel is visually coupled with the stay-configuration-form, forming a cohesive booking section. On desktop, it is placed to the right of the stay-configuration-form within the same main content column, occupying roughly 40–45% of the content column’s width. The top edges of the form and pricing panel align horizontally, giving the impression of a balanced, two-column grouping embedded within a larger single column. The panel itself is card-like, with a clear border or subtle shadow and 16–24px of internal padding. Key pricing information (nightly rate, totals, fees) is left-aligned within the panel, with line items stacked vertically and 8–16px spacing between them. There is a primary booking action button near the bottom of this panel, spanning most of the panel’s width. On tablet and mobile, the pricing-summary-panel stacks directly beneath the stay-configuration-form, aligned left and spanning the full width of the content column, separated from the form by 16–24px of vertical space to maintain clarity and document-like flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Responsive: participates in a two-column layout with the stay configuration form on wide screens, transitions to a full-width stacked position below the form on smaller screens. Scrolls within the normal page flow; no fixed sidebar behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The amenities-list appears below the booking section (form + pricing), separated by a distinct vertical margin of about 32px to indicate a transition from transactional content to informational content. It spans the full width of the main content column and is left-aligned, reinforcing the reading-focused, document-like style of the mockup. A bold section heading such as “Amenities” is placed at the top-left of this block, using a slightly larger font and a bottom margin of about 16px. The amenities are then presented as a structured list—either in simple vertical bullets or a neat multi-column list—while still staying within the boundaries of the central column. Spacing between individual amenities or rows is moderate (~8–12px), ensuring legibility without feeling dense. The entire section has consistent top and bottom padding relative to surrounding sections so it reads as one of the main subsections of the page.
