component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the linear scroll, occupying a standard section block aligned to the main content column. No sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The chalet-description-section follows the amenities-list in the vertical flow, with around 24–32px of vertical whitespace separating the two. It uses the same full-width main content column and is fully left-aligned, echoing the policy/terms layout from the mockup. At the top of this block, a bold heading like “Description” anchors the section, followed by multiple paragraphs of descriptive text. Paragraphs are arranged in a continuous document style with approximately 16px vertical spacing between them and a comfortable line height to make longer descriptions easy to read. There are no competing side elements; the entire width is devoted to text. This section may also include occasional subheadings or short bullet lists, but all remain within the single-column flow and respect the same spacing rules, mirroring the structure of numbered sections in legal text from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolls with the rest of the content as a core part of the reading experience within the central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The chalet-reviews-summary is placed below the description section, separated by roughly 32px of vertical space to signal a new content segment. It spans the same main content column width and is left-aligned. A bold heading such as “Guest Reviews” or “Reviews & Ratings” sits at the top-left with a 16px margin below it. Immediately under the heading, a concise summary—overall rating, number of reviews, and maybe a simple visual indicator like stars or a small bar—appears in a compact block that may take up the full width or a portion of it aligned to the left. If brief review excerpts are included, they are presented as stacked, text-focused blocks with 16–24px spacing between them, maintaining an airy, document-like rhythm. There are no side rails or complex filters here; the emphasis remains on straightforward, readable text and simple metrics.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, following the standard vertical flow within the main content column with no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorite-toggle-controller is a compact action element visually tied to the chalet-summary-header at the top of the content column. It is positioned near the top-right corner of the header block, aligning with the right edge of the main content column. Horizontally, it sits opposite the centered chalet title, so that when you glance at the header area, the title is dominant in the middle while this small icon/button is available but unobtrusive to the right. Vertically, it aligns either with the baseline of the title or slightly below it, with about 8–12px of internal padding around the icon to maintain touch-friendly size. It does not extend beyond the boundaries of the header’s height; instead, it feels like a small control floating within the header’s white space, keeping the layout visually clean and consistent with the minimal styling in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static relative to the chalet-summary-header; it scrolls away as the header scrolls. No fixed or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-navigation-controller serves as a streamlined action/navigation element for booking-related steps and appears in a way that supports, but does not compete with, the main document-like flow. On desktop, it can be implemented as a slim horizontal bar either: (a) pinned to the bottom of the viewport, spanning full width, or (b) positioned directly above the stay-configuration-form as a subtle step indicator. In the more action-oriented version (a), the bar sits fixed along the bottom edge of the viewport, below the main content column, using the brand blue as its background color to visually echo the top contact strip. It contains concise text (e.g., key dates, total price) and a prominent “Book Now” button aligned to the right, with internal padding of about 12–16px. The bar’s height is modest (roughly 10–12% of viewport height at most) so it feels like a thin utility strip rather than a heavy footer. On mobile, this fixed bar can span edge-to-edge and remain visible as users scroll, providing quick access to booking actions without disrupting the vertical reading flow. In the non-fixed variant (b), it occupies the full width of the main content column and sits just above the stay-configuration-form, separated from the gallery by about 24px, acting as a static step indicator or quick-jump bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Typically fixed to the bottom of the viewport for persistent booking access, spanning full width while respecting a modest height. Alternatively, it can behave as a static horizontal bar within the content column above the booking section, but in either case it remains visually lightweight and aligned with the brand’s blue accent.

name: Chalet Booking Form
title: Chalet Booking Form
description: Form page where guests confirm stay details, review pricing, provide contact information and special requests, and create a pending chalet booking before the review step.

core_features: Load chalet, stay summary, and price breakdown by calling getChaletBookingFormData with chaletId and current check-in, check-out, and guest counts from URL parameters or user input.
Allow users to adjust stay details (check-in, check-out, adults, children, pets) and re-call getChaletBookingFormData to update the stay summary and price_breakdown dynamically.
Collect lead guest details (first name, last name, contact email, contact phone) and special requests (including pet-related notes) into a structured form ready for submitChaletBookingForm.
Display a detailed price breakdown (base nightly rate, room charges, taxes, fees, total, currency) using data from getChaletBookingFormData.price_breakdown.
Optionally highlight whether the total price satisfies a budget constraint when such a budget value is provided via URL parameter, using the total from getChaletBookingFormData.price_breakdown.
Validate mandatory fields (chaletId, dates, guest counts, lead guest name, contact details) before submission and show structured error messages without using localStorage.
Create a pending Booking by calling submitChaletBookingForm with the current stay and guest details, then navigate to booking_review.html with bookingId taken from the returned Booking object.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Standard flow from chalet_detail.html: (1) User clicks “Book now” on chalet_detail.html, which opens booking.html with chaletId, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, and optional pets as URL parameters. (2) On page load, the controller parses these parameters and calls getChaletBookingFormData to retrieve chalet_summary, stay_summary, and price_breakdown. (3) The page shows the pre-filled stay details and price breakdown; the guest information form is initially empty. (4) The user can adjust dates and guest counts; each valid change triggers a new getChaletBookingFormData call to refresh stay_summary and price_breakdown and updates the URL query parameters. (5) The user fills in lead guest first name, last name, contact email, contact phone, and any special requests. (6) The user clicks “Continue to review”; client-side validation runs. (7) If valid, the page calls submitChaletBookingForm with chaletId, updated checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, petsCount, and guest info. (8) On success, the response includes a Booking object; the page reads booking.id and redirects to booking_review.html?bookingId={booking.id}. (9) If submitChaletBookingForm returns an error, the error is shown and the user can correct the form and retry.

Workflow 2 – User adjusts stay details after opening the form: (1) User arrives on booking.html with valid chaletId and initial dates/guest counts in the URL. (2) getChaletBookingFormData loads initial data and price_breakdown. (3) The user changes check-in/check-out or guest counts in the stay details form. (4) On each change that passes basic validation (e.g., check-out is after check-in), the controller updates the URL parameters and calls getChaletBookingFormData again. (5) The chalet_summary remains the same, but stay_summary and price_breakdown are updated. (6) If getChaletBookingFormData fails (e.g., invalid dates), an error message is displayed, and the “Continue to review” action is disabled until a successful quote is loaded.

Workflow 3 – Budget constraint awareness: (1) User arrives on booking.html with chaletId and stay parameters, and optionally a budget URL parameter representing their maximum budget. (2) After getChaletBookingFormData returns price_breakdown.total and currency, the budget indicator compares the total against the budget parameter. (3) If total is less than or equal to the budget value, the indicator shows that the stay is within budget; if greater, it shows that it exceeds the budget. (4) When the user adjusts dates or guest counts and getChaletBookingFormData is re-run, the budget indicator automatically re-evaluates and updates its message.

Workflow 4 – Direct access with incomplete parameters: (1) User opens booking.html with only chaletId or with missing/invalid date or guest parameters. (2) The controller initializes the stay details form with empty or defaulted fields and does not call getChaletBookingFormData until mandatory stay inputs (at minimum, valid check-in and check-out) are provided. (3) As the user enters or corrects dates and guest counts, once the minimal valid set of stay parameters is available, the controller calls getChaletBookingFormData and displays chalet_summary, stay_summary, and price_breakdown. (4) The user then proceeds as in the standard workflow: filling guest info and submitting the form.

Workflow 5 – Browser navigation and URL-state consistency: (1) While on booking.html, the user changes stay details; the controller updates URL parameters using history APIs without reloading the page. (2) If the user uses the browser back/forward buttons, the page listens for URL changes (popstate), re-parses the URL parameters, and re-synchronizes the stay details form fields. (3) After re-synchronization, if the required parameters are present and valid, the controller calls getChaletBookingFormData to refresh chalet_summary, stay_summary, and price_breakdown so that the UI matches the URL state.
interactions: On page load, the controller parses chaletId, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, pets, and optional budget parameters from the URL query string. If chaletId is missing, an error is displayed and no API calls are made; otherwise, it attempts to call getChaletBookingFormData with the available parameters when dates are valid.

When the user edits check-in or check-out in the stay details form, the input is validated (e.g., both dates present and check-out later than check-in). If valid, the controller updates the corresponding URL parameters and calls getChaletBookingFormData with the new values; until valid, the price breakdown and budget indicator are disabled or show a placeholder state.

When the user changes the number of adults, children, or pets in the stay details form, the controller validates that values are non-negative integers and that at least one guest is present, then updates URL parameters and calls getChaletBookingFormData to refresh stay_summary and price_breakdown.

If getChaletBookingFormData returns successfully, chalet_summary, stay_summary, and price_breakdown are bound to their respective components, which re-render the chalet information, stay overview, price breakdown, and budget indicator.

If getChaletBookingFormData fails (for example due to invalid combinations of dates or guests), the error notification component displays the message and the “Continue to review” button is disabled until a successful quote is available.

When the user enters lead guest first name, last name, contact email, and phone, each field updates the internal form state; email and phone fields perform basic format validation on blur and/or on submit, surfacing inline error messages if invalid.

The special requests text area updates an internal specialRequests value on change. This value is later passed directly to submitChaletBookingForm without pre-processing, allowing users to include pet-related or other notes.

When the user clicks the “Continue to review” button, the booking form component performs comprehensive validation: required fields must be filled, contact info must be valid, a recent successful price_breakdown from getChaletBookingFormData must be available, and chaletId must be present. If validation fails, the form error summary lists all issues and the submission is aborted.

If validation passes, the controller calls submitChaletBookingForm using the current in-memory state: chaletId, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, petsCount (mapped from pets), leadGuestFirstName, leadGuestLastName, contactEmail, contactPhone, specialRequests.

If submitChaletBookingForm returns success, the page reads booking.id from the Booking object in the response and immediately navigates to booking_review.html?bookingId={booking.id}. The current page does not persist any additional data beyond what is encoded in the URL of the next step.

If submitChaletBookingForm returns an error or success=false, the error notification component displays the provided message, and the user remains on booking.html with all form data preserved in memory and in relevant URL parameters, allowing corrections and re-submission.

At any time, the budget indicator listens for changes to price_breakdown.total and to the optional budget parameter from the URL. If both are present, it re-evaluates whether the stay total is within the provided budget and updates its status message accordingly.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is driven by URL query parameters and interface responses. On load, booking.html reads chaletId (required), checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, pets, and an optional budget parameter (for budget comparison) from the URL. If chaletId is absent, the page shows a blocking error and does not call getChaletBookingFormData. If chaletId is present but dates or guest counts are missing or invalid, the stay details fields are initialized but getChaletBookingFormData is deferred until valid inputs are supplied. When valid checkIn, checkOut, adults, and children values are available, the controller calls getChaletBookingFormData(chaletId, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, petsCount) and binds its chalet_summary, stay_summary, and price_breakdown to components. Whenever the user changes checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, or pets in the form, the page updates both its in-memory state and the corresponding URL query parameters (using history.replaceState or similar) without reloading, then conditionally re-calls getChaletBookingFormData if inputs are valid. The price and budget display always reflect the latest successful price_breakdown from getChaletBookingFormData and the current URL-derived budget value. The page listens for browser navigation events that change the URL; when detected, it re-parses the query string and re-synchronizes form fields and, if appropriate, refreshes chalet and pricing data via getChaletBookingFormData. For submission, all payload values for submitChaletBookingForm are taken from the current in-memory state (which is kept in sync with the URL for stay parameters). On successful submitChaletBookingForm, the only state carried forward is bookingId in the redirect to booking_review.html; no localStorage is used for this page.

components.functionality: Initializes the booking form by parsing URL parameters, coordinating calls to getChaletBookingFormData, holding the canonical in-memory state for stay and guest details, and orchestrating updates to child components.
Displays the selected chalet’s key information (name, address details, representative image) and confirms to the user which chalet they are booking.

Captures and edits stay parameters (check-in date, check-out date, number of adults, children, and pets), validates them, keeps them synchronized with URL parameters, and triggers price re-quoting via getChaletBookingFormData.
Shows the detailed pricing for the stay, including base nightly rate, total room charges, taxes, fees, and overall total, based on the current stay parameters returned by getChaletBookingFormData.
Evaluates whether the current total price for the stay is within an optional budget value provided via URL, and presents a simple status message (within/over budget).
Collects lead guest first name, last name, contact email, and contact phone, validates them, and stores them in the controller’s in-memory state to be included in submitChaletBookingForm.
Provides a multiline input for users to enter any special requests, including pet-related notes, and binds the value to the specialRequests field in the booking payload.

Aggregates and displays validation errors from the stay details form, guest info form, and errors returned from getChaletBookingFormData or submitChaletBookingForm, ensuring users understand why submission or pricing failed.
Validates all required booking form fields, calls submitChaletBookingForm with the current state when valid, and routes the user to booking_review.html with the returned bookingId on success.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid (Bootstrap-style), with content constrained in a centered max-width container and nav elements arranged in columns (logo, nav links, cart) within the same grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, centered content column beneath the existing stacked header (blue contact strip + white nav). Inside this column, content flows top-to-bottom in a document-like sequence: page title and brief intro, step/booking context, error summary (when needed), chalet summary, stay details form, pricing information (price breakdown + budget indicator), guest information form, special requests field, and finally the primary continue button. Each section is clearly separated with consistent vertical spacing and left-aligned headings, mirroring the legal-document style of the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, centered content column beneath the existing stacked header (blue contact strip + white nav). Inside this column, content flows top-to-bottom in a document-like sequence: page title and brief intro, step/booking context, error summary (when needed), chalet summary, stay details form, pricing information (price breakdown + budget indicator), guest information form, special requests field, and finally the primary continue button. Each section is clearly separated with consistent vertical spacing and left-aligned headings, mirroring the legal-document style of the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: On desktop, the main content column occupies roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, centered, leaving 15–20% whitespace margins on each side. Each major component spans the full width of this content column, with internal padding of about 16–24px. Vertically, the header stack consumes the top 10–15% of the viewport; below it, the content column begins with 32–48px of top padding before the page title. Major sections (summary vs. each form block vs. price panel) are separated by 24–32px; related elements within a section (like price breakdown and budget indicator) are separated by 16–24px to feel visually grouped without crowding.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout emphasizes readability and calmness: moderately spacious spacing, generous line-height, and clear section breaks. Forms are structured into logical blocks with labels and fields on separate lines for clarity, avoiding dense multi-column field layouts. Text inside panels (summary, price breakdown) is left-aligned with 16px spacing between lines or list items. There are no sidebars or competing visual zones; all attention remains on the linear progression of booking-related content.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow starts at the existing header, then drops directly to the centered page title. From there, the eye moves slightly left to the step/booking context indicator, then down through the column. If errors exist, the error summary in a contrasting but restrained alert style acts as an early attention stop. The chalet summary panel provides context before any input, followed immediately by the stay details form, which naturally leads into the price breakdown and budget indicator. The flow then continues to guest details, special requests, and finally lands on the prominent continue button at the bottom, reinforcing a clear top-to-bottom journey toward review.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s slim blue contact bar and white navigation bar, a single, centered content column appears, occupying about two-thirds of the viewport width on desktop and leaving generous white margins on both sides. A bold, centered page title like “Chalet Booking” anchors the top of this column, with a brief explanatory line below it. Just beneath, a subtle, left-aligned step indicator or booking context line cues the user that they are in the booking-creation step. When validation issues exist, a full-width error summary box sits directly under this context line, spanning the content column and creating a strong but clean horizontal band. Below that, a lightly framed chalet summary panel presents key chalet details in a text-first, card-like block. The layout then progresses downward into clearly separated sections: first the stay details form (dates, guests, etc.), then an immediately following pricing section where a price breakdown panel and a budget indicator sit in a tight vertical pair. Further down, a guest information form follows the same left-aligned, stacked-field pattern, and the special requests field sits as the final large textarea block. At the very bottom of the column, a prominent continue button is aligned toward the right edge of the content column (or centered on smaller screens), with ample top and bottom margin so it stands out without clutter. The entire page reads like a structured document with embedded forms—calm, linear, and focused—ensuring users can comfortably scan from top to bottom while understanding their context, costs, and next step.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the main centered content column, the booking-init-controller manifests as a subtle contextual header just below the main page title and subtitle. It is left-aligned, occupying the full width of the content column but with a relatively small visual footprint: for example, a single line of text like “Step 1 of 2 · Booking details” or “Create your booking” sitting approximately 16–24px below the title block. It does not appear as a large card but more as a slim, textual meta-strip that gently introduces the current step. On desktop, it sits about 80–100px from the top of the viewport once the user scrolls past the global nav. On mobile, it remains directly under the title, maintaining the same left-aligned, full-width presentation with slightly reduced vertical spacing to preserve hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in the normal document flow inside the central content column; scrolls with the page and does not stick or float. It does not reserve extra height beyond its textual content, serving as an inline contextual indicator.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The chalet-summary-panel appears as the first boxed content element in the column, positioned directly below the booking-init-controller (and below the error summary when errors are present). It spans the full width of the content column (roughly 60–70% of viewport width on desktop) and is centered horizontally. It is framed like a card: a white background with a subtle border or shadow, and internal padding of around 16–24px on all sides. The panel’s top edge sits about 24–32px beneath the preceding element, creating a clear but not excessive separation. Inside, chalet name and key information are left-aligned, with about 8–16px vertical spacing between lines or sections. On mobile, the panel simply stretches to fill the available width with the same padding, remaining stacked above the forms, ensuring users see the chalet context before entering any details.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element within the central column, scrolling normally. It does not stick or float; it remains in sequence above the forms as a stable contextual reference.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The stay-details-form sits directly below the chalet-summary-panel, separated by about 24–32px of vertical whitespace to signal a new section. It spans the full width of the content column and is left-aligned, resembling a section in a document: a bold, left-aligned subsection heading (e.g., “1. Stay Details”) at the top, followed by a vertical stack of labeled fields (check-in date, check-out date, number of guests, etc.). Each label is placed above its input, with approximately 8px between label and field and 16–24px between field rows, preserving the moderately spacious density described in the mockup. On desktop, all fields remain within this single column; field pairs that belong together (like check-in/check-out) may sit side-by-side but still within the same content column width, leaving generous internal gutters. On mobile, the layout collapses to strictly one field per row. The bottom of the stay-details-form is about 24–32px above the price-breakdown-panel, visually tying the pricing immediately to the stay inputs.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as a standard block in the document column, scrolling naturally. It does not use sticky or floating behaviors; all inputs remain anchored in the linear reading flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The price-breakdown-panel is placed immediately below the stay-details-form, with about 24px of vertical spacing so it feels clearly related but still distinct as its own section. It spans the full width of the content column and resembles a clean, text-heavy card: white background, subtle border or light tinted background, and 16–24px of internal padding. The top of the panel contains a left-aligned subheading or label such as “Price Breakdown,” followed by line items (nightly rate, cleaning fee, taxes, etc.) stacked vertically with about 8–12px between lines and a slightly stronger bottom line showing the total. This panel visually anchors the financial information directly beneath the stay details, reinforcing a logical cause-and-effect relationship. On mobile, it remains full-width and stacked, maintaining the same spacing hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard, non-sticky card within the content column. It scrolls as part of the main content and always appears above the budget-indicator in the vertical sequence.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The budget-indicator appears directly beneath the price-breakdown-panel, separated by about 16–24px, visually grouped as part of the pricing section. It occupies most of the content column width—around 70–90% of the column—centered horizontally so there is a small margin on either side inside the column, making it feel like a visual bar rather than a full card. The component is a horizontal indicator (e.g., a bar or simple line with markers), with a concise label above or to the left, all left-aligned within its zone. On mobile, it stretches to full column width while preserving its stacked position between the price breakdown and the next form section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in regular document flow, directly following the price-breakdown-panel and preceding the guest-info-form. No sticky or floating behavior; it scrolls away as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The guest-info-form is positioned beneath the budget-indicator with approximately 24–32px of vertical separation, clearly signaling a new major section dedicated to contact details. It spans the full width of the content column and mirrors the stay-details-form in structure: a bold, left-aligned subsection heading (for example, “2. Guest Information”), followed by a stacked series of labeled fields (name, email, phone, address, etc.). Each label sits above its corresponding input, maintaining 8px label-to-field spacing and 16–24px between field rows. The content is strictly left-aligned and stays within the central column to preserve the document-like feeling. On mobile, all fields remain single-column and stacked in the same order, with consistent vertical rhythm. The bottom edge of this form sits about 24px above the special-requests-field, preparing users for the final free-text input area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard, scrollable form section within the central column. It does not stick or float and stays in linear order between the pricing section above and the special-requests-field below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The special-requests-field appears as the final form content block before the primary action button. It is placed about 24–32px below the guest-info-form, spanning the full width of the content column. A left-aligned label such as “Special Requests” sits at the top of this section, followed by a large textarea-style field occupying a greater vertical height than standard inputs—roughly 3–5 lines visible by default—to invite freeform text. There is about 8px between the label and the textarea, and 16–24px of spacing below the textarea before the continue-to-review-action button. The section visually reads like the concluding, optional narrative part of the form sequence, still integrated into the same clean document flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a normal block element within the column, scrolling with the page and sitting just above the primary action button. No sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The form-error-summary occupies a prominent horizontal band near the top of the content column whenever validation errors are present. It is placed directly below the booking-init-controller and above the chalet-summary-panel, with around 16–24px spacing above and below so it clearly stands out but does not overwhelm the layout. The summary spans the full width of the content column and is styled as a clean alert box: a lightly tinted background (e.g., pale red or amber), subtle border, and 16–24px padding on all sides. Error messages or a brief bullet list are left-aligned, with 8–12px between lines for readability. Because it sits high in the flow, it becomes one of the first elements users see when they return to the page after submitting invalid data, aligning with the document-like, top-down reading pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional, but when visible it is a standard block in the document flow at the top of the form stack. It scrolls naturally with the content and does not overlay other elements; its placement above the main panels ensures it remains an early attention anchor.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The continue-to-review-action button is the final interactive element in the column, positioned directly below the special-requests-field. There is a generous 24–32px of vertical space between the bottom of the textarea and the top of the button to isolate it as the clear next step. On desktop, the button is aligned to the right edge of the content column, matching the corporate, form-like aesthetic while still sitting comfortably within the column’s width. The button itself is slightly larger than standard text buttons, with ample horizontal padding (e.g., 16–24px) and 8–12px vertical padding, and uses the brand’s flat blue color, making it the strongest color accent in the form area. On mobile, the button expands to nearly the full width of the content column and is center-aligned, maintaining the same generous vertical spacing above and a 32–48px margin below to keep a clean visual finish to the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level or inline-block element anchored at the bottom of the main content column; it scrolls with the page and is not sticky by default. It always appears as the last element in the vertical sequence, reinforcing a clear end-point to the booking form step.

name: Chalet Booking Review
title: Chalet Booking Review
description: Review all chalet booking details, policies, and pricing before confirming the reservation, then confirm and access the booking confirmation.

core_features: Load booking review data using the bookingId URL parameter and the getBookingReview interface, including booking details, chalet information, and policies.
Display a structured summary of the stay: chalet details, address, check-in/check-out dates, number of nights, and guest composition.
Show guest and contact details, including lead guest name, contact email/phone, and any special requests captured in the booking.
Provide a detailed price breakdown using booking pricing fields (room charges, taxes, fees, extras total, grand total, and currency).
Display important policies (cancellation policy, payment terms, and house rules) returned by getBookingReview so users can review conditions before confirming.
Allow users to navigate back to the booking form page (booking.html) with the current bookingId to edit booking details.
Allow users to confirm a pending booking by calling confirmBooking with bookingId, handling loading and error states.
After successful confirmation, display a confirmation state that includes the booking reference_code, confirmation_message, and key stay details.
From the confirmation state, provide navigation to manage the booking on booking_details.html using the confirmed bookingId.
From the confirmation state, provide navigation back to the home page (index.html).
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Load booking review:
1) User arrives on booking_review.html with a bookingId URL parameter.
2) The page reads bookingId from the URL and validates that it is present.
3) If bookingId is present, the page calls getBookingReview(bookingId).
4) While getBookingReview is in progress, a loading indicator is displayed and core content is disabled.
5) On success, the page populates all review sections with data from booking, chalet, and policies.
6) If the returned booking.status indicates a non-pending state (for example confirmed or cancelled), the page switches to an informational state where confirmation controls are hidden or disabled and a message explains that the booking can no longer be confirmed here.
7) If getBookingReview fails or returns no booking, an error state is shown with the option to retry the data load.

Workflow 2 – Review stay, guest details, and pricing:
1) After data is loaded, the user reviews chalet details and address from chalet, and stay dates and nights from booking.
2) The user reviews guest composition (adults_count, children_count, pets_count) and lead guest information (lead_guest_first_name, lead_guest_last_name), plus contact_email/contact_phone if available.
3) The user checks special_requests if any text is present.
4) The user reviews the price breakdown using booking.room_charges_total, booking.taxes, booking.fees, booking.extras_total, booking.grand_total, and booking.currency.
5) The user expands or reads policy sections using the policies object (cancellation_policy, payment_terms, house_rules).

Workflow 3 – Go back to edit booking details:
1) From the review page, the user clicks a control such as 'Back to booking details'.
2) The page reads the current booking.id from the loaded booking.
3) The browser navigates to booking.html with a bookingId URL parameter set to the current booking.id (booking.html?bookingId={booking.id}).
4) The booking form page (outside this scope) can then use the bookingId to load and allow editing of the booking.
5) After the user edits and resubmits the booking on booking.html, they may be redirected again to booking_review.html with an updated or identical bookingId for a new review step.

Workflow 4 – Confirm a pending booking:
1) When the user is satisfied with all details and the booking.status is pending, the user clicks 'Confirm booking'.
2) The page disables the confirm button and shows an in-progress state for confirmation to prevent duplicate submissions.
3) The page calls confirmBooking(bookingId) using the booking.id loaded from getBookingReview.
4) While confirmBooking is in progress, a loading indicator for confirmation is shown; editing and navigation controls that could conflict with confirmation are temporarily disabled.
5) If confirmBooking returns success true, the page updates its internal state to 'confirmed', updates the current booking object with the returned booking data (including updated status and reference_code), and displays the confirmation_message.
6) The review UI (editable state and confirm button) is hidden or changed to a read-only view, and a dedicated confirmation panel is shown instead, using the now-confirmed booking data.
7) If confirmBooking returns success false or an error occurs, an error message is displayed, the confirm button is re-enabled, and the user can retry the confirmation.

Workflow 5 – Post-confirmation navigation:
1) After a successful confirmation, the user sees the confirmation panel showing booking.reference_code, confirmation_message, and key stay details.
2) The user can click 'Manage this booking' to navigate to booking_details.html with a bookingId URL parameter set to the confirmed booking.id (booking_details.html?bookingId={booking.id}).
3) Alternatively, the user can click 'Back to home' to navigate to index.html without additional parameters.
4) If the user refreshes booking_review.html after confirmation, the page again calls getBookingReview(bookingId); if booking.status is confirmed, the page directly shows an informational or confirmation-style view, and the confirm button stays disabled or hidden.
interactions: On page load, bookingId is read from the URL query string. If missing or empty, the page sets an error state indicating that no booking can be loaded, and disables all booking-related sections.
On successful getBookingReview response, the page binds booking, chalet, and policies data to the respective components (summary, guest details, pricing, and policies).
If getBookingReview fails (network error or invalid bookingId), the error component is displayed with a 'Retry' action that re-triggers getBookingReview using the same bookingId from the URL.
When the user clicks 'Back to booking details', the page constructs a URL booking.html?bookingId={booking.id} and instructs the browser to navigate to it.

When the user clicks 'Confirm booking' while booking.status is pending, the page triggers confirmBooking(booking.id). During this call, the 'Confirm booking' button shows a loading state and is disabled to prevent multiple submissions.

After confirmBooking returns success true, the page switches from review mode to confirmation mode by updating its internal state, displaying the confirmation panel, and keeping booking data in sync with the returned booking object.

If confirmBooking returns success false or if the interface call fails, the confirmation error area is shown with a message derived from the error, and the 'Confirm booking' button becomes enabled again for a retry.

When the user clicks 'Manage this booking' in the confirmation panel, the page navigates to booking_details.html?bookingId={booking.id}, where booking.id is taken from the confirmed booking object returned by confirmBooking.
When the user clicks 'Back to home' in the confirmation panel, the page navigates to index.html with no additional query parameters.

If the booking.status returned by getBookingReview is not pending, the 'Confirm booking' button is either hidden or disabled and an informational message is presented; navigation actions to booking_details.html and index.html remain available.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state driver is the bookingId URL parameter. On each page load, the script extracts bookingId from the query string of booking_review.html. If bookingId is absent or empty, the page enters a 'missingBookingId' error state and no interface calls are made. If bookingId is present, the page enters a 'loadingReview' state and calls getBookingReview(bookingId). On success, the page transitions to a 'reviewReady' state and stores the returned booking, chalet, and policies objects in memory for binding. The booking.status value determines whether the page stays in a review-capable state or becomes read-only: if booking.status is 'pending', the page enables confirmation controls; otherwise, it shows an informational state where confirmation is disabled. When the user triggers confirmation, the page enters a 'confirming' sub-state (within either the review or confirmation context) during which confirmBooking(bookingId) is in progress and the confirm action is disabled. If confirmBooking succeeds with success true, the page updates its in-memory booking with the returned booking object and transitions to a 'confirmed' state, displaying the confirmation panel and adjusting controls to reflect that the booking is no longer pending. The URL remains booking_review.html?bookingId={booking.id}, so a page refresh or later revisit will again call getBookingReview using this bookingId. Because booking.status is now confirmed, the initial load will directly render the read-only or confirmation-style view. No booking information is persisted via localStorage or similar mechanisms; all cross-page and reload behavior relies solely on the bookingId URL parameter and the latest data fetched from the assigned interfaces.

components.functionality: Reads the bookingId from the URL, validates it, and triggers the getBookingReview interface to load booking, chalet, and policies data. Manages the top-level loading and error states for the page.
Displays a loading state while getBookingReview or confirmBooking is in progress and hides itself when the corresponding operation completes.
Shows error messages when bookingId is missing, when getBookingReview fails, or when the booking cannot be loaded. Offers a retry action when a previous attempt to load the booking failed.

Displays key information about the stay: chalet name, address fields, city/region/postal_code/country, check_in, check_out, nights, and an overview of guest counts (adults_count, children_count, pets_count) from the booking object.

Shows lead guest details (lead_guest_first_name, lead_guest_last_name), contact_email, contact_phone, and special_requests from the booking. Only displays optional fields when corresponding booking properties are present.

Presents a structured price breakdown using booking.room_charges_total, booking.taxes, booking.fees, booking.extras_total, booking.grand_total, and booking.currency. The component recalculates any derived display (such as subtotals) purely from booking fields without hardcoded values.
Displays the cancellation_policy, payment_terms, and house_rules from the policies object returned by getBookingReview. Supports expanding/collapsing sections but does not alter policy content.

Provides primary actions at the bottom of the review view: navigating back to booking.html with the current booking.id for editing, and triggering confirmation via confirmBooking for pending bookings. Disables or hides the confirm control when booking.status is not pending or while confirmation is in progress.

Appears after a successful confirmBooking call or when getBookingReview returns a booking with a non-pending status. Shows booking.reference_code, confirmation_message from confirmBooking (when available), and key stay information from booking and chalet. Provides navigation actions to booking_details.html with bookingId set to booking.id and to index.html.

Interprets booking.status from the loaded booking and controls whether the page is in review mode (with active confirmation controls) or in read-only/confirmation mode (with confirmation controls disabled or hidden). Ensures that confirmBooking is only callable when the booking is in a pending state.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid (Bootstrap-style), with content constrained in a centered max-width container and nav elements arranged in columns (logo, nav links, cart) within the same grid.
layout.layout_pattern: All booking review elements sit inside the main centered content column that appears beneath the existing global header (blue contact strip + white nav bar). The content follows a linear, top-to-bottom document flow: page title at the top of the column, then booking summary, guest details, price breakdown, policies, and finally the action bar. Loading and error states occupy the same central column area where the main content would appear. The confirmation view reuses this same column, replacing the review content with a confirmation panel and related messaging, preserving the document-style flow seen in the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All booking review elements sit inside the main centered content column that appears beneath the existing global header (blue contact strip + white nav bar). The content follows a linear, top-to-bottom document flow: page title at the top of the column, then booking summary, guest details, price breakdown, policies, and finally the action bar. Loading and error states occupy the same central column area where the main content would appear. The confirmation view reuses this same column, replacing the review content with a confirmation panel and related messaging, preserving the document-style flow seen in the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top 10–15% of the viewport is occupied by the shared site header (contact strip and nav) and is not modified by this page. Below that, the booking review content is constrained to a single centered column that spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, leaving about 15–20% whitespace margins on the left and right. Each major block (summary, guest details, price breakdown, policies, confirmation) spans the full width of this column. Vertical spacing between these blocks follows the 24–32px section margin, while internal padding for any card-like panels is around 16–24px. The action bar sits at the bottom of the column with extra spacing above to visually separate it as the final step.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density mimics a legal/policy document: text is left-aligned, with comfortable line height and roughly 16px spacing between paragraphs. Blocks are clearly separated without feeling sparse. Information-dense areas like price breakdown and policies are grouped into well-structured sections, possibly with subtle dividers or light card backgrounds, but no heavy decoration. There are no sidebars or secondary columns; all information is stacked, readable, and easy to scan from top to bottom.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: User attention flows from the existing header down to a bold, centered page title introducing the booking review. Immediately below, a visually prominent booking summary panel acts as the first anchor, giving a high-level overview of the chalet, dates, and key details. The eye then moves downward through guest details and price breakdown in a natural sequence, and into the policies section, which echoes the numbered, document-like structure of the mockup’s terms page. Finally, attention settles on the horizontally oriented action bar at the bottom, where the primary confirmation button stands out on the right. On confirmation, the visual focus shifts to a prominent confirmation panel near the top of the column, with the rest of the layout maintaining the same centered, linear flow.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s fixed blue contact strip and white navigation bar, the Chalet Booking Review page presents a single, centered content column that occupies about two-thirds of the viewport width on desktop, framed by generous white margins on both sides. At the top of this column, a large, bold page title is centered, creating a clear starting point. Just beneath, a full-width booking summary panel provides a concise, visually prominent overview of the chalet and stay details, followed in sequence by a left-aligned guest details section and a structured price breakdown. These blocks read like stacked sections of a clean document, each separated by consistent vertical spacing. Further down, a policies section adopts the mockup’s legal-text style, with numbered subheadings and well-spaced paragraphs running the full width of the column. At the very bottom, a horizontal action bar spans the column, aligning the main confirmation button to the right for a strong final call to action. Loading and error states temporarily occupy the central portion of this column, centered for quick recognition, while a confirmation state swaps in a prominent confirmation panel near the top of the column, keeping the same clean, text-focused, corporate-resort aesthetic throughout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This data-loader component is non-visual and acts as a logical wrapper around the entire booking review content area. It conceptually occupies the same centered main content column that starts just below the global navigation bar and extends down to the bottom of the page content. It does not render visible elements or create additional spacing; instead, it determines whether the column shows the loading indicator, error state, review details, or confirmation panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendering controller that wraps the main content column; it does not affect layout spacing directly but controls which child components are mounted within the column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the loading indicator is placed within the main centered content column, occupying roughly the same horizontal space as the future booking content (about 60–70% of the viewport width). It appears directly below the nav/header stack, with about 48–64px of top padding from the bottom of the navigation bar to avoid feeling cramped. The indicator itself is horizontally centered within this column: a spinner or progress icon sits in the center with a short status line beneath it. The entire loading block is vertically compact but surrounded by generous whitespace above and below so it stands alone in the middle of the column without competing elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the main content column; replaces the normal booking content while loading. It scrolls with the page but typically appears above the fold due to its high placement.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error state appears in the same main content column position as the loading indicator and review content—starting a comfortable distance (around 48px) below the global navigation. It spans the full width of the column and is left-aligned, following the document-like style. Visually, it can be presented as a subtle message block or card: a short heading like an error title on the left, with a brief explanation below and possibly a retry action. Internal padding around the message is about 16–24px, while vertical margin above and below the block is around 24–32px, ensuring it reads as a distinct section without crowding the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the main content column, replacing the review content when an error occurs. It scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the loaded review state, the booking summary header is the first substantive block beneath the centered page title. After the title, there is about 24–32px of vertical spacing, and then this component begins, spanning the full width of the centered content column. It behaves like a summary card: a lightly framed or subtly shaded rectangle with 16–24px of internal padding on all sides. On desktop, the chalet name and primary details (e.g., chalet title, date range) are aligned to the left within the card, with any secondary metadata (e.g., number of nights, occupancy) aligned either beneath or to the right, but still within the same full-width block. The card’s height is determined by its content but is visually substantial enough to act as a key anchor at the top of the page. There is a 24–32px gap below this block before the guest details summary starts.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block spanning the full content column width. It scrolls with the document and retains its position immediately below the page title.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The guest details summary sits immediately below the booking summary header, separated by approximately 24px of vertical space. It also spans the full width of the centered content column and is left-aligned for easy scanning. The component can either appear as a lightly separated section (with a subtle divider line above) or as a simple text block with a bold subsection title (e.g., “Guest Details”) at the top, followed by structured lines such as guest name, contact information, and special requests. Internal vertical spacing between each line of details is around 8–16px, maintaining a text-focused, document-like rhythm. The block reads as a secondary section: less visually heavy than the booking summary card but clearly part of the same stacked flow. It has 24–32px of space below before the price breakdown begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline section within the main content column, stacked directly after the booking summary header. It scrolls normally with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The price breakdown section follows the guest details summary, with a vertical gap of roughly 24–32px. It spans the full width of the main content column and is left-aligned, but internally it uses a two-column feel within its own width: labels (e.g., nightly rate, cleaning fee, taxes) are aligned to the left, and corresponding amounts are aligned to the right edge of this component, creating a clear receipt-like layout while remaining within the single-column grid. Line spacing between each price row is about 8–16px, with a slightly larger gap (16–24px) before any subtotal and total lines. The grand total is emphasized at the bottom with bolder text and possibly a slightly larger font size. The entire price block can be lightly framed or divided by thin horizontal rules to enhance legibility without breaking the minimal, corporate-resort aesthetic. There is a clear 24–32px space below this section before the policies begin.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline section occupying the full content column width, scrolling with the document. It is structurally important but remains within the linear top-to-bottom flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policies section is positioned below the price breakdown, separated by about 32px of vertical space to signal a conceptual shift from numbers to legal text. It occupies the full width of the single centered column and mirrors the mockup’s terms/policies style: a left-aligned section title (e.g., “Booking Policies”) followed by numbered or titled subsections. Each subsection begins with a bold heading (potentially prefixed with a number) and has multi-line paragraphs beneath it. Paragraphs have comfortable line height and roughly 16px spacing between them, while subsections have 24–32px spacing between each other. There are no side columns or complex layouts here—just a clean, document-like text block extending vertically down the page. This section may be the tallest content block on the page and is intended for scrolling, with whitespace on both sides reinforcing the focused reading experience. A slightly larger gap (around 32px) separates the bottom of this section from the action bar to clearly distinguish content from controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Tall, inline text section that scrolls within the main column. It behaves like a standard document area, with no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The action bar appears at the bottom of the booking review content, directly beneath the policies section with a vertical gap of about 32px. It spans the full width of the centered content column but is relatively shallow in height. The bar presents a horizontal layout: on desktop, secondary actions such as “Back” or “Edit booking” are aligned to the left, while the primary confirmation button is aligned to the right, visually anchoring the call to action. Internal horizontal padding is around 16–24px from both left and right edges of the column, and vertical padding inside the bar is about 12–16px. The bar itself can be visually distinguished with a subtle top border or a very light background tint, while still preserving the minimal, corporate look. On long pages, the bar sits at the bottom of the scrollable content; on shorter pages, it appears just above the fold, maintaining a clear end-of-flow signal.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline at the bottom of the main content column. It scrolls with content by default; optionally, it can adopt a subtle sticky behavior near the bottom of the viewport on desktop to keep the confirmation button accessible, but always within the column width and without covering the header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Once the booking is confirmed, the confirmation panel occupies the same central column as the review content, but it moves to a position directly below the page title, effectively replacing the review sections. A generous 24–32px gap separates the centered page title from the top of this panel. The panel spans the full width of the column and adopts a prominent card-like appearance: a light background or subtle border, 24px internal padding, and a clearer hierarchy. A success icon or short success label can appear toward the top left, followed by a bold confirmation title and key details such as booking ID, chalet name, and dates. Additional information like payment summary or next steps can be stacked beneath, maintaining 8–16px spacing between lines. This panel becomes the main focal block on the page, possibly with a short confirmation message or instructions for accessing booking details placed below it. Vertical spacing beneath the panel is 24–32px, with any secondary content (e.g., links to manage booking) continuing the same text-focused, document-style flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the main content column, positioned immediately after the page title in the confirmation state. It replaces or hides the review-related sections while active and scrolls normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This state-controller component is invisible and sits conceptually at the top of the booking review content area, just inside the main centered column. It does not occupy any visible space or change the spacing between elements, but it determines whether the user sees the loading indicator, error state, full review flow (summary, guest, price, policies, action bar), or the confirmation panel. The user experiences smooth transitions in the same physical area of the page as the guard switches between these states.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller at the root of the content column that governs which child layout is rendered. It does not itself render UI elements or affect spatial layout directly.

name: Store & Category Listing
title: Store
description: Unified store page to browse top-level categories and view products within selected categories or subcategories with filters and sorting.

core_features: When no categoryId URL parameter is present, retrieve and display the list of top-level store categories using getTopLevelStoreCategories so users can choose what to browse.

When a categoryId URL parameter is present, retrieve category-specific filter metadata via getStoreCategoryFilters(categoryId) and show a filter UI for price, rating, shipping, and subcategories (if any).

List products for the selected category (and optional subcategoryKey) using listStoreProducts(categoryId, subcategoryKey, filters, sortBy), displaying key product data including image, price, rating, and delivery/shipping attributes.

Allow users to refine the product list by adjusting filters (price range, minimum rating option, shipping options) and update the URL parameters accordingly, then reloading products via listStoreProducts with the updated filters.

Support subcategory navigation within a category (e.g., different product_type groupings) using the subcategories returned by getStoreCategoryFilters and an on-page subcategory selector that drives the subcategoryKey URL parameter.
Provide sorting controls (e.g., by price or rating) that update a sortBy URL parameter and re-fetch the product listing via listStoreProducts with the chosen ordering.
Enable navigation from any product card to its dedicated product detail page by linking to product.html with the selected product_id as a URL parameter.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Landing on the store with no category selected: (1) User opens store.html with no categoryId parameter. (2) store-page-controller detects the missing categoryId and calls getTopLevelStoreCategories. (3) top-level-category-grid renders the returned StoreCategory items as clickable category entries. (4) User clicks a category; url-state-sync updates the URL to include categoryId for the selected category. (5) store-page-controller reacts to the URL change, switches to category view, calls getStoreCategoryFilters(categoryId) and listStoreProducts(categoryId, current subcategoryKey and filters derived from URL), and renders category-specific UI.

Workflow 2 – Browsing a specific category with filters and sorting: (1) User opens store.html?categoryId={someCategoryKey} either via navigation or a deep link. (2) store-page-controller parses categoryId, subcategoryKey, filters, and sortBy from the URL. (3) It calls getStoreCategoryFilters(categoryId) and passes categoryId, subcategoryKey, filters, and sortBy into listStoreProducts. (4) filter-sidebar displays filter controls configured from getStoreCategoryFilters response, pre-populated from current URL filter parameters. (5) product-list-toolbar shows the active sorting option based on sortBy and can display derived result summary from the product list. (6) User adjusts filters or sorting; filter-sidebar and product-list-toolbar emit events. (7) url-state-sync updates URL parameters (e.g., priceMin, priceMax, ratingMin, shippingOptions, sortBy) without reloading the page. (8) store-page-controller listens to URL changes, rebuilds the filters object, calls listStoreProducts again, and product-grid re-renders with the updated product list.

Workflow 3 – Navigating via subcategories within a category: (1) On a category view, store-page-controller has already called getStoreCategoryFilters(categoryId) and provided subcategory data to subcategory-filter-strip. (2) subcategory-filter-strip highlights the currently selected subcategoryKey from the URL, if present. (3) User clicks a different subcategory option. (4) subcategory-filter-strip emits an event with the chosen subcategoryKey. (5) url-state-sync updates subcategoryKey in the URL (and may reset pagination-related filter parameters). (6) store-page-controller detects the URL change, calls listStoreProducts with the new subcategoryKey and current filters/sortBy, and product-grid updates to show products for that subcategory.

Workflow 4 – Deep-linking into a pre-filtered category: (1) User follows a link to store.html?categoryId={key}&subcategoryKey={key}&priceMin={value}&priceMax={value}&ratingMin={value}&shippingOptions={keys}&sortBy={value}. (2) store-page-controller parses all URL parameters and builds a filters object used for the listStoreProducts call and filter-sidebar default state. (3) getStoreCategoryFilters(categoryId) is called so filter-sidebar can render the appropriate filter controls. (4) listStoreProducts is called with categoryKey, subcategoryKey, filters, and sortBy. (5) product-grid renders the filtered and sorted results, and filter-sidebar shows the filter state consistent with URL parameters. (6) User can modify any filter or sorting, and the URL is updated so the state remains sharable.

Workflow 5 – Navigating to product details: (1) User sees a product in product-grid. (2) User clicks anywhere on the product card or a dedicated details button. (3) product-grid triggers navigation to product.html?id={product_id} using the product_id from listStoreProducts response. (4) The store page does not modify cart or booking data; it only passes the product identifier via URL to the product detail page.
interactions: Click on a top-level category tile in top-level-category-grid: updates the URL to include categoryId for the selected category and clears subcategoryKey, filter, and pagination URL parameters as appropriate.

Click on a subcategory option in subcategory-filter-strip: sets or updates the subcategoryKey URL parameter and may reset pagination-related URL parameters, triggering a new call to listStoreProducts.
Change price range filter in filter-sidebar: updates corresponding URL parameters (e.g., priceMin, priceMax) and triggers a new listStoreProducts call with updated filters.
Select a rating threshold filter in filter-sidebar: updates a ratingMin URL parameter based on the selected rating_options entry and triggers a new listStoreProducts call with updated filters.

Toggle one or more shipping options in filter-sidebar: updates a shippingOptions URL parameter (for example, as a delimiter-separated list of selected shipping option keys) and triggers a new listStoreProducts call with updated filters.

Click a 'Clear filters' control in filter-sidebar or product-list-toolbar: removes all filter-specific URL parameters while preserving categoryId (and optionally subcategoryKey), then triggers a new listStoreProducts call with a minimal or empty filters object.
Change sort option in product-list-toolbar: sets or updates the sortBy URL parameter and triggers a new listStoreProducts call with the selected sort order.
Click on a product card in product-grid: navigates the browser to product.html?id={product_id} using the product_id from the listStoreProducts result item.

Navigate via browser back/forward buttons: url-state-sync detects URL changes, notifies store-page-controller, and store-page-controller re-runs getStoreCategoryFilters and/or listStoreProducts as needed to ensure UI matches the URL state.

Load or reload the page: store-page-controller reads the current URL parameters, determines whether to show category overview or product listing, and calls getTopLevelStoreCategories, getStoreCategoryFilters, and/or listStoreProducts accordingly.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state is derived from URL query parameters and determines what the page displays and which interfaces are called. (1) If categoryId is absent: store-page-controller calls getTopLevelStoreCategories and renders top-level-category-grid; no product API calls are made. (2) If categoryId is present: the page is in 'category view' mode; store-page-controller always calls getStoreCategoryFilters(categoryId) for the filter UI and listStoreProducts with categoryKey set to categoryId. (3) If subcategoryKey is present: store-page-controller passes this value as the subcategoryKey argument to listStoreProducts and sets the active subcategory in subcategory-filter-strip. If missing, listStoreProducts is called without a subcategoryKey or with a neutral value. (4) Additional URL parameters represent filters; for example, priceMin and priceMax are mapped into a price range object inside the filters argument passed to listStoreProducts, ratingMin is mapped to a rating-related filter, and shippingOptions (e.g., a delimiter-separated string) is parsed into a list of shipping option keys that are also placed in the filters argument. (5) An optional sortBy URL parameter is passed directly to listStoreProducts as the sortBy argument and used by product-list-toolbar to highlight the active sort option. (6) Pagination-related URL parameters (e.g., page and pageSize) are read by store-page-controller, mapped into pagination fields inside the filters object for listStoreProducts, and used by product-list-toolbar or pagination controls to show the current page; when these parameters change, listStoreProducts is called again. (7) Whenever user interactions require a state change (selecting a category, changing filters, sorting, pagination), url-state-sync updates the URL query string to reflect the new state without using localStorage; store-page-controller observes these URL changes and ensures data fetching and component state remain consistent with the URL. (8) All shared state between components (current category, selected subcategory, filter selections, sort order, and pagination) is derived from or synchronized into URL parameters so that copying or reloading the URL reproduces the same view.

components.functionality: Central orchestrator for the store page. On initialization and whenever the URL changes, it reads categoryId, subcategoryKey, and filter/sort parameters from the URL; decides whether to show the category overview or product listing; calls getTopLevelStoreCategories when needed; calls getStoreCategoryFilters when a category is active; and calls listStoreProducts with the correct arguments. It distributes the resulting data to child components and listens to their events to request URL updates via url-state-sync.

Encapsulates reading and updating URL query parameters without reloading the page. Provides helper methods to parse current categoryId, subcategoryKey, filters, sortBy, and pagination parameters into a structured state object and to push state changes back into the URL. Emits events to store-page-controller when the URL changes due to user actions or browser navigation (back/forward).

Displays a grid or list of top-level store categories when no categoryId is selected. Uses the StoreCategory items returned by getTopLevelStoreCategories. Each category entry is clickable and triggers navigation to a category-specific view by setting categoryId in the URL via url-state-sync.

Visible when a categoryId is present. Shows contextual information for the current category such as the category name (resolved from the StoreCategory list where possible) and may display the total number of products currently loaded. Provides a back-to-categories control that removes categoryId (and related parameters) from the URL and returns the user to the top-level category overview.

Horizontal or compact list of available subcategories for the active category, based on the subcategories array returned by getStoreCategoryFilters(categoryKey). Highlights the current subcategoryKey from the URL. Allows users to switch subcategories or deselect subcategory filtering, and communicates these changes by requesting url-state-sync to update the subcategoryKey URL parameter.

Provides controls to refine the product list using the category-specific filter configuration returned by getStoreCategoryFilters. Renders a price range input based on price_range metadata; rating options using rating_options; and shipping filters using shipping_options. Initializes control values from current URL parameters so the UI reflects shared state. When the user applies or clears filters, it emits events with the new filter state so store-page-controller and url-state-sync can update the URL and re-fetch products via listStoreProducts.

Shown when a category is active. Displays sorting controls (e.g., sort by price or rating) and may show a textual summary of the currently applied filters and the number of products currently displayed. Initializes the active sort option from the sortBy URL parameter. When the user changes the sort option or requests filter clearing from this toolbar, it emits events so url-state-sync can update sortBy (and optionally filter-related parameters) in the URL and trigger a fresh listStoreProducts call.

Renders the collection of products returned by listStoreProducts for the currently selected categoryId, subcategoryKey, and filters. Each item displays relevant fields such as name, image, price, currency, rating, review_count, is_free_shipping, and can_deliver_to_chalet. Clicking a product card triggers navigation to product.html with the product_id in the id URL parameter.

Manages paging through the product list when there are more products than shown on one page. Uses pagination-related URL parameters (such as page and pageSize) as the single source of truth, and emits events when the user navigates between pages. store-page-controller uses the updated URL-derived pagination state to pass appropriate pagination fields inside the filters argument of listStoreProducts.

Displayed when listStoreProducts returns an empty array for the current category and filters. Informs the user that no products match the selection and offers actions to clear filters or adjust subcategory. Invoking these actions triggers events that lead to URL parameter updates (such as removing filter-related parameters) and a subsequent listStoreProducts call.

Shows and hides loading feedback while data is being fetched. Activates when store-page-controller is waiting for responses from getTopLevelStoreCategories, getStoreCategoryFilters, or listStoreProducts, and deactivates once the corresponding data is available or an error state is reached.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid (Bootstrap-style), with content constrained in a centered max-width container and nav elements arranged in columns (logo, nav links, cart) within the same grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a stacked, top-to-bottom layout: existing site header (contact strip + main nav) at the very top; beneath that a single centered content container for the store. Inside this container, the flow is: page title (template element, not in this list), then the top-level-category-grid as the primary entry point; once a category is active, the category-context-bar appears directly below, followed by the subcategory-filter-strip. Below that, the layout splits into a two-column content area: a narrow filter-sidebar on the left and a wider main column on the right containing the product-list-toolbar, product-grid, and pagination-controls. The empty-state-panel and loading-indicator are conditional overlays or replacements within the main content column, not separate sections.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a stacked, top-to-bottom layout: existing site header (contact strip + main nav) at the very top; beneath that a single centered content container for the store. Inside this container, the flow is: page title (template element, not in this list), then the top-level-category-grid as the primary entry point; once a category is active, the category-context-bar appears directly below, followed by the subcategory-filter-strip. Below that, the layout splits into a two-column content area: a narrow filter-sidebar on the left and a wider main column on the right containing the product-list-toolbar, product-grid, and pagination-controls. The empty-state-panel and loading-indicator are conditional overlays or replacements within the main content column, not separate sections.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: On desktop, the main store content is constrained to a central container about 60–70% of the viewport width, leaving 15–20% margin on each side. Within that container, the top-level-category-grid, category-context-bar, and subcategory-filter-strip span the full container width. Below them, the filters and products share the container using a 3/9 or 4/8 style split (roughly 25–30% width for the filter-sidebar, 70–75% for the product column). Vertical spacing follows the 8px-based scale: ~32px from the nav to the main store title, ~24–32px between major sections (category grid, context bar area, filter+grid section), and ~16px between tightly related elements (e.g., toolbar and product-grid). On tablet/mobile, the content container remains centered but effectively becomes near-full width, and the filter-sidebar collapses into a full-width block above the product-list-toolbar.
layout_strategies.content_density: Text and controls are laid out with comfortable breathing room, similar to a legal or policy document: generous line height, clear separation between groups, and no clutter or visual noise. Grid elements (top-level categories and products) use a moderate card density: 3–4 columns on large screens with 16–24px gutters. Filters in the sidebar are vertically spaced by ~16–24px to avoid crowding. Empty and loading states use more generous padding (~48–64px vertical) to create calm, easily scannable feedback areas.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye moves from the existing blue contact bar and white nav across the top, down to the centered store title and immediately into the top-level-category-grid as the primary browsing entry. When a category is selected, attention drops to the bold, left-aligned category-context-bar, then progresses to the horizontal subcategory-filter-strip. From there, the flow moves left-to-right into the two-column region: the eye checks the filter-sidebar on the left, then spends most time on the right-hand product column, first scanning the product-list-toolbar (sorting, view options), then the product-grid itself. The pagination-controls anchor the bottom of the page, clarifying the sense of completion. Empty and loading states temporarily dominate the product column, becoming the main visual anchor while still respecting the overall structure.

layout.overall_description: Below the existing contact strip and main navigation, the store page content sits in a clean, centered white container that spans about two-thirds of the viewport, framed by generous side margins. A large, centered store title at the top of this container establishes context, with ample space above and below. Immediately beneath, a responsive top-level-category grid presents the main store categories in evenly spaced cards, aligned in a tidy grid that feels airy rather than crowded. Once a category is selected, a bold, left-aligned category context bar appears, stretching the full width of the content column and acting like a section heading for the product area. Directly below it, a horizontal strip of subcategory filters provides quick pivots within the chosen category. The layout then resolves into a quiet, two-column structure: a slim, vertically stacked filter sidebar on the left and a broader product area on the right. The right column begins with a compact toolbar for sorting and result controls, followed by a neatly spaced product grid and finishing with pagination controls that anchor the lower edge of the content. Throughout, a restrained use of brand blue for highlights, plenty of white space, and consistent typography echo the calm, document-like layout of the original mockup, while supporting efficient product browsing and filtering.
