component_layouts.layout_narrative: The store-page-controller is a non-visual, page-level component that conceptually wraps the entire store content area beneath the site’s main navigation. It spans the full width and height of the store page context but does not render any visible UI elements or occupy any perceived space in the layout. It orchestrates which content sections are visible (e.g., whether the user sees only the top-level-category-grid or also the category-context-bar, filters, and product-grid) while preserving the stacked, top-to-bottom structure of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller; logically encompasses the full main content container below the nav but does not render a box or affect spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-sync component is invisible and layered logically over the entire store page. It tracks and updates URL parameters in response to category, subcategory, filter, sort, and pagination changes, but it does not render any visible element. From a layout perspective, it exists in the background and does not consume any space within the centered content column or alter the visual flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; operates globally across the store page with no rendered footprint, simply coordinating state with the URL.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The top-level-category-grid appears near the top of the main content container, immediately below the centered page title. It spans the full width of the content container (about 60–70% of the viewport), centered with equal left and right margins of white space. On desktop, it presents category tiles in a responsive grid of 3–4 columns per row, each tile having equal width with 16–24px horizontal and vertical gaps between them. The grid is separated from the title above and the category-context-bar below by about 24–32px of vertical space, giving it a clear, self-contained section feel. The overall effect is a calm but prominent entry area where users can scan and choose high-level categories without visual clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as a standard block-level section in the vertical stack; reflows responsively (fewer columns) on narrower screens while remaining centered within the main content container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category-context-bar sits directly below the top-level-category-grid (or immediately beneath the page title when the grid is hidden or minimized), stretching across the full width of the central content container. It behaves like a prominent, document-style section heading for the selected category: a bold, left-aligned category title, potentially with breadcrumb and product count, arranged horizontally within the bar. The bar has a modest vertical height, with ~16–24px padding top and bottom, and may be visually distinguished by a subtle bottom border or lightly tinted background while still maintaining the overall white, minimal look. There is ~16–24px of vertical space beneath it before the subcategory-filter-strip, clearly separating it as its own horizontal band. This bar anchors the context for everything that follows in the product area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element that spans the content width; can be made sticky at the top of the content viewport on scroll if desired, but by default scrolls with the page while maintaining its position above subcategory filters.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The subcategory-filter-strip is placed immediately below the category-context-bar, spanning the same full width of the central content container. It appears as a low-profile horizontal band containing a row (or multiple wrapped rows) of subcategory chips or buttons, left-aligned but with enough right-hand whitespace to keep the area airy. Vertical padding is modest (~8–12px), with ~16–24px of vertical margin below it before the main two-column product area begins. On desktop, chips are arranged in a single row with wrapping as needed, maintaining 8–16px gaps; on mobile, the strip may become horizontally scrollable or wrap into compact rows while still feeling like one continuous horizontal control area linked to the category heading above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element that sits between the category-context-bar and the filters/products section; on narrow screens, it remains full-width and may enable horizontal scrolling for chips while preserving vertical stacking.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-sidebar occupies the left portion of the main product region, directly beneath the subcategory-filter-strip. Within the central content container, the area below the strip is divided into two columns: the sidebar takes roughly 25–30% of the container width (about 3–4 columns of a 12-column grid), aligned flush with the left edge of the container. It runs vertically from just under the strip down alongside the product area, with each filter group stacked top-to-bottom and separated by ~16–24px. There is comfortable internal padding (~16–24px) and a subtle right-hand divider (border or shadow) to distinguish it from the product column while keeping the look clean. On desktop, its top aligns with the top of the product-list-toolbar on the right. On mobile and small tablets, the sidebar collapses to a full-width block that appears above the product-list-toolbar, effectively becoming a stacked filter panel with the same internal spacing but no horizontal split.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, behaves as a left column in a two-column flex or grid layout; it may be configured as a sticky panel that remains visible within the viewport as the user scrolls the product list. On smaller screens, it reflows as a standard full-width block positioned above the product-list-toolbar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-list-toolbar sits at the top of the right-hand product column, aligned horizontally with the top of the filter-sidebar. It occupies the remaining 70–75% width of the central content container on the right side. Internally, its contents are arranged in a lean horizontal layout: result count or context text on the left, sorting controls and view toggles aligned to the right. The toolbar has a low profile with ~8–16px vertical padding and a bottom margin of ~16–24px before the first row of products, ensuring it reads as a separate control strip without feeling heavy. It aligns visually with the category-context-bar above through consistent typography and spacing, reinforcing the structured, document-like hierarchy. On mobile, it spans the full width below the (now full-width) filter-sidebar block and may stack its content into two rows while maintaining clarity and breathing space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the right column in a two-column layout on desktop; reflows as a full-width block beneath the filter-sidebar on smaller screens, maintaining its position directly above the product-grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-grid fills the main body of the right-hand column below the product-list-toolbar. It occupies the full width of this column (approximately 70–75% of the content container) and is laid out as a responsive grid of product cards. On large desktops, it typically displays 3–4 product cards per row, depending on the available width, with 16–24px of horizontal and vertical spacing between cards. The grid extends downward, aligning its top visually with the start of the first filter group in the sidebar, so the top rows of products sit adjacent to the top part of the filter-sidebar. The products are given enough horizontal breathing room so that the overall page retains the clean, minimally cluttered feel of the mockup. At the bottom of the grid, a clear vertical gap (~24px) separates the last row of products from the pagination-controls, maintaining a readable rhythm. On tablet and mobile, the grid gracefully reduces to 2 columns and then 1 column, always maintaining consistent gutters and alignment within the container.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the right column in a flexible two-column layout; expands and contracts responsively based on viewport width and sidebar behavior. Scrolls as normal page content, with no fixed positioning, but may be partially overlaid by the loading-indicator during data fetches.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls are positioned directly beneath the product-grid within the right-hand column, forming the visual bottom edge of the main product area. They align with the left edge of the product-grid and are centered horizontally within this column or left-aligned depending on the chosen style, but confined to the same width as the grid. There is a distinct vertical margin (~24px) above the pagination area to clearly separate it from the last row of products, and a similar margin or slightly larger (~32px) below it before the end of the main content container. The controls are compact and minimally styled, in keeping with the clean corporate aesthetic, and they provide a clear endpoint for each page of products without crowding the grid above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element that is anchored at the bottom of the product column; it scrolls naturally with the page and stays within the bounds of the main content container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-state-panel appears in place of the product-grid (and typically above or instead of the pagination-controls) whenever no products match the current category/filters. It is confined to the right-hand product column, maintaining the two-column structure so that the filter-sidebar remains visible and usable on the left. Within that right column, the empty state is centered horizontally and given generous vertical padding (~48–64px top and bottom) to create a calm, standalone message area. It may include a simple icon, a bold message, and a short description or reset-filters action, all stacked vertically with ~16px spacing. There is still a small margin (~24px) between the bottom of this panel and the end of the content container, preserving the overall vertical rhythm. On mobile, it spans the full width below the filter panel, visually dominating the main content region while still feeling light and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the product-grid (and effectively the pagination area) within the right column; behaves as a standard block element that scrolls with the page and does not affect the left filter-sidebar layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-indicator appears as an overlay or temporary content state primarily within the main content container, focusing on the product area without obscuring the global header. On initial full-page loads or major transitions, it can span the full width of the central content container and a substantial portion of the viewport height (e.g., at least 40–60%), with a translucent white backdrop that lightly dims the underlying product-grid area. A spinner or progress indicator is centered both vertically and horizontally within this area, with generous padding (~48px) around it so it does not feel cramped. The filter-sidebar and category-context-bar may remain visible and interactive or appear slightly muted depending on behavior, but visually the loading indicator becomes the primary focal element in the product column. On mobile, it still covers the main content region under the nav and above the footer, centered within the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Renders as an overlay positioned relative to the main content container or the right product column; typically uses fixed or absolute positioning over the product-grid area, intercepting interactions while active. It does not permanently take up layout space, disappearing entirely once loading completes.

name: Product Detail
title: Product Detail
description: Displays detailed information for a single store product and lets users configure variants, quantities, and (for gift cards) recipient details before adding the product to the shopping cart.

core_features: Read the product ID from the URL parameter `id` and use it to load full product details via `getProductDetails`.
Display complete product information including name, description, image(s), price, currency, rating, and review count from the `Product` model.
Show shipping information and clearly indicate when free shipping is available using the `shipping.is_free_shipping` field from `getProductDetails` and/or `Product.is_free_shipping`.
Indicate whether the product can be delivered to a chalet as part of a stay using `shipping.can_deliver_to_chalet` and/or `Product.can_deliver_to_chalet`.
Present available product variants (e.g., color, size) using `ProductVariant` data and allow the user to select a combination that will be sent to `addProductToCart`.
Allow the user to set the desired quantity, constrained by `Product.stock_quantity` when available, and ensure only valid positive quantities can be added to the cart.

For digital gift card products (`Product.is_gift_card` and `Product.is_digital`), display configuration fields for gift card amount, recipient email, and optional custom message; validate these inputs before calling `addProductToCart`.
Submit the configured product (including selected variant, quantity, and optional gift card configuration) to `addProductToCart` and handle the response including updated cart totals.
After a successful add-to-cart operation, show a confirmation state with cart summary information from the returned `cart` object and provide navigation to `cart.html` ('View cart').
Handle error states from both `getProductDetails` and `addProductToCart` by showing functional error messages and preventing invalid actions.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: View product details
1) User opens `product.html?id={productId}` from the store or homepage.
2) On page load, the controller component reads the `id` URL parameter.
3) The page calls `getProductDetails` with `productId = id`.
4) When the response arrives, the product information, variants, price, rating, review count, and shipping/delivery indicators are bound to the detail components.
5) If no product is returned or the product is inactive, an error state is shown instead of the purchase controls.

Workflow 2: Configure a physical product and add to cart
1) User reviews the product summary (images, description, price, rating).
2) If variants exist, the user selects desired options (e.g., color and/or size) via the variant selector; internal state is updated with the selected variant attributes.
3) User sets the quantity using the quantity selector; validations ensure the quantity is positive and, if `stock_quantity` is present, does not exceed available stock.
4) User clicks the 'Add to cart' button.
5) The add-to-cart form validates that required selections (e.g., variant choice) are made.
6) The page calls `addProductToCart` with parameters: `productId` from URL, `quantity` from selection, `selectedColor` and `selectedSize` derived from the chosen variant, and `giftCardConfig = null`.
7) On success, the page updates its local cart summary from the `cart` object in the response and shows a confirmation message plus a 'View cart' navigation option.
8) On error, an inline error message is shown and the user can retry.

Workflow 3: Configure a digital gift card and add to cart
1) User navigates to a product that is identified as a digital gift card (`is_gift_card = true` and `is_digital = true`).
2) Alongside the standard product information, the gift card configuration form is displayed.
3) User enters or selects a gift card amount, types a recipient email address, and optionally enters a custom message.
4) User chooses quantity if multiple identical gift cards are desired.
5) On clicking 'Add to cart', the form validates that: a) a valid amount is provided (e.g., greater than zero), b) a syntactically valid recipient email is entered, and c) any mandatory gift card fields are completed.
6) The page calls `addProductToCart` with `productId`, `quantity`, variant parameters if any, and `giftCardConfig` containing the amount, recipient email, and message.
7) On success, the UI shows that a digital item has been added and can highlight that delivery will be via email (based on the cart’s or product’s digital nature). The user can click 'View cart' to proceed.

Workflow 4: Understand chalet delivery eligibility
1) User opens `product.html?id={productId}` for a product that may be deliverable to a chalet.
2) The `getProductDetails` response provides `shipping.can_deliver_to_chalet` and/or `Product.can_deliver_to_chalet`.
3) The delivery context component shows a clear indicator when chalet delivery is available for this product.
4) If the user subsequently adds the product to the cart, the confirmation component can additionally display any chalet-related context included in the returned `cart` object (e.g., `cart.chalet_name` if already set in a previous step in the shopping flow).

Workflow 5: Navigate to cart after adding items
1) User adds a product to the cart using the add-to-cart form.
2) On successful response from `addProductToCart`, a confirmation message and a 'View cart' action become visible.
3) When the user clicks 'View cart' or the cart icon within the confirmation, the browser navigates to `cart.html`.
4) The cart page then handles the display of all items and delivery configuration based on its own logic.
interactions: On initial page load, the controller reads the `id` URL parameter and triggers `getProductDetails(id)`; if `id` is missing or empty, it sets an error state and skips the API call.

Variant selection interactions: when the user selects a color or size option, the variant selector updates its internal selected variant state; this state is exposed to the add-to-cart form so the correct `selectedColor` and `selectedSize` values are passed to `addProductToCart`.

Quantity selector interactions: clicking increment/decrement buttons or editing the quantity input updates the numeric quantity state; validation logic prevents values below 1 and, when `Product.stock_quantity` exists, prevents values above available stock.

Gift card form interactions: for digital gift cards, typing into the amount, recipient email, and message fields updates corresponding local state; on blur or on submit, validations are run (e.g., email format, amount presence and positivity).

Add-to-cart button interaction: when clicked, it triggers form-level validation; if validation passes, it calls `addProductToCart` with the current configuration; while the request is in flight, the button is disabled and a loading indicator state is set.

Add-to-cart response handling: on a successful `addProductToCart` response with `success = true`, the page stores the returned `cart` snapshot in local state for display in the confirmation component and resets transient fields (e.g., loading state); if `success = false` or the call fails, an error message is shown and the button is re-enabled.

Cart navigation interaction: clicking 'View cart' in the confirmation component triggers a navigation to `cart.html` using a standard link; no additional URL parameters are required on that navigation from this page.

Error handling interactions: if `getProductDetails` fails or returns no active product, the page shows a functional error message and hides purchase controls; the user may use browser navigation to return to the previous page.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is determined by the URL parameter `id`, which is required and used as `productId` for `getProductDetails`. On load or whenever the URL changes, the controller re-reads `id` and re-fetches product details. All subsequent UI state (selected variant attributes, quantity, gift card configuration, loading and error flags, and the most recent cart snapshot from `addProductToCart`) is maintained in in-memory page/component state and is not persisted via localStorage. Optional future URL parameters (for example, to preselect a variant or preset a gift card amount) can be read on load and used only to initialize these in-memory states without changing the `getProductDetails` call signature. Navigation to the cart uses a simple redirect to `cart.html` without relying on URL parameters from this page; the cart context itself is managed elsewhere in the application.

components.functionality: Central controller that reads the `id` URL parameter, calls `getProductDetails`, manages loading and error states, and provides the loaded product, variants, and shipping data to child components.
Displays key product information including name, category context (if available), rating, review count, and a short description header to orient the user.
Shows the primary product image and any additional images if available, allowing basic navigation between them (e.g., next/previous or thumbnail click).
Displays the product price and currency, and indicates shipping conditions such as free shipping using the shipping data from `getProductDetails` and the product model.

Indicates whether the product is eligible for chalet delivery and explains how it can be delivered as part of a chalet stay (when applicable). Can also show chalet-related context from the latest cart snapshot returned by `addProductToCart`.
Presents available product variants (e.g., color, size) and lets the user choose a combination. Exposes the currently selected color and size to the add-to-cart form.
Conditionally rendered form that appears only when the product is a digital gift card; collects gift card amount, recipient email, and an optional message, and provides validation feedback.
Allows the user to choose the desired quantity of the product. Enforces a minimum of 1 and, when available, a maximum based on `Product.stock_quantity`.
Aggregates selected variant data, quantity, and (when applicable) gift card configuration into a single submission. Calls `addProductToCart` and manages loading and validation states.

Displays success or error messages after an add-to-cart attempt. On success, shows cart summary information (e.g., updated subtotal and item count) from the `cart` returned by `addProductToCart`, and provides a 'View cart' navigation control.
Shows functional error messages when `id` is missing, `getProductDetails` fails, or the product is unavailable. Hides purchase-related components when active.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid (Bootstrap-style), with content constrained in a centered max-width container and nav elements arranged in columns (logo, nav links, cart) within the same grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing global header (blue contact strip + white navigation bar), all product detail content sits inside a single centered main column. Components are arranged in a linear, top-to-bottom sequence that mirrors a document: product header/title at the top, then the main visual (image gallery), followed by informational sections (price, shipping, delivery context), then configuration sections (variants, gift card details, quantity), and finally the transactional element (add to cart and feedback). Error and loading states occupy this same central column and either replace or overlay the normal content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing global header (blue contact strip + white navigation bar), all product detail content sits inside a single centered main column. Components are arranged in a linear, top-to-bottom sequence that mirrors a document: product header/title at the top, then the main visual (image gallery), followed by informational sections (price, shipping, delivery context), then configuration sections (variants, gift card details, quantity), and finally the transactional element (add to cart and feedback). Error and loading states occupy this same central column and either replace or overlay the normal content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content is constrained within a centered container spanning about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, leaving 15–20% white space on either side. The stacked site headers take up roughly the top 10–15% of the viewport; the product page content begins just below with a comfortable top margin. Each major component (gallery, price block, variant selector, forms) is treated as a vertical section with 24–32px separation between sections and 16px between related internal elements. On narrower viewports, the same hierarchy is preserved but the content container expands closer to full width with 16–24px side padding.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains a moderately spacious, reading-optimized density. Text elements (titles, labels, descriptions) have ample line height and clear separation, similar to a legal document layout: 16px between paragraphs or related text lines, and generous vertical padding around section headings. Interactive controls (selectors, form fields, buttons) are grouped compactly enough to feel efficient, but never crowded—each control block has breathing room around it, avoiding dense multi-column control layouts.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: After the user’s eye is drawn across the global header (contact strip, logo, nav, cart), it drops into the product header where the centered product title creates a strong focal point. From there, the flow is strictly vertical: first the image gallery as the main visual asset, then left-aligned informational text blocks (price, shipping, delivery notes), followed by clearly segmented configuration sections. Each configuration block behaves like a subsection in a document, often with a bold label or heading, guiding the user step-by-step down the page toward the primary action: the Add to Cart button. Notifications and error messages appear in-line within this column, preserving the vertical reading rhythm.

layout.overall_description: The product detail page sits beneath the familiar blue contact strip and white navigation bar, which together form a clean, fixed-feeling header band across the top of the screen. Immediately below, a centered content column—about two-thirds the width of the viewport on desktop—creates a quiet, document-like canvas surrounded by generous white margins. At the top of this column, the product name is presented as a bold, centered title, with any brief summary or meta information aligned just below it, creating a clear starting point. The main product imagery follows as a single, prominent gallery that fills the content column, while all accompanying product information flows downward in left-aligned blocks that resemble well-structured sections in a policy document. Price and shipping details form the first informational segment, followed by delivery context and configuration steps (variants, gift card specifics) that read like numbered sections. Controls such as selectors and forms are arranged in simple vertical stacks with ample spacing, and the Add to Cart area appears as the final, emphasized section in the column. Status messages—loading overlays, success feedback, and error states—occupy this same central space, maintaining the linear, uncluttered reading experience. The overall effect is a calm, corporate-resort aesthetic: blue-brand highlights on a mostly white, text-forward layout that feels both trustworthy and easy to scan.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data-loader component acts as an invisible wrapper for the entire product detail content within the centered main column. It occupies the full width of the content container (about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop) and spans from just below the site navigation to the natural end of the page content. When in a loading state, it places a subtle, semi-opaque overlay and a centered loading indicator over this entire column, dimming all underlying components without affecting the global header. On successful load, it becomes visually transparent, allowing the stacked components (header, gallery, price, forms) to flow normally from top to bottom within the same column. On load failure, it replaces the standard content stack with the error display component in the same spatial footprint.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a full-width wrapper within the main content column; when loading, it overlays that column with a centered spinner or skeleton while keeping the global header visible and unaffected.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product header is the first visible component in the main content column, positioned directly beneath the global navigation with a top margin of approximately 32–48px. It spans the full width of the centered content container. The product name appears as a large, bold, centered title, echoing the page-title treatment from the mockup. Directly below the title, any subtitle, short description, or metadata (such as category or SKU) is left-aligned within the same column, creating a subtle transition from a centered focal point to a left-aligned reading flow. Vertical spacing within this section is moderate: 16px between the title and subtitle, and another 16–24px of space separating the header from the image gallery that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal document flow, anchored at the top of the main content column with no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The image gallery sits immediately below the product header, occupying the full width of the centered content column while respecting its max-width constraint. The main image area is centered within this column, with a fixed aspect ratio (for example, 3:2 or 4:3) that extends to roughly 50–60% of the viewport width on desktop, leaving generous white space on the left and right margins. Thumbnails, if present, are arranged in a single horizontal strip directly beneath the main image, left-aligned with the image content and separated by 8–16px gaps, or stacked vertically below on smaller screens. There is approximately 24px of vertical space above and below the gallery, visually framing it as its own section before the text-heavy information begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in normal document flow; scales responsively within the central column, with images shrinking proportionally on smaller screens while maintaining margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The price and shipping summary component appears directly below the image gallery, separated by about 24px of vertical space. It is left-aligned and spans the full width of the content column, but its inner content (price lines, small text) occupies only as much horizontal space as needed, leaving clear white space to the right. The main price is the dominant element at the top of this block, appearing on its own line with slightly larger type and bold weight. Immediately below, in smaller type, shipping cost summaries and key highlights (e.g., ‘Free shipping over X’, ‘Instant email delivery for gift cards’) are presented as one or two short paragraphs or bullet-like lines with about 8–12px spacing between them. The entire block feels like a concise, document-style section header: compact yet clearly separated vertically from the next section by another 24–32px.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-line component that sits between the image gallery and the delivery/contextual text; no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The delivery context component is positioned immediately beneath the price and shipping summary, with approximately 24px of vertical separation. It occupies the full width of the content column and is fully left-aligned, echoing the legal-text style from the mockup. At its top, a short bold heading such as ‘Delivery & Redemption’ or ‘Shipping Details’ is aligned flush left, mirroring the subsection headings in the policy layout. Below that, one or more multi-line paragraphs describe delivery timing, digital voucher behavior, or location-specific constraints, with about 16px between paragraphs and comfortable line height for easy reading. This section may take up 1/4 to 1/3 of the initial viewport height depending on text length, but remains visually unobtrusive due to the generous white space around it and lack of decorative elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard document-flow behavior, expanding vertically as needed based on content length while maintaining fixed padding and margins relative to adjacent sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The variant selector is placed below the delivery context section, separated by about 24–32px of whitespace to signal a new interaction step. It sits fully within the centered content column, aligned left. At the top of this block, a bold label such as ‘1. Choose an option’ or ‘Select variant’ echoes the numbered subsections from the mockup, acting as a clear anchor. Under this heading, dropdowns, pill buttons, or radio groups are arranged in a simple vertical stack, with each control block (e.g., ‘Size’, ‘Color’, ‘Amount’) occupying the full column width on mobile and up to about 60–70% of the column width on desktop to keep the layout airy. Each control label sits directly above its control with approximately 8px spacing, and 16–24px separate each variant group. The bottom of this block is separated from the next form or section by another 24px, preserving the rhythm of discrete, document-like sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section that expands vertically with the number of options; no floating or sticky behavior, and controls reflow to single-column stacking on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When applicable, the gift card configuration form appears immediately following the variant selector, offset by approximately 24–32px of vertical spacing to reinforce that it is a distinct configuration step. It fully occupies the width of the content column and is left-aligned, matching the rest of the text-focused layout. A bold heading such as ‘2. Gift card details’ or ‘Recipient information’ appears at the top, styled like a numbered section header in a terms document. Beneath it, form fields (recipient name, email, message, delivery date) are laid out in a single vertical column, each spanning most of the column width but leaving comfortable side padding so they don’t touch the container edges. Labels are positioned above each input, with 4–8px of space between label and field, and 16–24px between successive fields. If explanatory copy is needed (e.g., ‘The gift card will be emailed to the recipient’), it appears as small, left-aligned text directly beneath the relevant field with about 8px spacing. The total form block may occupy roughly 1/3 of the viewport height, depending on field count, but retains a calm, uncluttered appearance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard, vertically stacking form area without floating elements; on small screens, fields stretch nearly full width with consistent internal padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quantity selector is placed near the bottom of the configuration flow, just before the Add to Cart call to action. It is separated from the preceding section (variant selector or gift card form) by about 24px of vertical space. Within the content column, it is left-aligned and relatively compact. On desktop, it typically appears on the same horizontal row as the Add to Cart button: the quantity control (e.g., minus button, numeric input, plus button or a dropdown) sits to the left, occupying roughly 20–25% of the content column width, with 16–24px of horizontal gap before the Add to Cart button. On mobile and narrower viewports, the quantity selector stacks above the Add to Cart button with about 16px vertical separation, preserving the linear reading flow. A small label such as ‘Quantity’ appears above the control with a 4–8px gap, ensuring consistency with the form-like styling of preceding sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows in-line with the Add to Cart form; forms a shared row with the button on wider screens and reflows to a stacked vertical layout on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-cart form anchors the bottom of the main interaction area, following the quantity selector. On desktop, the entire form presents as a single horizontal band occupying about 60–70% of the content column width, aligned left. The quantity selector sits on the left of this band, while the primary Add to Cart button appears to its immediate right, standing out with the brand’s blue fill color and white text. There is a consistent horizontal gap (16–24px) between the quantity control and the button to keep the row visually balanced. On mobile, the form becomes a vertical stack: quantity selector on top, the Add to Cart button directly beneath it, both full-width within the content column with 16px between them. The entire form block is separated from the preceding configuration components by about 24–32px of vertical whitespace, emphasizing its role as the final step. Below the button, a small line of auxiliary text (e.g., ‘Secure checkout’ or ‘You won’t be charged yet’) can appear, left-aligned, with 8–12px spacing if needed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow component; may group quantity selector and button on a common horizontal row at larger breakpoints while preserving a single-column, stacked layout on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-cart feedback component appears directly beneath the add-to-cart form within the same content column, introduced with about 16px of vertical spacing. It spans the full width of the column but remains relatively shallow in height, manifesting as a slim, document-like notification bar. The background is a light tint (e.g., light green for success, light red for error) with a subtle border, while the text is left-aligned and concise, describing the result of the add-to-cart action. Any iconography (such as a check or alert) is placed at the far left of the bar, aligned with the text baseline, and given a small 8–12px horizontal gap from the message text. The notification pushes subsequent content downward and disappears when not needed, avoiding overlays or toasts that might break the page’s calm, text-first reading experience.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline notification that appears and disappears in the normal document flow, expanding vertically only as much as its content requires; no fixed or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product detail error state occupies the same central content column as the rest of the page and replaces the normal component stack when a critical error occurs (for example, if the product cannot be loaded). It is positioned directly below the global navigation with a generous top margin of about 48px, mirroring the positioning of the standard product header. The error content is centered horizontally within the column but left-aligned in terms of text arrangement, consistent with the document style. A bold error title (e.g., ‘We’re having trouble loading this product’) appears at the top, followed by one or two explanatory paragraphs with 16px spacing between them. Optionally, a prominent link or secondary button (e.g., ‘Back to shop’) is placed below the text, left-aligned and separated by about 24px. The block itself can be visually framed with extra vertical padding (24–32px) above and below the text to make it feel like a standalone card, while still respecting the overall white, minimal aesthetic. No other product components appear when this error state is active.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the standard component stack within the main content column; sits in normal document flow with no overlay, allowing the global header to remain visible and unchanged.

name: Cart
title: Cart
description: Review items added from the store, adjust quantities, configure chalet delivery options, validate bundles and budgets, and proceed to checkout.

core_features: Fetch and display the current cart, including all cart items and order-level totals, using getCart.
Allow users to adjust item quantities and immediately refresh cart totals using updateCartItemQuantity.
Allow users to remove items from the cart and refresh cart contents using removeCartItem.
Display and update cart subtotal, estimated shipping, estimated tax, and total in real time based on API responses.
Provide delivery option selection (including chalet-related options when appropriate) and persist the selection using setCartDeliveryContext.
Support chalet selection and delivery date selection when a chalet-related delivery option is chosen, persisting these to the cart via setCartDeliveryContext.
Help users meet bundle composition rules (e.g., item counts or combinations) by validating the cart against constraints defined via URL parameters and giving clear feedback.
Help users stay within a budget by comparing the cart subtotal (or a subset of items) against a budget limit passed via URL parameters and surfacing warnings or confirmations.
Create a draft order from the current cart using createOrderFromCart and, on success, navigate to the checkout page with the returned orderId.
Provide a way to continue shopping by linking back to the store page while preserving relevant URL context (such as the last category or bundle key).
user_workflows: View and review cart contents: (1) User opens cart.html (via header icon or mini-cart link). (2) Page parses URL parameters (e.g., bundle configuration, budget limit, chalet context). (3) Page calls getCart. (4) When the response arrives, the page displays each cart item (name, attributes, unit price, quantity, line total) and cart-level totals. (5) Bundle and budget validation runs against the loaded cart and the URL-defined constraints, and the results are shown on the page.

Adjust item quantities and remove items: (1) User clicks plus/minus controls or edits a quantity field for a given cart item. (2) Component triggers updateCartItemQuantity with the item’s ID and the new quantity. (3) While the request is pending, the UI shows a loading or disabled state for that row. (4) On success, the updated cart from the response replaces the current cart state, automatically updating items and totals and re-running bundle/budget validation. (5) If the user clicks a remove action on an item, the page calls removeCartItem with that item’s ID and on success updates the cart and validations similarly.

Configure chalet delivery and delivery date: (1) User selects a delivery option radio button (e.g., chalet fridge stocking, delivery to chalet, standard shipping), filtered by cart contents (digital/physical) and any URL-provided preferences. (2) If a chalet-related option is chosen, chalet and delivery date inputs become required; they are pre-filled from URL parameters when available. (3) When the user confirms or changes these fields, the page calls setCartDeliveryContext with deliveryOption, chaletId, and deliveryDate. (4) The API response updates cart.delivery_option, cart.chalet_id, cart.chalet_name, cart.delivery_date, and possibly estimated_shipping/total. (5) Bundle and budget indicators refresh if needed.

Validate bundles and budgets before checkout: (1) Page reads bundle-related and budget-related parameters from the URL (for example: bundle key, required categories or product IDs, minimum/maximum item counts, budget limit, which categories count toward the budget). (2) When the cart is loaded or updated, a validation routine evaluates the current cart items against these constraints. (3) The result is shown as a clear indicator (e.g., all conditions met, conditions missing, or budget exceeded), along with actionable guidance (such as how many items are missing or by how much the budget is exceeded). (4) Depending on configuration, the Proceed to checkout button may be disabled when required constraints are not met, with an explanation.

Proceed to checkout: (1) User reviews items, delivery options, and validation messages. (2) User clicks the Proceed to checkout button. (3) The button checks that there are cart items, that any required delivery context is set, and that any required bundle/budget constraints (from URL) are satisfied. (4) If validations pass, the page calls createOrderFromCart. (5) On success, the newly created orderId is read from the response and the user is redirected to checkout.html?orderId={order.id}. (6) If the API call fails, an error message is shown and the button is re-enabled.

Continue shopping from the cart: (1) User clicks the Continue shopping link. (2) Page reads any context parameters from the current URL (for example, last store category, bundle key, or returnToCategory) and includes them when constructing the navigation URL to store.html. (3) Browser navigates to store.html with those parameters, so the user returns to a relevant store context.
interactions: On page load: Parse query string parameters (e.g., bundle configuration, budget limit, chalet context, deliveryOption) and call getCart; initialize all components with returned cart data.

Quantity change interaction: When a user increments, decrements, or directly edits a quantity field for a cart item, trigger updateCartItemQuantity with the affected cartItemId and the new quantity; while loading, disable the controls for that item and show progress; on success, refresh the cart and validations from the response.

Item removal interaction: When a user clicks Remove on a cart item, optionally confirm the action, then call removeCartItem; on success, update the cart items, totals, and validation indicators; if the last item is removed, show the empty-cart state and disable Proceed to checkout.

Delivery option selection: When a user selects a radio button for a delivery option, update local page state and, if possible, immediately call setCartDeliveryContext with the chosen option and any available chaletId and deliveryDate; if chalet or date is missing for chalet-related options, prompt the user to provide them before calling the API.

Chalet selection input: When a user selects or changes a chalet in the chalet dropdown (or text input if only one chalet is available from context), update local state and call setCartDeliveryContext with the new chaletId and current deliveryOption/deliveryDate.

Delivery date selection: When the user picks or changes a delivery date (e.g., via a date picker), validate the date against any URL-provided constraints (such as check-in/out range, if available) on the client side and then call setCartDeliveryContext with the updated deliveryDate if valid.

Bundle validation interaction: On any cart update (add/remove/change quantity) or URL parameter change (e.g., user navigates with different bundle config), re-run the bundle validation logic to determine if the cart meets the configured bundle rules; display messages and enable/disable checkout accordingly.

Budget validation interaction: When cart totals or bundle-relevant subtotal change, recompute budget utilization based on the URL-defined budget limit and the subset of items specified by the URL configuration; update a visual budget indicator and provide messaging if the budget is exceeded.

Proceed to checkout click: When clicked, validate that the cart is non-empty, delivery context is valid for the selected option, and any required bundle/budget rules are satisfied; if any check fails, prevent the call to createOrderFromCart and show an appropriate message; otherwise, call createOrderFromCart and on success navigate to checkout.html with the returned orderId.

Continue shopping click: When clicked, read any category or return context from the current URL and navigate to store.html, optionally appending those parameters so the store page can restore the user’s previous context.
page_functionality.state_logic: The cart page derives its state solely from the current URL parameters and the data returned by the assigned interfaces. On initial load (and on any URL change), the page parses query parameters and then calls getCart to retrieve the latest cart. Typical URL parameters include: (1) deliveryOption: if present, used to preselect the delivery option radio button and, after getCart resolves, optionally compared to cart.delivery_option; mismatches can trigger a call to setCartDeliveryContext to sync the server-side cart with the URL-indicated option. (2) chaletId and chaletName: when provided, they are used to prefill the chalet selection input for chalet-related delivery options and to pass chaletId into setCartDeliveryContext. (3) deliveryDate: if present, used to prefill the delivery date control and to pass into setCartDeliveryContext; the string is interpreted as a date/time according to client rules. (4) bundle configuration parameters (e.g., bundleKey, bundleRules, bundleRequiredCategoryIds, bundleRequiredProductIds, bundleMinItems, bundleMaxItems): these define how the cart should be validated for bundle composition. The page does not hard-code any particular counts or IDs; instead, it reads these values from the URL and compares them against the live cart.items data. For example, it can count items per category_id, match specific product_id occurrences, or enforce minimum/maximum total item counts based on the parameters. (5) budget-related parameters (e.g., budgetLimit, budgetCurrency, budgetCategoryIds, budgetIncludeGiftCards): these define which items count toward a budget and what the threshold is. The page computes the relevant subtotal by filtering cart.items according to these parameters and comparing the sum to budgetLimit; no numeric thresholds are baked into the code. (6) store return parameters (e.g., returnCategoryId, returnTo): these parameters are stored in transient page state and used only to construct the URL for the Continue shopping link to store.html. The authoritative source of cart contents and totals is always the latest response from getCart, updateCartItemQuantity, removeCartItem, or setCartDeliveryContext; the page never caches cart data in localStorage. Any time a cart-related interface returns an updated cart, that object becomes the single source of truth for all components, and bundle/budget validations are re-run against that new state.

components.functionality: Coordinates cart loading, URL parameter parsing, global cart state, and validation logic for bundles and budgets. On page load, it parses query parameters, invokes getCart, and distributes the resulting cart data to child components. Whenever the cart is updated via any interface, it refreshes validations (bundle, budget, delivery completeness) and toggles the availability of checkout actions.

Displays the list of all cart items, including product name, key attributes if available, unit price, quantity, and line total. It renders one cart-item-row per item and receives updated Cart data from the controller whenever the cart changes. It also shows an empty-cart view if there are no items.

Represents a single cart item, showing name, optional color (via selected_color), gift card details when applicable (amount, recipient email, message), player counts for games, unit_price, quantity, and line_total. Provides controls to increase/decrease quantity, directly edit quantity, and remove the item. It delegates API calls to the controller but triggers the appropriate events for quantity updates and removals.

Listens to quantity change events from cart-item-row and translates them into calls to updateCartItemQuantity. It normalizes quantities (e.g., ensuring they are positive integers, respecting zero as a signal to remove if allowed by business rules), triggers the API call, and on success notifies cart-page-controller with the updated cart from the response.

Handles item removal requests originating from cart-item-row. When triggered, it may request user confirmation (in UI) and then calls removeCartItem with the given cartItemId. After a successful response, it forwards the updated cart to the controller, which propagates changes to all components.

Displays the cart subtotal, estimated_shipping, estimated_tax, and total. It updates whenever the Cart object changes as a result of getCart, updateCartItemQuantity, removeCartItem, or setCartDeliveryContext. It may also show additional informational text such as how close the cart is to any URL-defined budget threshold, without hardcoding any numeric thresholds.

Provides selection controls for the cart-level delivery_option and, when relevant, chalet and delivery_date. It filters available delivery options based on the composition of cart items (e.g., disabling a purely digital option when physical items are present) and any URL-specified preferred option. When the user changes the option, chalet, or date, it calls setCartDeliveryContext to persist these settings and refresh totals.

Evaluates whether the current cart satisfies bundle composition rules defined via URL parameters (such as required categories, required product IDs, and minimum/maximum counts). It does not embed any specific rules in the component itself; instead, it reads configuration derived from the query string and compares it to Cart.items. It displays whether the bundle is complete or incomplete and exposes a status flag used by the checkout button to decide if proceeding is allowed.

Computes the relevant subtotal for budget comparison based on URL-specified budget configuration (for example, which categories or items to include) and compares it with a URL-specified budget limit. It updates a stateful result (e.g., within budget, at budget, over budget) and can surface both a numeric delta and an advisory message, without hardcoding any numeric limits internally.

Triggers final validation and order creation. When clicked, it checks if the cart is non-empty, if any required delivery_option/chalet/delivery_date are set, and if bundle and budget constraints configured via the URL are satisfied (when they are marked as required). If validations pass, it calls createOrderFromCart and, upon success, navigates to checkout.html with the orderId in the query string. It displays loading state while the API is in progress and shows error feedback on failure.

Navigates the user back to store.html. It reads navigation-related parameters from the current URL (such as the last store category or a returnTo identifier) and appends them to the target URL so that the store page can restore the user’s previous browsing context.

Displays when the loaded Cart has no items. It provides a concise message indicating that the cart is empty and prominently renders the Continue shopping link component to guide users back to store.html. The Proceed to checkout button is hidden or disabled while this state is active.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid (Bootstrap-style), with content constrained in a centered max-width container and nav elements arranged in columns (logo, nav links, cart) within the same grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain the stacked global header (blue contact strip + white navigation bar) as site chrome. The cart page content sits entirely below this header in a single, centered main column. Within this column, the cart follows a linear top-to-bottom document-style flow: page title at the top, then the cart item list, then bundle/budget validations, then the cart summary, then the delivery options form, and finally the primary and secondary actions. When the cart is empty, the list/summary/form/indicators are replaced by a centered empty state while keeping the same overall column structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain the stacked global header (blue contact strip + white navigation bar) as site chrome. The cart page content sits entirely below this header in a single, centered main column. Within this column, the cart follows a linear top-to-bottom document-style flow: page title at the top, then the cart item list, then bundle/budget validations, then the cart summary, then the delivery options form, and finally the primary and secondary actions. When the cart is empty, the list/summary/form/indicators are replaced by a centered empty state while keeping the same overall column structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: On desktop, the main content column occupies roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, centered, with 15–20% whitespace margins on each side. The stacked header uses approximately the top 10–15% of the viewport height. Vertical spacing between major sections (title, item list, summary, delivery form, actions) is around 24–32px, with 16–24px between individual rows/items and controls. On smaller screens, the column expands to nearly full width with consistent 16–24px side padding.
layout_strategies.content_density: Use a moderately spacious, document-like density: comfortable line heights, clear separation between cart rows, and distinct but not oversized section gaps. Item rows are visually compact but not cramped, using light dividers or subtle background blocks to separate them. Summary and form panels feel like clearly defined sections, not crowded cards. No sidebars or extraneous visual elements compete with the reading/decision flow.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the blue contact strip and white nav, down to a bold, centered cart title, then into a strong vertical flow: first scanning the list of items (left-aligned text), then noticing any validation messages, then reviewing the numerical summary, then choosing delivery options, and finally landing on the primary checkout button. Totals and key pricing information are right-aligned within their sections to create clear visual anchors, while headings and labels are left-aligned to maintain a natural reading rhythm.

layout.overall_description: Below the fixed global header, the cart page presents a clean, centered column against a white background. At the top of this column, a bold, centered page title announces the cart, with generous space above and below. Directly beneath, the cart item list runs full width within the column, each item laid out on a tidy horizontal row with text details on the left and quantities/prices on the right. After the last item, compact but clearly distinguishable validation messages about bundles and budget appear as short, left-aligned text blocks. Below these, a simple, lightly framed summary section spans the column, aligning labels on the left and currency amounts on the right. The delivery options form follows as another full-width section with stacked labels and controls, mirroring the orderly, document-like structure. At the bottom of the column, the primary checkout button sits prominently toward the right edge, with a more understated “Continue shopping” link offset to the left, creating a clear decision point without clutter. When the cart is empty, the center of the column is dominated by an empty-state message and a call to continue shopping, surrounded by ample whitespace, preserving the same calm, corporate reading atmosphere as the legal-text-inspired mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines the main content area that begins just below the global navigation bar. It creates a centered content column with a max-width of approximately 960–1140px, aligned in the horizontal center of the viewport. This column has 24–32px padding at the top (from the bottom of the nav) and 24–32px padding at the bottom (above the footer). All other cart components (title, list, validation indicators, summary, delivery form, actions, and empty state) are vertically stacked inside this column in a linear, document-like order. On desktop, this content column appears as a wide, readable strip occupying around two-thirds of the screen width with clear white margins on both sides. On tablet and mobile, the column stretches to nearly full width, with 16–24px side padding to keep content from touching the edges. The controller also handles layout branching: when the cart is empty, it shows the empty-cart-state centered within this same column, hiding the list, summary, form, and related indicators.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable container; its content scrolls vertically with the page while remaining anchored below the fixed global header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart item list is the first major block in the main column after the page title, separated from the title by about 24–32px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the content column from left to right, with a light top border or subtle top padding to visually mark the start of the list. The list renders each cart-item-row as a stacked vertical sequence, with 16–24px spacing between rows and optional thin dividers running full width of the column to improve scanability. On desktop, the list can stretch significantly down the page if many items exist, forming the central visual mass of the cart, while always maintaining consistent side alignment with the title, summary, and forms. On smaller screens, the list maintains the same stacking but allows each row’s internal layout to adapt vertically while preserving the single-column reading flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element in the vertical flow; grows downward as more items are added.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each cart item row is a horizontally structured block within the cart-item-list, occupying the full width of the content column with 16–24px internal padding on all sides. The row is visually divided into a left information region and a right controls/pricing region. On desktop, the left region takes roughly 60–65% of the row width and is left-aligned; it contains the product name (top line, slightly bold), optional brief details (e.g., variation, size, chalet-specific notes) beneath it, and any relevant meta-information in smaller text. The right region occupies roughly 35–40% of the row width and is right-aligned; it contains the per-item price, the quantity handler, and the remove control grouped into a neat vertical or compact cluster. Prices are typically near the top-right of the row, with the quantity controls horizontally grouped just below, and the removal action rendered as a small text link or icon aligned beneath or beside the quantity control. The row may use a subtle bottom border or alternating row background to distinguish it from adjacent rows without breaking the clean, corporate feel. On tablet and mobile, the row stacks: the left (info) block remains at the top, the price aligns right on the same line as the name where possible, and the quantity and remove controls shift onto a second line under the text, still right-aligned but within the same full-width block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fully responsive inline-block within the list; internal layout shifts from a two-region horizontal arrangement on desktop to a stacked arrangement on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quantity handler appears within the right-hand segment of each cart-item-row. On desktop, it sits just below or beside the item’s unit price, aligned to the right edge of the row. It typically comprises a horizontal control group (minus button, numeric field, plus button) occupying about 25–35% of the row width on the right side, with the controls sized for easy tapping but visually restrained to match the minimal style (flat buttons with subtle borders and plenty of white space around). There is roughly 8–12px of vertical space between the price text and the quantity controls, keeping them visually related but not cramped. On smaller screens, the quantity handler shifts down beneath the main text area within the same row, but remains right-aligned so that it still forms a clear interactive anchor at the bottom-right corner of the item row. The spacing above and below the control remains consistent with the overall 8–16px micro-spacing system.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Embedded interactive cluster within each row; remains anchored to the right side of its parent row and responds to row width changes, but does not float or stick independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The removal handler appears as a light-weight secondary action associated with each cart-item-row. On desktop, it is positioned close to the quantity handler in the right region of the row—either as a small text link (e.g., “Remove”) directly below the quantity controls with around 4–8px of vertical spacing, right-aligned to the same edge, or as a compact icon-only button adjacent to the quantity group with 8–12px horizontal spacing. Its typography and color are more muted than the primary text (e.g., smaller font size and a soft gray or subdued blue), ensuring it is visible but not dominant. On smaller screens, the removal handler remains in the lower-right part of the row, either directly under the quantity controls or on the same line if space permits, preserving a consistent mental model: the bottom-right of each item row is where you manage or remove the item.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline control within each row; its position is tied to the quantity and pricing cluster and shifts only as the row layout adapts to screen size.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart summary panel appears beneath the cart-item-list and the bundle-constraints-indicator, separated from the items by approximately 32px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the content column and is visually framed as a simple, low-profile panel—either via a thin border and 16–24px internal padding or a subtle, slightly tinted background—so it reads as a cohesive block without feeling like a separate card-based layout. Inside, summary rows (e.g., subtotal, delivery estimate, taxes, discounts, and grand total) are arranged in a vertical stack. Each row uses a two-column internal layout: the label is left-aligned and the corresponding amount is right-aligned, with 8–16px vertical spacing between lines, forming a clean, policy-document-like list of line items. The grand total row is emphasized with slightly bolder typography and an extra 8–12px of top margin to set it apart. This panel occupies roughly one third to one half of the visible height when content is short, and serves as a visual anchor just above the delivery options and primary action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section within the vertical flow; stays in the same order relative to list, indicators, and delivery form, scrolling with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The delivery options form is placed directly below the cart summary panel, with about 24–32px of vertical separation, occupying the full width of the main content column. It follows a simple, stacked form layout: section title or label at the top (left-aligned), then a single-column arrangement of form fields (e.g., chalet selection, check-in date, delivery time window, special instructions). Each field block is left-aligned, with the field label on a line above its input, separated by 4–8px, and each field block separated from the next by around 16–24px of vertical space. The form’s left edge lines up cleanly with the left edge of the title and item text, reinforcing the linear reading flow. On desktop, the form may show two shorter related fields (like date and time) side-by-side within a single row, each occupying roughly 48–50% of the column with an 8–16px gap between them, but the overall feel remains single-column and text-focused. On smaller screens, all fields collapse into a single vertical stack. The bottom of the form sits about 24px above the budget indicator or, if that is absent, above the primary/secondary actions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section in the vertical flow; its height expands based on the number of fields but remains fully integrated within the scrollable main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bundle constraints indicator appears directly beneath the cart-item-list, occupying the full width of the content column but limited to a low-profile height (typically one to three lines of text). It is separated from the last item row by around 16–24px of vertical space. The text is left-aligned and presented in a concise paragraph or bullet-style line that explains bundle requirements (e.g., “Add 2 more items to complete this breakfast bundle”). A small icon or colored accent (using the brand blue or a soft warning tone) sits on the far left, aligned with the text baseline, helping the indicator stand out without overwhelming the layout. The background remains white to stay consistent with the document style, though a light left border or subtle tint may be used to distinguish it from regular text. When present, this indicator is the first item the eye encounters after scanning the list, before moving down to the summary panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline message that appears and disappears based on validation state; it does not alter surrounding layout beyond occupying its reserved vertical space in the flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The budget indicator appears nearer the bottom of the content column, where the user is evaluating totals and next steps. It is typically positioned immediately beneath the cart summary panel (or beneath the delivery options form, if budget depends on delivery configuration), with about 16–24px of vertical space separating it from the element above. Like the bundle indicator, it spans the full width of the column but presents as a compact text block—one or two lines—left-aligned with the main text. A subtle icon or colored accent (e.g., green when under budget, amber/red when over budget) to the left of the text helps communicate status. The indicator sits above the primary proceed-to-checkout-button, acting as a contextual hint about whether the cart meets budget guidelines before the user commits to checkout. Its vertical spacing ensures it is clearly related to the total but not visually merged with the summary rows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline validation indicator; appears/disappears based on budget logic but remains in a fixed position in the vertical sequence when present.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The proceed-to-checkout button is the main call to action at the bottom of the cart content. It appears below the budget-indicator or delivery-options-form (whichever is last), separated by approximately 24–32px of vertical space to create a clear, decisive action zone. On desktop, the button is horizontally aligned to the right edge of the content column, occupying about 30–40% of the column width, with enough height (40–48px) to feel substantial but not oversized. The button uses the brand blue as a solid fill, with white text and minimal styling, making it the most visually prominent element in this lower section. On smaller screens, the button stretches to nearly full width of the column and is centered horizontally, with consistent side padding. There is 8–16px of space between this button and the continue-shopping-link, often with the link aligned to the left on the same baseline or stacked below the button when space is limited.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard clickable button within the vertical flow; scrolls with the page and does not remain sticky, ensuring it fits the document-like reading pattern.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The continue shopping link is a secondary action positioned near the proceed-to-checkout-button. On desktop, it typically sits to the left of the primary button on the same horizontal line: left-aligned with the content column edge, while the checkout button is aligned to the right, creating a balanced, two-sided footer action area. A horizontal gap of roughly 16–24px separates the link’s text from the button, keeping them distinct. The link uses a standard text style with brand-colored text but no heavy button framing, visually signaling its lower priority. On narrower screens where side-by-side placement is not feasible, the link appears directly below the primary button, left-aligned, with about 8–12px vertical spacing. In the empty-cart-state, the same link (or a more prominent text/button variant) can appear centered beneath the empty-state message to guide users back to shopping.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline text/link element that maintains its relative position near the primary CTA, adapting between horizontal and vertical arrangements based on available width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When no items are in the cart, the empty-cart-state occupies the main content region governed by the cart-page-controller, replacing the cart-item-list, validation indicators, summary, form, and CTAs. It is vertically centered within the portion of the viewport below the nav where possible, or positioned with generous top padding (approximately 64px or more) so it feels calm and spacious. The empty state content is centered horizontally within the main column, typically comprising a subtle icon or illustration at the top, followed by a short, bolded heading (e.g., “Your cart is empty”) and a brief explanatory line beneath, each separated by 8–16px of vertical space. Below this text, the continue-shopping-link (or a button styled similarly to other primary actions but possibly less dominant in color) is placed, centered, with 16–24px of space from the message above. The overall block uses the white background of the page and relies on negative space, rather than heavy decorative elements, to echo the minimal, corporate style of the original mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional full-width state view within the main column; it becomes the sole content (aside from global header/footer) when active and scrolls normally if content exceeds the viewport height.

name: Checkout
title: Checkout
description: Page for finalizing a store order by confirming delivery options, entering purchaser details, reviewing the order summary, and selecting a payment method without processing payment.

core_features: Load and display the current checkout summary for the order identified by the URL parameter `orderId` using `getCheckoutSummary`.

Allow the user to confirm or modify delivery options (delivery_option, chalet_id, chalet_name, delivery_date) for physical items using `updateOrderDeliveryOptions`, while clearly indicating that digital gift cards remain digital delivery based on item flags.
Collect and update purchaser details (purchaser_name, purchaser_email, billing address fields, optional notes) using `updateOrderPurchaserDetails`.
Display a real-time order summary (items, quantities, line totals, subtotal, tax, shipping_fee, total, currency, delivery context) using `getCheckoutSummary` after each update.

Provide a step to view available payment methods for the current order using `getPaymentMethods`, after the user has completed delivery and purchaser details, without capturing or processing any payment data.
Offer navigation back to the home page (`index.html`) from the payment selection step or after reviewing the order.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Load checkout for an order:
1. User arrives on `checkout.html?orderId={id}` from `cart.html` after creating a draft order.
2. The page reads `orderId` from the URL.
3. The page calls `getCheckoutSummary(orderId)`.
4. If the order is returned successfully, the page displays the current delivery options, purchaser details (if any), and the order summary.
5. If the order cannot be retrieved or is not in a draft or pending state, the page shows an error and suggests returning to the home page.

Workflow 2 – Confirm or update delivery options:
1. After the initial summary loads, the delivery section shows the current `delivery_option`, chalet information (if present), and `delivery_date` from the `Order` object.
2. The user selects or changes a delivery option (from the allowed `delivery_option` values) if physical items are present in the order.
3. If a chalet-related delivery option is chosen, the user selects a chalet from a control bound to `order.chalet_id` / `order.chalet_name` (values coming from the current order context) and optionally adjusts `delivery_date`.
4. The component validates that any required combination (for example, chalet delivery requiring a chalet and date) is present before saving.
5. On save, the page calls `updateOrderDeliveryOptions(orderId, deliveryOption, chaletId, deliveryDate)`.
6. If the response indicates success, the page replaces the current `Order` state with the returned `order` and re-renders the delivery section and the order summary.
7. If the update fails, an error message is shown and the previous values remain displayed.
8. Throughout, the UI clearly indicates that items with `is_digital = true` or `is_gift_card = true` in the order’s items will be delivered digitally and are not affected by changes to physical delivery options.

Workflow 3 – Enter and save purchaser details:
1. After or in parallel with delivery configuration, the purchaser details section displays any existing values from `order.purchaser_name`, `order.purchaser_email`, and billing-related fields.
2. The user enters or edits their name, email, and billing address details, and optionally notes.
3. When the user submits the form, basic validation is performed client-side (for example, required name and email fields) before calling the interface.
4. The page calls `updateOrderPurchaserDetails(orderId, purchaserName, purchaserEmail, billingAddress)` where `billingAddress` is an object that maps to the `billing_*` fields of the `Order` model.
5. On success, the returned `order` replaces the in-page order state, and the section is marked as complete for the current session.
6. On failure, an error is shown, and the user can adjust the information and retry.

Workflow 4 – Review order summary and proceed to payment selection:
1. Once delivery options and purchaser details are successfully saved, the user reviews the order summary panel which displays items (from associated `OrderItem` records via the `Order.items` reference), subtotals, tax, shipping_fee, total, currency, and delivery context.
2. If the user wants to adjust anything, they return to the relevant section (delivery or purchaser details) and repeat Workflow 2 or 3.
3. When satisfied, the user clicks a control such as “Continue to payment” that is only enabled once mandatory fields (for example, purchaser name and email) are present in the in-page order state.
4. The page optionally re-calls `getCheckoutSummary(orderId)` to ensure the summary is up to date and displays it alongside the payment methods step.

Workflow 5 – View and select a payment method (no payment processing):
1. After the user moves to the payment step, the page calls `getPaymentMethods(orderId)` to retrieve the list of enabled `PaymentMethod` options.
2. The payment methods list is displayed, showing each method’s `display_name` and `description` where available.
3. The user can select one method from the list; this selection is tracked only in client-side state and is not sent to any backend interface on this page.
4. The page shows a confirmation state indicating which payment method is selected and clarifies that the next step (outside this page’s scope) would be the actual payment process.
