5. From this step, the user can choose to go back to edit delivery or purchaser details (jump back to earlier sections on the same page) or click a “Return to home” control that navigates to `index.html` without modifying the order.

Workflow 6 – Error and edge case handling:
1. If `orderId` is missing from the URL, the checkout page cannot load an order; it displays an error and a link back to `index.html`.
2. If any interface call (`getCheckoutSummary`, `updateOrderDeliveryOptions`, `updateOrderPurchaserDetails`, `getPaymentMethods`) returns an error, the page shows an error banner with retry options where appropriate.
3. All data shown is always sourced from the most recent successful interface response; no fallback or override uses static or hardcoded values.
interactions: On initial page render, read `orderId` from the query string and trigger `getCheckoutSummary(orderId)`; show a loading state until the response arrives.

When the user changes the delivery option selection control, update internal pending delivery state; when the user clicks the delivery section save/confirm button, call `updateOrderDeliveryOptions(orderId, deliveryOption, chaletId, deliveryDate)` and then refresh the displayed order summary based on the returned `order`.

When the user edits chalet or delivery date fields in the delivery section, keep those values in the component’s temporary state until the user confirms; then include them in the `updateOrderDeliveryOptions` call.

When the user edits purchaser name, email, or billing address fields and submits the purchaser details form, call `updateOrderPurchaserDetails(orderId, purchaserName, purchaserEmail, billingAddress)` and update the page’s `Order` state with the returned `order`.
When the delivery or purchaser details update calls are in progress, disable the corresponding forms’ submit buttons and show a loading indicator for that section.

When the user clicks the “Continue to payment” control, verify that required purchaser fields (such as `purchaser_name` and `purchaser_email`) are present in the current `Order` state; if not, show inline validation messages and keep the user on the current step.
When moving to the payment step, call `getPaymentMethods(orderId)` once and render the list of available `PaymentMethod` options.

When the user clicks on a payment method option, mark it as selected in the client-side step state; update the UI to indicate the active method but do not call any backend interface to capture or process payment.
When the user clicks a “Return to home” link or button, navigate to `index.html` (no parameters) using standard link navigation.

If any interface call fails, display an error in a shared error component; provide a retry control that repeats the last attempted call using the existing `orderId` and previously entered values where applicable.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary piece of state for this page is the `orderId` URL query parameter. On every visit or reload of `checkout.html`, the page reads `orderId` from `window.location` (for example, `checkout.html?orderId={id}`). If `orderId` is present, it is used as the single identifier in all interface calls: `getCheckoutSummary(orderId)`, `updateOrderDeliveryOptions(orderId, ...)`, `updateOrderPurchaserDetails(orderId, ...)`, and `getPaymentMethods(orderId)`. The `Order` object returned from these calls becomes the single source of truth for all displayed order-related fields. When a delivery or purchaser update succeeds, the existing in-page `Order` state is replaced by the returned `order`. No order or checkout state is stored in localStorage or other persistent client storage; instead, the page always relies on the backend via the assigned interfaces and the `orderId` URL parameter to reconstruct state on reload. Client-side UI step state (for example, whether the user is viewing delivery, purchaser details, or payment methods) and transient values (such as currently selected but unsaved delivery information or selected payment method) are maintained only in memory for the duration of the page session and are not encoded into URL parameters or persisted beyond navigation away from this page.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading based on the URL parameter `orderId`, stores the current `Order` object in page state, and orchestrates communication between sections (delivery, purchaser details, order summary, and payment methods).
Displays loading states while `getCheckoutSummary` or update interfaces are in progress and hides when data is ready or an error occurs.
Shows error messages when interface calls fail or when `orderId` is missing or invalid, and provides retry or navigation actions.

Displays and allows editing of order-level delivery details such as `delivery_option`, `chalet_id` / `chalet_name`, and `delivery_date` for physical items, while indicating that digital items (such as gift cards) will be delivered digitally regardless of these choices. On save, calls `updateOrderDeliveryOptions` and updates the order state.

Collects and updates purchaser details, pulling initial values from the current `Order` and allowing the user to edit purchaser name, email, billing address fields, and optional notes. On submit, calls `updateOrderPurchaserDetails` and refreshes the order state with the returned `order`.

Displays the current order summary, including a list of items from `Order.items` (with access to fields such as `product_name`, `quantity`, and `line_total`), along with `subtotal`, `tax`, `shipping_fee`, `total`, `currency`, and delivery-related context from the `Order` model. It refreshes whenever the `Order` state is updated from any interface.

Manages progression between logical checkout steps (delivery configuration, purchaser details, and payment selection) based on the presence of required data in the `Order` state. It ensures that the payment step cannot be accessed until essential purchaser information is present.

When the user reaches the payment step, calls `getPaymentMethods(orderId)` to retrieve enabled payment options. Displays methods using `display_name` and `description`, allows the user to select one method, and clearly indicates that no actual payment is processed on this page. The selection is kept only in client-side state for UX purposes.
Provides a control to navigate back to `index.html` from the checkout page, typically surfaced on the payment selection step and in error states.

Each section is treated like a document section: full-width within the content column, with clear headings and generous spacing between them.

Each section is treated like a document section: full-width within the content column, with clear headings and generous spacing between them.

From there, the eye moves down the left-aligned content: first any error or status indication, then the delivery options, purchaser details, order summary, and payment methods, in that order. layout.overall_description: Beneath the slim blue contact strip and the white navigation bar with logo, menu, and cart, the checkout page content begins at a comfortable distance, centered within a broad band of white space. A single, tall content column sits in the middle of the screen, about two-thirds of the viewport width, leaving clean white margins on both sides. At the top of this column, the checkout page title is centered and prominent, followed by a horizontal step indicator that runs left-to-right just below it, clearly showing which stage of the checkout is active. Immediately below, any loading spinner or error banner appears full-width within this column so users see system feedback before interacting with the form. The main checkout content then unfolds in stacked, full-width sections: a delivery options block, a purchaser details block, a framed order summary panel, and a payment methods panel. Each section reads like a well-spaced document section—left-aligned headings, tidy rows of inputs, and clearly separated subsections—without any secondary column or sidebar to distract from the flow. Generous vertical spacing between these sections creates a clear rhythm, while consistent padding and type hierarchy hold everything together. At the very bottom of the column, slightly separated from the main action, a subtle text link allows users to return to the home page, reinforcing the linear, top-to-bottom completion journey.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout-root-controller defines the main content area of the checkout page directly beneath the site’s global header. It is centered horizontally in the viewport and constrained to a max-width of approximately 960–1040px, occupying around 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop. On larger screens, it leaves equal white space on both left and right, roughly 15–20% of the viewport width per side. The top edge of this container begins around 48–64px below the bottom of the white navigation bar, creating a breathable gap. Inside, it applies 24–32px padding to the top and bottom, and 24px (desktop) down to 16px (mobile) on the sides. All other checkout components are arranged in a vertical stack inside this controller: page title and step controller at the top, followed by status indicators (loading, error), then the delivery and purchaser forms, then the order summary, then payment methods, and finally the return-home link at the bottom. On tablet and mobile, this container expands to nearly full width of the screen with consistent side padding, preserving the same linear stacking order.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, block-level container that participates in normal document flow. It stays centered and responsive, shrinking and expanding with the viewport while maintaining fixed internal padding and vertical stacking of child components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout-loading-indicator appears near the top of the checkout content column, just below the page title and step controller but above all form sections. When visible, it occupies the full width of the content column and is horizontally centered; the indicator itself (spinner or progress bar) is centered within a vertical space of approximately 80–120px height, with at least 24px of whitespace above and below it to prevent crowding adjacent sections. On desktop, it visually feels like a light, in-flow status zone—possibly a centered spinner with a short status line underneath—rather than a full-page overlay, so that the blue contact bar and navigation remain visible. When not active, this component collapses completely, leaving no extra whitespace and allowing the delivery-options-form to move up immediately beneath the step controller.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: In-flow block that conditionally appears. When active, it pushes subsequent content downward; when inactive, it is effectively removed from the layout. It does not float or overlay other elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout-error-banner is positioned within the main content column below the step controller and just below (or instead of) the loading indicator. It spans the full width of the content column, forming a horizontal band that is visually aligned with the column edges. The banner height is driven by its content but typically ranges from 56–96px, with 16–24px of internal padding on all sides. It uses a subtle tinted background and/or a thin border to stand out against the white page while still feeling light and consistent with the clean corporate style. There is approximately 16–24px of vertical spacing above and below the banner, separating it from the step controller above and the first form section (delivery-options-form) below. On smaller screens, the banner reflows text to multiple lines but always remains full-width within the content column, preserving a clear, readable block of error messaging.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: In-flow block element that appears only when there is an error. It pushes down other content when present and collapses completely when absent, with no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The delivery-options-form is the first major functional section in the checkout flow, positioned below any error banner/loading indicator and comfortably separated by about 24–32px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the centered content column, reading like a lightly framed form section or card. At the top of this section, a left-aligned heading (e.g., 'Delivery Options') anchors the user’s attention, set with 0–8px space above and 16–24px below before the fields begin. Inside the section, radio buttons, dropdowns, and/or address selectors are arranged in a single column, aligned to the left, with 16px vertical spacing between related controls and 24px between distinct option groups. The entire section has 16–24px internal padding from its outer boundary, and approximately 32px of vertical space separates it from the purchaser-details-form below, preserving a clear distinction between steps. On mobile, all elements remain stacked in a single column, stretching close to the full width of the viewport with the same vertical spacing rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow section that expands vertically based on content. It remains full-width within the main content column and does not stick or float; it scrolls naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The purchaser-details-form sits directly below the delivery-options-form, separated by ~32px of vertical whitespace to signal a new, but related, step. Like the delivery section, it occupies the full width of the main content column and is visually structured as a clean, single-column form block with a left-aligned heading (e.g., 'Purchaser Details') at the top. The heading has 0–8px margin above and 16–24px below. Input fields (name, email, phone, billing address, etc.) are laid out in a vertical stack, left-aligned with consistent width, with 12–16px spacing between individual fields and 24px between logical groups (e.g., contact info vs. address). Internal padding of 16–24px on all sides keeps the content comfortably away from the section’s edges. There is approximately 32px of vertical spacing below this form before the order-summary-panel, allowing users to clearly perceive the transition from data-entry to review. On larger screens, shorter paired fields (e.g., first name / last name) may appear side-by-side within the same column width, but always within this single central block, maintaining the one-column layout feeling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: In-flow, full-width form section. It grows vertically to accommodate form fields and remains part of the natural scroll, without fixed positioning or overlay behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-summary-panel is placed below the purchaser-details-form, separated by roughly 32px of vertical space to emphasize a transition from input to review. It spans the entire width of the content column but is visually differentiated as a summary card: a white background with a subtle border or soft shadow and 16–24px internal padding on all sides. At the top of the panel, a left-aligned heading (e.g., 'Order Summary') is followed by 16px of space before the list of items and totals. Line items (product name, quantity, price) are arranged in a vertical list with 8–12px between rows and a slightly bolder treatment for totals at the bottom. Prices and totals may be right-aligned within each row, creating a subtle internal left-to-right reading line while the overall panel remains centrally aligned. Below the list, the total amount is given slightly more emphasis with 16px spacing above it and perhaps a thin separator line. There is about 24–32px of vertical whitespace beneath the panel before the payment-methods-panel begins. On mobile, the content of the summary panel stacks neatly, maintaining clear separation between item details and pricing while still occupying the full width of the main column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: In-flow informational panel. While important, it does not stick or float; it scrolls with the page, maintaining its position between the purchaser-details-form and payment-methods-panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout-step-controller is positioned near the top of the checkout content column, directly below the centered page title and above any loading indicator or error banner. It spans the full width of the content column but is visually composed as a low-profile horizontal strip that maps the user's progress (e.g., Step 1: Delivery, Step 2: Details, Step 3: Payment). Individual steps are arranged from left to right, evenly distributed or left-aligned, with each step label and number vertically centered within a band roughly 40–56px high. There is about 16–24px of vertical space above the step controller (beneath the page title) and about 16–24px below it before any status indicators or the first form section. The active step has stronger emphasis (bolder text or accent color) but overall styling remains clean and understated, in line with the minimal corporate aesthetic. On smaller screens, the controller remains full-width and may wrap labels to multiple lines if needed, while preserving a simple, linear left-to-right reading of steps.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed in place within the vertical flow beneath the page title. It does not sticky-scroll; it moves up and down with the rest of the content as the user scrolls through the checkout sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment-methods-panel is positioned below the order-summary-panel, separated by approximately 24–32px of vertical space, signaling the final confirmation step of the checkout. It spans the full width of the main content column and behaves like a structured form section. A left-aligned heading (e.g., 'Payment Method') sits at the top with 16–24px of space below it. Available payment options (e.g., credit card, bank transfer, on-site payment) are laid out as vertically stacked cards or radio-button rows, each spanning most of the column width with 12–16px spacing between them. The selected method’s details (such as explanatory text or brief instructions) appear just below its label within the same block, indented slightly or separated by 8–12px. Internal padding of 16–24px around the panel ensures it feels like a cohesive section. At the bottom of this panel, the primary action control (e.g., 'Confirm Order' or 'Continue') is right-aligned or centered within the content column, with about 16–24px of space above it and 24–32px below it before reaching the return-home-link. On mobile, the action button spans most of the column width and remains near the bottom of this panel, maintaining priority in the visual hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: In-flow panel that expands vertically based on the number and complexity of payment options. It scrolls normally with the page and does not employ sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The return-home-link is the final element in the checkout content column, placed below the payment-methods-panel with a vertical gap of approximately 24–32px to separate it from the primary call to action. It is left-aligned within the content column, appearing as a simple text link or a low-emphasis inline element (e.g., smaller font size, regular weight, brand-blue text) without a heavy button style. The link sits within a small vertical band of around 24px height, with 8–12px padding above and below to prevent it from feeling cramped against the bottom of the viewport. On desktop, it appears near the bottom-left corner of the centered content column, preserving the clean margins on both sides. On mobile, it remains at the very bottom of the scrolling content, still aligned to the left margin of the text. This placement ensures it is visible but not competing with the main checkout actions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow text link at the bottom of the content. It scrolls with the page and does not stick to the viewport; its position is purely based on document flow.

name: Favorites
title: Favorite Chalets
description: Displays all chalets the user has marked as favorites, with tools to review, compare, open details, and remove chalets from the favorites list.

core_features: Load the user’s favorite chalets using getFavoriteChalets and show them as a list or grid of chalet cards with images, base nightly price, currency, rating, and review count.
Allow users to open a favorite chalet in chalet_detail.html by clicking anywhere on the chalet card’s primary click area.
Allow users to remove a chalet from favorites directly from each card using toggleFavoriteChalet and update the list and favorites count accordingly.
Provide basic sorting controls (e.g., by added date, price, rating, name) and view mode (list or grid), driven by URL parameters.
Optionally hide inactive chalets (status !== 'active') via a URL-controlled filter so users can focus on bookable options.
Allow clearing all favorites by iterating over the current favorites and calling toggleFavoriteChalet for each chalet_id.
user_workflows: View and inspect favorites: 1) User opens favorites.html. 2) Page parses URL parameters (sortBy, order, view, page, hideInactive). 3) favorites-data-loader calls getFavoriteChalets. 4) Results are filtered/sorted/paginated based on URL parameters. 5) favorites-list renders chalet cards with core info for quick comparison.

Open chalet details: 1) User clicks the main area of a chalet card in favorites-list. 2) favorites-list triggers navigation to chalet_detail.html with ?id set to that chalet’s id (favoriteChaletId). 3) User views full details and booking options there.

Remove a single favorite: 1) User clicks the favorite-toggle control on a chalet card. 2) favorite-chalet-card calls toggleFavoriteChalet with that chalet’s id. 3) On success with is_favorited = false, the card is removed from the in-memory favorites list and favorites-summary-header updates the total count; the page re-applies sort/filter/pagination and re-renders.

Re-add a just-removed favorite (undo-like behavior within this page): 1) Immediately after a successful removal, the same favorite-toggle control remains available. 2) If the user clicks it again before navigating away, favorite-chalet-card calls toggleFavoriteChalet again with the same chalet id. 3) On success with is_favorited = true, the chalet is reinserted into the favorites collection, and the current URL-based sort/filter state is applied before re-render.

Sort and change view mode: 1) User selects a sort option or toggles between list/grid in favorites-toolbar. 2) favorites-state-controller updates the URL parameters (sortBy, order, view) using history APIs without reloading the page. 3) The current favorites list is re-sorted and the layout is updated based on the new parameters.

Hide or show inactive chalets: 1) User toggles the 'Hide inactive' switch in favorites-toolbar. 2) favorites-state-controller updates the hideInactive parameter in the URL. 3) favorites-list recomputes the visible collection by including or excluding chalets where status is not 'active', and re-renders.

Clear all favorites: 1) User clicks the 'Clear all favorites' control in favorites-toolbar. 2) The component iterates through the currently loaded favorites and calls toggleFavoriteChalet for each chalet_id. 3) As each call succeeds with is_favorited = false, the corresponding chalet is removed from the in-memory favorites list. 4) Once all calls complete (or after each success), favorites-summary-header and favorites-list update; if no favorites remain, favorites-empty-state is shown.
interactions: On page load, favorites-data-loader invokes getFavoriteChalets; favorites-loading-indicator shows while awaiting a response.
On successful getFavoriteChalets response, favorites-state-controller applies URL-driven sorting, filtering, and pagination before passing data to favorites-list.
On getFavoriteChalets error, favorites-error-banner displays a generic error message and a retry control that re-invokes getFavoriteChalets.
When a user clicks a chalet card’s main area, favorites-list navigates to chalet_detail.html?id={chalet.id}.
When a user clicks the favorite toggle icon/button in a card, favorite-chalet-card calls toggleFavoriteChalet(chaletId) and updates the UI based on the returned is_favorited and favorites_count.
When a user changes the sort option, favorites-toolbar triggers favorites-state-controller.onSortChange, which updates the sortBy and order URL parameters and re-sorts the in-memory list.

When a user toggles between list and grid view, favorites-toolbar triggers favorites-state-controller.onViewChange, which updates the view URL parameter and re-renders favorites-list in the chosen layout.

When a user toggles the 'Hide inactive chalets' control, favorites-toolbar triggers favorites-state-controller.onFilterInactiveToggle, which updates the hideInactive URL parameter and recomputes the visible items.

When a user changes page in favorites-pagination, favorites-state-controller.onPageChange updates the page URL parameter and favorites-list slices the in-memory favorites list to show only items for the current page.
When a user clicks 'Retry' in favorites-error-banner, favorites-data-loader re-calls getFavoriteChalets and re-runs the standard render pipeline.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven primarily by URL query parameters and the data returned from getFavoriteChalets. Supported URL parameters include: sortBy (e.g., 'added_at', 'price', 'rating', 'name'), order ('asc' or 'desc'), view ('list' or 'grid'), page (current page number, integer >= 1), and hideInactive ('true' or 'false'). On initial load, favorites-state-controller parses these parameters and applies defaults if any are missing. After getFavoriteChalets returns, the full favorites collection is stored in memory. The visible items are then derived by: 1) optionally filtering out chalets where chalet.status is not 'active' if hideInactive is true; 2) sorting the filtered list based on sortBy and order, using chalet fields (e.g., base_nightly_price for 'price', rating for 'rating', name or short_name for 'name') or added_at from the FavoriteChalet wrapper; 3) slicing the sorted list according to page and a configurable page size (determined in the implementation, not hardcoded in this specification). Any user interaction that modifies view, sortBy, order, page, or hideInactive updates the URL (using the history API) and triggers a re-computation of the derived visible collection without re-fetching data. Interactions that change the favorites set (single toggle or clear all) call toggleFavoriteChalet, then update the in-memory favorites list (add/remove the chalet based on is_favorited) and re-derive visible items using the current URL parameters. Navigation to chalet_detail.html passes the chalet’s id as the 'id' query parameter without changing other state on this page.

components.functionality: Initializes and refreshes the favorites dataset by calling getFavoriteChalets and exposing the raw array of { favorite_id, added_at, chalet } objects to other components.

Parses and manages URL-driven state (sortBy, order, view, page, hideInactive), applies this state to the favorites collection from favorites-data-loader, and exposes the derived visible favorites plus pagination metadata to the rest of the page.
Displays a concise summary of the favorites list, such as the total number of favorited chalets and, when applicable, how many are currently visible based on filters and pagination.

Provides user controls above the list: sort selector, view mode toggle (list/grid), optional 'Hide inactive chalets' switch, and a 'Clear all favorites' action that iterates through the current favorites and calls toggleFavoriteChalet for each chalet_id.

Renders the current page of visible favorites as a list or grid of favorite-chalet-card components based on the view mode, receiving a pre-filtered and pre-sorted subset from favorites-state-controller.

For a single favorite chalet, displays primary image (from chalet.images if available), chalet name, location fields, base_nightly_price with currency, rating and review_count, core amenity indicators derived from chalet.amenities, and a favorite toggle control.

Shows pagination controls (current page, next/previous/numbered pages) based on the total number of visible favorites and page size, and notifies favorites-state-controller when the user selects a different page.
Displays an informative message and guidance when getFavoriteChalets returns an empty array or when all favorites have been removed via toggleFavoriteChalet.
Shows a loading state while favorites-data-loader is awaiting a response from getFavoriteChalets or while bulk actions (such as clear all) are in progress.
Displays errors encountered during calls to getFavoriteChalets or toggleFavoriteChalet and provides a retry action for loading favorites.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid (Bootstrap-style), with content constrained in a centered max-width container and nav elements arranged in columns (logo, nav links, cart) within the same grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing global contact strip and navigation bar, the Favorites page content is organized in a single, centered column. The flow is strictly top-to-bottom: a summary header (page title and meta) at the top of the content column, then any error messaging, followed by a functional toolbar, then the main favorites list area (which shows either stacked chalet cards or an empty state), and finally pagination controls at the bottom. Data loading and state management components sit behind the scenes, with a loading indicator overlaying the list region when active.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing global contact strip and navigation bar, the Favorites page content is organized in a single, centered column. The flow is strictly top-to-bottom: a summary header (page title and meta) at the top of the content column, then any error messaging, followed by a functional toolbar, then the main favorites list area (which shows either stacked chalet cards or an empty state), and finally pagination controls at the bottom. Data loading and state management components sit behind the scenes, with a loading indicator overlaying the list region when active.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content sits inside a centered max-width container (around 960–1120px), occupying about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, with 15–20% white margins on each side. Vertically, there is generous top padding (32–48px) between the navigation bar and the summary header, then 24–32px between major sections (summary header, error banner, toolbar, list, pagination), and 16–24px between repeated items like favorite chalet cards. The top contact strip and nav together still consume the upper ~10–15% of the viewport; all Favorites page components live below that within the main content area.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains a moderately spacious, document-like density. Text and controls are comfortably spaced, using the 8px spacing scale: around 16–24px internal padding for banners and cards, 16px gaps between paragraphs or list items, and 24–32px between larger blocks. Line height is generous (~1.5–1.6), and there are no sidebars or extra columns, so the focus stays on the favorites list content without feeling crowded.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: After the eye passes the bright blue contact bar and white navigation (which are consistent across the site), attention lands on the centered page title in the summary header. From there, the flow shifts into a left-aligned reading and interaction path: down to any error banner, then the toolbar controls, then the vertical list of chalet cards. Each card acts like a visual anchor similar to numbered headings on a policy page, guiding the user down the page sequentially. Pagination at the bottom provides a clear end-of-content marker. The loading indicator and empty state appear in the same visual slot as the list, so they are naturally discovered in the main reading flow.

layout.overall_description: Under the existing blue contact strip and white navigation bar, the Favorites page opens into a clean, centered content column framed by generous white margins. At the top of this column, a bold, centered “Favorites” title and brief summary line echo the document-style header from the mockup, establishing the page’s purpose. Immediately below, a slim, full-width error banner appears only when needed, followed by a left-aligned toolbar with sort, compare, and bulk actions that stretch horizontally but stay comfortably spaced within the column. The bulk of the page is occupied by a vertical stack of full-width chalet cards, each separated by consistent white space and subtle borders, creating a readable rhythm similar to paragraphs in a legal document but tuned for product browsing. When there are no favorites, a centered empty-state panel replaces the card list in that same region. At the very bottom of the column, centered pagination controls sit with ample breathing room, acting as a clear terminus to the content. A loading indicator, when active, gently overlays the list region without disturbing the header or toolbar, keeping the experience calm and controlled. The overall impression is a modern, minimal, corporate layout with resort-brand touches, focused on legible, linear consumption of favorites content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader is a non-visual layer positioned logically at the top of the Favorites page’s content structure, just inside the main centered content container. It does not occupy visible space on the screen; instead, it orchestrates when the favorites list area is populated, empty, or in loading state. Its functional footprint maps to the same vertical region as the favorites list (between the toolbar and pagination), but it never renders visible UI itself—any visible feedback is delegated to the loading indicator, empty state, or error banner.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual infrastructure component anchored at the top of the content column in the DOM hierarchy; it has no direct spatial presence and only affects other components’ visibility and state.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The state controller sits alongside the data loader in the non-visual layer of the main content area, effectively wrapping the summary header, toolbar, list, empty state, pagination, loading indicator, and error banner. It does not draw any pixels on screen. Conceptually, its spatial scope matches the entire Favorites content column, from just below the navigation bar down to just above the page footer, enabling it to govern which visible components appear at each point in the user flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, full-page state manager that scrolls with the page but has no rendered box or fixed position; it only toggles visibility and state of visible components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites summary header is the first visible component in the Favorites content column. It is placed directly beneath the global navigation bar with a generous top margin of about 32–48px to visually separate it from the site chrome. The header spans the full width of the centered content column (roughly 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop) and is horizontally centered within the page. The main page title (e.g., “Favorites”) is centered within this column, set in a larger, bold type, and sits at the top of the component. If there is a short descriptive line or total count (e.g., “You have 8 chalets saved”), it appears directly below the title, left-aligned or slightly indented but still within the same width, with about 8–16px vertical spacing. The bottom margin of the summary header is around 24–32px, creating clear separation from the following error banner or toolbar while maintaining the clean, document-like rhythm of the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element at the top of the content column; it scrolls off the screen normally as the user moves down the page and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites error banner occupies a slim, full-width strip within the main content column, directly below the summary header and above the toolbar when an error is present. It aligns flush with the left and right edges of the centered column but does not touch the viewport edges, preserving the 15–20% side margins. The banner has internal padding of about 12–16px on all sides, with its text left-aligned in a single or double line, adopting the same comfortable line height as the rest of the content. Visually, it sits in a colored container (e.g., a soft red or warm tint) with subtle rounded corners and no heavy shadows, reflecting the understated, modern corporate style. There is around 16–24px of vertical space separating it from the summary header above and 16px before the toolbar below, ensuring the banner is noticeable but not visually overwhelming within the overall linear flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline banner within the content column; appears and disappears depending on error state and scrolls naturally with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites toolbar is positioned immediately below the summary header (or below the error banner when present) within the central content column. It spans the full width of this column but its controls are primarily left-aligned, reflecting the text-focused, document-like layout. The toolbar has a modest height (around 48–56px) with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding inside its container. On desktop, primary actions like sort, filters, and compare toggles sit along the left edge in a horizontal row, with 8–16px gaps between controls. Secondary actions such as “Remove all favorites” sit aligned to the right edge of the column, creating a balanced horizontal bar. The toolbar’s bottom margin of about 24px creates a clear gap before the favorites list begins, maintaining the spacious vertical rhythm seen in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static control bar anchored near the top of the content column; it scrolls off the screen during downward scroll and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites list occupies the main body of the Favorites page and sits directly below the toolbar with a top margin of about 24–32px. It shares the same centered width as the rest of the content (around 60–70% of the viewport), fully aligned with the toolbar and summary header edges. Within this list container, favorite chalet cards are stacked vertically in a single column on desktop to preserve the simple, continuous reading pattern of the mockup. Each card spans nearly the full width of the content column with 16–24px vertical spacing between cards, creating a repeating rhythm similar to paragraphs in a policy document but with richer content. The list can extend significantly down the page, and the user scrolls through these cards in a clean, uninterrupted vertical flow until reaching the pagination controls at the bottom.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrollable content section within the main column; it grows vertically as more favorites are present and simply extends the page length, without fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each favorite chalet card is rendered as a full-width card within the favorites list, aligned to the left and right edges of the content column. The card’s outer container uses a subtle border or very soft shadow with a white background, consistent with the minimalist corporate aesthetic. It includes 16–24px internal padding on all sides and a vertical height typically in the 200–260px range on desktop, depending on content. Inside the card, the layout is horizontally divided: on larger screens, the chalet image thumbnail sits on the left occupying roughly 30–40% of the card’s width, while the right 60–70% contains the chalet name, brief description, key attributes (capacity, location, price), and action controls (view details, compare, remove from favorites) arranged in a clean, left-aligned stack or grid. Spacing between text elements inside the card follows the 8–16px increments, with comfortable line height. The bottom of the card aligns with the top of the next card, separated by about 16–24px of white space, reinforcing the stacked, document-like feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card item within the list; it does not float or resize dynamically beyond standard responsive behavior (e.g., stacking image above text on narrow screens) and scrolls along with the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites pagination component is anchored at the very bottom of the main content column, appearing after the last chalet card in the list (or after the empty state when there are no favorites). It is centered horizontally within the column, occupying about 40–60% of the content width on desktop. The pagination controls (page numbers, next/previous arrows) are arranged in a single horizontal row with 8–12px spacing between items, and 8–12px vertical padding in the clickable area. There is a generous top margin of 32–40px separating pagination from the preceding content, which visually signals the end of the list, and a bottom margin of around 48–64px before any global footer, preserving a calm, open feel at the page’s end.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element that marks the end of the scrollable list; it remains part of the normal document flow and is not fixed to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites empty state shares the same vertical slot as the favorites list. When there are no favorites, it appears directly below the toolbar (and any error banner), centered within the content column. The empty-state container spans roughly 60–70% of the column’s width, effectively occupying about 40–50% of the viewport width on desktop, leaving visible white space around it. There is a generous top margin of 40–64px from the toolbar to visually distinguish it as a key message rather than a simple list item. Inside, a simple illustrative icon or outline graphic sits at the top center, followed by a prominent, center-aligned headline and one or two short, center-aligned explanatory lines with 8–16px vertical spacing. A primary action button (e.g., “Browse Chalets”) is placed below the text, also centered. The component has 24–32px internal padding with ample whitespace around its content, making it the clear focal point when shown, similar in emphasis to a top-level section heading in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that conditionally replaces the favorites list; it sits in the normal flow of the page and scrolls naturally, though its height is typically smaller than a full list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites loading indicator appears over the favorites list region whenever data is being fetched. Spatially, it is centered within the same horizontal bounds as the content column, aligning with the list edges. Vertically, it is anchored to the list’s approximate viewport area: on initial load, it appears about 24–32px below the toolbar, in the middle of the area where the first chalet cards would normally appear; on subsequent loads, it can overlay the existing list content. The indicator might be a circular spinner or subtle animated icon with a concise loading label beneath it, both centered. A semi-transparent white overlay can lightly dim the list area below while keeping the summary header and toolbar fully visible above, maintaining focus but not feeling heavy. The overlay respects the document-like layout by not covering the entire viewport—only the list’s content band—so the page structure remains legible even during loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays the favorites list area as an absolute-positioned layer within the content column, blocking interactions with underlying list items while allowing the rest of the page (header, toolbar) to remain visible; it disappears when loading completes and does not affect the natural scroll position.

name: Manage Booking
title: Manage Booking
description: Page where users look up an existing chalet booking using a booking reference code and the lead guest’s last name, then are routed to the booking details page if a match is found.

core_features: Display a booking lookup form with required fields for booking reference code and lead guest last name.
Perform basic client-side validation on the lookup form (e.g., non-empty fields) before triggering a lookup.
Call the lookupBookingByReference interface with the submitted referenceCode and lastName.

Handle the lookupBookingByReference response by either redirecting to booking_details.html with bookingId when booking_found is true, or showing an appropriate error state when booking_found is false.
Use URL parameters to prefill form values and to reflect lookup results (success or error) so that the page state can be restored via browser navigation or shared links.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Manual booking lookup
1. User navigates to manage_booking.html via the header "Manage booking" link (no URL parameters required).
2. Page initializes and displays the booking lookup form with empty reference code and last name fields.
3. User enters a booking reference code into the reference code field.
4. User enters the lead guest last name into the last name field.
5. User clicks the form submit button.
6. The booking-lookup-form component performs client-side validation to ensure both fields are not empty.
7. If validation fails, the component displays inline validation errors and does not call lookupBookingByReference.
8. If validation passes, the component calls lookupBookingByReference(referenceCode, lastName).
9. The lookup-response-handler receives the API response.
10. If response.success is true and response.booking_found is true, the handler reads response.booking.id and navigates to booking_details.html?bookingId={response.booking.id}.
11. If response.success is false or response.booking_found is false, the handler updates the URL with lookupStatus=error and errorCode={response.error_code} and sets an internal error state.
12. The lookup-error-panel reads the error state (or errorCode from the URL) and displays an appropriate error message to the user while keeping the form values intact so the user can correct and resubmit.

Workflow 2: Deep-link with prefilled values and auto-lookup
1. User arrives at manage_booking.html with referenceCode and lastName parameters in the URL (e.g., manage_booking.html?referenceCode={value}&lastName={value}).
2. The url-state-sync component reads these URL parameters during page load.
3. The booking-lookup-form component initializes its fields with the referenceCode and lastName values from the URL.
4. If both values are present and non-empty, the page may automatically trigger the lookupBookingByReference call (configurable behavior), or wait for the user to press submit.
5. On submit, the same validation and lookup steps as in Workflow 1 occur.
6. If a booking is found, the user is redirected to booking_details.html?bookingId={response.booking.id}.
7. If no booking is found, the URL is updated with lookupStatus=error and errorCode={response.error_code}, and the form remains prefilled with the URL-provided values.

Workflow 3: Returning to Manage Booking from the browser Back button
1. User successfully looked up a booking and is on booking_details.html.
2. User presses the browser Back button.
3. Browser returns to manage_booking.html with the previous URL (including any referenceCode, lastName, lookupStatus, and errorCode parameters).
4. The url-state-sync component reads these parameters.
5. The booking-lookup-form is re-populated with the previous referenceCode and lastName values.
6. The lookup-error-panel re-displays any previous error state if lookupStatus=error is present.
7. User can adjust the values and resubmit without needing to retype from scratch.
interactions: Form field input (reference code): User types or pastes a value into the reference code input field. onChange updates the component’s internal state for referenceCode and may clear any field-level validation errors for that field.
Form field input (last name): User types a value into the last name input field. onChange updates internal state for lastName and may clear any field-level validation errors for that field.

Field blur validation: When a user leaves a field (onBlur), the booking-lookup-form component can run lightweight validation, marking the field as invalid if it is empty and showing an inline validation message.

Form submission: User clicks the lookup button or presses Enter while focused in a form field. onSubmit prevents default browser behavior, performs validation on both fields, and if valid, calls lookupBookingByReference(referenceCode, lastName).

API response handling: After lookupBookingByReference resolves, the lookup-response-handler inspects success, booking_found, and error_code. If booking_found is true, it triggers a client-side navigation to booking_details.html with the bookingId parameter. If booking_found is false or success is false, it updates error state and the URL query string (lookupStatus, errorCode) and exposes this state to the lookup-error-panel.

Error message dismissal or override: When the user changes either form field after an error has been shown, onChange can clear or downgrade the global error state (while keeping errorCode in URL unchanged until the next submit) so that the interface reflects that the user is editing the input.

URL-driven state initialization: On page load or when the URL changes (e.g., via browser navigation), the url-state-sync component parses referenceCode, lastName, lookupStatus, and errorCode from the query string and updates the booking-lookup-form fields and lookup-error-panel state accordingly.

Optional auto-lookup trigger: If both referenceCode and lastName are present in the URL and no lookupStatus is present, url-state-sync may trigger an automatic onSubmit behavior for booking-lookup-form to perform the lookup without waiting for explicit user action.
page_functionality.state_logic: The manage_booking.html page uses URL query parameters to represent and restore page state. The main parameters are: referenceCode (string), lastName (string), lookupStatus (string), and errorCode (string). On initial load, url-state-sync reads referenceCode and lastName, and passes them to booking-lookup-form to prefill the form. If both values are present and non-empty and lookupStatus is not set, the page may automatically call lookupBookingByReference; otherwise, the user can trigger lookup by submitting the form. After each form submission, the lookup-response-handler updates the browser URL (using navigation or history APIs) to include the submitted referenceCode and lastName and either clears lookupStatus and errorCode (for a successful lookup that redirects away) or sets lookupStatus=error and errorCode to the value returned by lookupBookingByReference. The lookup-error-panel derives its display state from lookupStatus and errorCode. This approach ensures that using the Back/Forward buttons or sharing the URL will restore the same form values and, when applicable, the error state, without relying on localStorage.

components.functionality: Collects the booking reference code and lead guest last name from the user, performs basic client-side validation, and, on submit, triggers the booking lookup via lookupBookingByReference. It also initializes its fields from URL parameters when provided.
event_handlers: onInit: Read initial referenceCode and lastName values from the url-state-sync component and populate form fields.
onChange(referenceCodeField): Update internal referenceCode state and clear field-level validation errors associated with this field.
onChange(lastNameField): Update internal lastName state and clear field-level validation errors associated with this field.
onBlur(referenceCodeField): Validate that the field is non-empty; if empty, mark it invalid and expose a validation error for the lookup-error-panel or inline display.
onBlur(lastNameField): Validate that the field is non-empty; if empty, mark it invalid and expose a validation error.

onSubmit: Prevent default form submission, validate that referenceCode and lastName are both non-empty; if invalid, set validation errors and do not call any interface; if valid, invoke lookupBookingByReference with the current referenceCode and lastName and delegate the response handling to the lookup-response-handler component.

components.functionality: Receives and interprets the response from lookupBookingByReference, updates URL-based state, and controls navigation to the booking details page when a booking is found.
event_handlers: onLookupSuccess(response): If response.success is true and response.booking_found is true, read response.booking.id and navigate to booking_details.html?bookingId={response.booking.id}. Optionally update the current URL before navigation to include referenceCode and lastName for consistency.

onLookupNotFound(response): If response.success is true but response.booking_found is false, set a global error state with errorCode=response.error_code, update the URL to include referenceCode, lastName, lookupStatus=error, and errorCode, and notify lookup-error-panel to display an appropriate message.

onLookupError(response): If response.success is false, set an error state using errorCode=response.error_code (if provided), update the URL with lookupStatus=error and errorCode, and notify lookup-error-panel to display an error state.

onFormStateChange(referenceCode, lastName): When booking-lookup-form field values change, optionally update the URL’s referenceCode and lastName parameters in real time or on debounce to keep the URL in sync with the current form state.

components.functionality: Displays validation and lookup error messages based on current validation state and error codes, without embedding any booking data. It reacts both to client-side validation results and to error_code values returned by lookupBookingByReference.
event_handlers: onValidationStateChange(fieldErrors): Render or hide field-specific validation messages (e.g., required fields missing) based on updates from booking-lookup-form.

onLookupErrorStateChange(lookupStatus, errorCode): When lookupStatus indicates an error, map errorCode (such as values defined by the interface) to user-facing error messages. The component does not call APIs; it just interprets the current error state and displays appropriate feedback.

onInputChangeAfterError: When notified that the user has modified a form field following an error, optionally hide or downgrade the global error message to encourage resubmission with corrected details.

components.functionality: Synchronizes the booking lookup form and error panel state with URL query parameters. It reads parameters on load and when the URL changes, and writes parameters when the form is submitted or when state transitions occur.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Parse the current URL for referenceCode, lastName, lookupStatus, and errorCode query parameters. Provide referenceCode and lastName to booking-lookup-form for initialization, and lookupStatus and errorCode to lookup-error-panel for initial error display.
onHistoryChange: When browser navigation (Back/Forward) changes the URL, re-run parsing and update the state of booking-lookup-form and lookup-error-panel accordingly.

onFormSubmit(referenceCode, lastName): After a valid form submit is initiated, update the URL query parameters to include the current referenceCode and lastName (and remove or clear lookupStatus and errorCode) before or in parallel with calling lookupBookingByReference.

onLookupError(referenceCode, lastName, errorCode): When lookup-response-handler reports an error, write referenceCode, lastName, lookupStatus=error, and errorCode into the URL so that the error state and inputs can be restored by reloading or navigating back to the page.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid (Bootstrap-style), with content constrained in a centered max-width container and nav elements arranged in columns (logo, nav links, cart) within the same grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same stacked structure as the mockup: a slim full-width blue contact strip at the very top, then the full-width white navigation bar with logo on the left, nav links centered, and cart on the right. Below this global header, the 'Manage Booking' page content sits in a single centered column. At the top of this column is a large, centered page title ('Manage Booking'), optionally followed by one or two short left-aligned explanatory lines clarifying what information is needed. Directly beneath this explanatory text sits the booking lookup form as the main interactive element. Beneath the form, reserved space handles feedback: the error panel appears directly under the form when validation or lookup errors occur, and any transient lookup or loading messages managed by the response handler appear in the same vertical flow. The URL state sync has no visible presence and does not affect visual arrangement.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same stacked structure as the mockup: a slim full-width blue contact strip at the very top, then the full-width white navigation bar with logo on the left, nav links centered, and cart on the right. Below this global header, the 'Manage Booking' page content sits in a single centered column. At the top of this column is a large, centered page title ('Manage Booking'), optionally followed by one or two short left-aligned explanatory lines clarifying what information is needed. Directly beneath this explanatory text sits the booking lookup form as the main interactive element. Beneath the form, reserved space handles feedback: the error panel appears directly under the form when validation or lookup errors occur, and any transient lookup or loading messages managed by the response handler appear in the same vertical flow. The URL state sync has no visible presence and does not affect visual arrangement.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The contact strip and navigation together occupy roughly the top 10–15% of the viewport height on desktop, matching the mockup. The main content area beneath them is vertically generous, with the central content column spanning about 60–70% of the viewport width and centered, leaving 15–20% white space on either side. Within this column, the page title has a larger top margin from the nav (around 48–64px) and a noticeable bottom margin (24–32px) before any explanatory text. The explanatory text has moderate spacing (16px) before the form. The booking lookup form then stretches to the full width of this content column, with internal padding around 16–24px. The error panel and any response messages also span the width of the content column and are stacked vertically with 16–24px gaps from the elements above them. On smaller screens, the column approaches full width, but vertical stacking and spacing remain consistent and airy.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is intentionally moderate and reading-focused, similar to a policy page: generous line height, 16px vertical spacing between paragraphs or form rows, and clear separation between the title, explanatory text, and the form. The form itself is not cramped: each input field has ample vertical padding and 16px+ spacing between fields. Inline feedback like error text is clearly separated with small but distinct gaps to avoid visual crowding. No sidebars or extra columns compete for attention; the layout focuses on a simple, linear experience where users can easily read instructions and complete the form without distraction.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow mirrors the mockup: the eye first catches the blue contact strip and then naturally moves down to the logo and navigation. From there, attention drops into the main column, where the bold, centered 'Manage Booking' title acts as a key focal point. The eye then flows downward into the left-aligned explanatory text, which funnels attention into the booking lookup form. Within the form, the eye moves from top to bottom: first the booking reference field, then the lead guest last name, then the primary action button. If an error occurs, the error panel appears immediately below the form and stands out via color and spacing as the next thing the eye notices. Any loading or state messages managed by the response handler appear in this same vertical path, ensuring users see the result of their lookup without scanning elsewhere. The invisible URL state sync does not alter the visual flow.

layout.overall_description: The 'Manage Booking' page sits beneath the existing site-wide header: a slim, full-width blue contact strip at the very top and, directly under it, a white navigation bar containing the chalet brand logo aligned to the left, the main navigation links centered, and the cart icon or summary aligned to the right. Below this stacked header, the main content is centered within the viewport in a single, clean column that occupies roughly two-thirds of the page width on desktop, surrounded by generous white margins on both sides. At the top of this column, a large, bold, centered page title reading 'Manage Booking' establishes the purpose of the page. Immediately beneath, one or two concise, left-aligned lines explain that the user can look up an existing chalet booking by entering their booking reference and the lead guest’s last name. After a comfortable vertical gap, the booking lookup form sits as a simple, lightly framed block that fills the width of the content column. Form fields are stacked vertically with clear labels and spacing, leading the eye naturally down to a prominent submit button. Directly below the form, an unobtrusive area is reserved for state feedback: when appropriate, an error panel appears here as a full-width message block with a subtle, brand-consistent error styling, and any 'searching' or 'no booking found' messages managed by the response handler also occupy this region. The page overall feels airy and focused, using flat white backgrounds, confident blue accents, and document-like spacing to keep attention on the single task of looking up a booking, with no sidebars or competing elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking lookup form is the central interactive block of the page and sits within the main content column directly below the introductory text. Horizontally, it aligns with the column’s left and right edges, effectively spanning about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop and nearly full width on mobile. There is a vertical gap of about 24–32px between the last line of explanatory copy and the top edge of the form, following the spacious section spacing seen in the mockup. The form itself may be presented as a simple card with a subtle border or very light shadow, with internal padding of around 16–24px on all sides, preserving the text-focused, minimally styled corporate feel. Inside the form, labels are left-aligned and sit directly above each input field with roughly 8px spacing; input fields are full width within the form, with 16px vertical spacing between them. The first field for the booking reference appears at the top, the second field for the lead guest’s last name is directly beneath it, and the primary submit button sits below the second field with 16–24px vertical spacing. On desktop, if space allows, the two fields may optionally appear side by side occupying roughly half the form width each with a 16–24px gutter between them, but they still stack vertically on smaller viewports. The bottom of the form leaves a 24px gap before any error panel or state message block, ensuring the form remains visually distinct while still connected to its feedback area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The form scrolls naturally with the page content and is not fixed or sticky. It remains centered in the main content column beneath the header. On narrower viewports, it stretches to near full width with maintained vertical spacing between its internal elements, always preserving comfortable margins to the edges of the screen.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The lookup response handler is primarily a behavioral component but corresponds visually to a reserved feedback region directly beneath the booking lookup form. This region spans the full width of the same central content column, aligning perfectly with the left and right edges of the form above. By default, the area has no visible elements and effectively collapses, preserving a clean vertical flow. When in a loading or informational state (for example, showing 'Searching for your booking…' or confirming that a match is found before redirect), the handler renders a short message block starting approximately 16–24px below the bottom of the form. The message text is left-aligned to maintain the document-like reading flow, but it may be visually centered within the column by spacing alone. The message sits on a transparent or very lightly tinted background, using standard body text size with slightly emphasized typography (such as semi-bold or brand accent color) to make it noticeable without turning it into a dominant banner. When not active, this area takes up no noticeable vertical space, so it does not clutter the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This component is not fixed or sticky; it appears and disappears inline in the normal document flow directly under the form. Its visibility is entirely state-driven: when inactive, it has no visible footprint; when active, it pushes subsequent content downward in a smooth, natural way without overlapping other elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The lookup error panel occupies a clearly defined area immediately below the booking lookup form and shares the same centered alignment and width as the form and main content column. A vertical space of about 16px separates the bottom of the form from the top edge of the error panel, creating a direct visual link while still distinguishing the error as a separate block. The panel spans 100% of the content column width and is styled as a compact rectangular block with 12–16px padding on all sides. It uses a subtly tinted background in an error color (for example, a soft red or warm accent consistent with the brand) with a slightly darker border or left accent line to make it easily noticeable without overwhelming the minimal aesthetic. The error text is left-aligned, using standard body font size with bold or semi-bold emphasis on the first few words (e.g., 'We couldn't find that booking.') followed by a brief explanatory sentence. The panel’s height adjusts to its content but remains visually compact to preserve the overall clean, document-like appearance. When no error exists, the panel is not rendered or collapses entirely, so no blank box is visible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The error panel appears and disappears inline with the page content and scrolls naturally with the main column. It is not fixed or sticky and does not overlay the form; instead, it pushes content below it downward when visible. Its placement immediately below the form ensures the user sees the error message in the normal eye path after submitting the form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state sync component is entirely non-visual and has no direct spatial representation on the page. It operates behind the scenes at the page level, synchronizing the lookup state (such as booking reference and last name) with the URL query parameters. Conceptually, it is attached to the main content view rather than to a specific visible region. It does not occupy any width or height in the layout and does not alter the spacing or positioning of any visible elements, including the header, title, form, or feedback panels.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This component has no spatial behavior; it neither scrolls nor fixes to any part of the viewport. It functions purely as an invisible behavioral layer, listening to changes in the form and navigation state and updating the browser URL, while leaving the physical layout entirely unchanged.

name: Booking Details & Extras
title: Booking Details & Extras
description: View a confirmed chalet booking, review existing stay extras, configure additional services (airport transfer, daily breakfast, late checkout), see their combined cost, compare against a user-defined extras budget, and save changes to the booking.

core_features: Read the bookingId from the URL and load full booking details, including any existing extras, using getBookingDetailsWithExtras.
Load all available stay extras that can be attached to the booking (airport transfer, daily breakfast, late checkout) using getAvailableStayExtras.
Display a booking summary (chalet, dates, guests, reference code, pricing breakdown) based on the Booking data returned by getBookingDetailsWithExtras.
Display a structured list of all currently attached BookingExtra records (grouped by extra_type) and the extras_total and extras_per_adult returned by getBookingDetailsWithExtras.

Provide dedicated editors for each configurable extra type (airport_transfer, daily_breakfast, late_checkout) that let users add, change, or remove draft extras using StayExtra definitions and the booking context.

Calculate a draft combined extras cost on the client using StayExtra.base_price, StayExtra.pricing_model, and the user’s draft configuration so that users can see a running extras total before saving.

Provide an optional budget helper where users can enter their own extras budget (total or per-adult); the page compares the draft extras total and per-adult amount against this budget and visually indicates whether the draft configuration is within or above the user’s budget.

Support downgrading extras (for example, switching airport transfer from round_trip to one_way, or reducing guests_count or date range for breakfast) and immediately updating the draft cost calculation and budget indicator.

Allow users to apply their draft extras configuration by calling updateBookingExtras with structured parameters for airportTransfer, dailyBreakfast, and lateCheckout, then updating the page with the returned Booking and BookingExtra data.
Show confirmation or error states after updateBookingExtras returns, without leaving the page, and keep the URL bookingId in sync so users can refresh or return directly to this booking.
Provide a navigation link back to manage_booking.html for users who wish to look up a different booking.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: View booking details and existing extras
1. User lands on booking_details.html with a bookingId URL parameter.
2. The page reads bookingId from the query string.
3. The booking-details data loader calls getBookingDetailsWithExtras(bookingId).
4. In parallel, it calls getAvailableStayExtras(bookingId).
5. When both responses return, the booking summary component displays chalet, dates, guests, and pricing; the extras summary component lists current BookingExtra items and shows extras_total and extras_per_adult.
6. If there are no existing extras, the extras summary indicates that no extras are currently attached.

Workflow 2: Configure or modify extras with live cost feedback
1. With booking and stay extras loaded, the extras editor components initialize their forms based on any existing BookingExtra items.
2. User selects an extra to configure (e.g., airport transfer) via its editor section.
3. The relevant editor reads the appropriate StayExtra definition (matching extra_type) and the Booking context (e.g., nights, guest counts, dates).
4. As the user changes fields such as direction (for airport transfer), passengers_count, guests_count, date_from/date_to, or toggles an extra on or off, the page calculates draft line totals per extra using StayExtra.base_price and StayExtra.pricing_model.
5. The extras summary component aggregates all draft line totals into a draft extras total and draft extras_per_adult value and displays them alongside the currently saved extras totals for comparison.
6. If the user has entered a budget in the budget helper, the budget indicator compares the draft extras total and/or per-adult amount to the budget and shows whether the draft configuration fits the budget.

Workflow 3: Save extras changes to the booking
1. After adjusting extras, the user clicks the 'Update booking' action.
2. The action bar component gathers the current draft configurations from the extras editor components and normalizes them into three objects: airportTransfer, dailyBreakfast, and lateCheckout.
3. The page calls updateBookingExtras with parameters (bookingId, airportTransfer, dailyBreakfast, lateCheckout).
4. While waiting for the response, the action bar may display a disabled state for the update button and a loading indicator.
5. When updateBookingExtras returns success=true, the booking-details data in page state is replaced with the returned Booking, extras array, extras_total, extras_per_adult, and currency.
6. The draft state is reset to match the newly saved extras, the extras summary shows the updated totals, and a short in-page confirmation message is displayed.
7. If success=false or the call fails, an error message is shown, and the draft state is preserved so the user can correct input and retry.

Workflow 4: Use URL parameters and navigation
1. From manage_booking.html or booking_review.html, the user clicks a link such as 'View booking details & extras' which navigates to booking_details.html?bookingId={id}.
2. The page reads bookingId from the URL and loads data as described in Workflow 1.
3. If the user clicks 'Back to booking lookup', the page navigates to manage_booking.html (without altering booking data).
4. If the user adjusts the budget field, the page updates an extrasBudget (and optionally extrasBudgetPerAdult) URL parameter via history replacement so that reloading the page preserves the budget helper input.
5. If a deep-link parameter such as view is present in the URL (for example, view=airport_transfer), the page opens the corresponding extras editor section by default.
6. Changing sections (e.g., switching to breakfast editor) updates the view parameter in the URL so users can bookmark or share a link that opens directly to a specific extras section.
interactions: On initial load, when booking_details.html is opened with a bookingId query parameter, the page triggers getBookingDetailsWithExtras(bookingId) and getAvailableStayExtras(bookingId).
If bookingId is missing or empty in the URL, the booking loader component shows an inline error message and omits API calls, prompting the user to return to manage_booking.html.

When getBookingDetailsWithExtras returns, the booking summary component binds to the Booking data and renders chalet name, check_in/check_out dates, number of nights, adults_count, children_count, reference_code, and pricing fields including room_charges_total, extras_total, taxes, fees, and grand_total.
When getAvailableStayExtras returns, the extras editor components filter the StayExtra array by extra_type to initialize airport_transfer, daily_breakfast, and late_checkout configuration editors.
Clicking 'Back to booking lookup' on the page header navigates directly to manage_booking.html via a standard link, with no dependency on JavaScript state.

In the airport transfer editor, toggling the extra on or off updates the draft configuration for airportTransfer in page state and immediately recalculates the draft extras total and extras_per_adult shown in the extras summary.

Changing the direction field (e.g., selecting one_way or round_trip) in the airport transfer editor updates the airportTransfer draft configuration and triggers recalculation of that extra’s draft total using StayExtra.base_price and pricing_model.

Changing the passengers_count field in the airport transfer editor updates the draft configuration, with optional input validation based on Booking guest counts, and forces recalculation of the draft airport transfer total and the aggregated draft extras total.

In the daily breakfast editor, adjusting guests_count or the date range (date_from and date_to within the stay period) updates the dailyBreakfast draft configuration and the calculated draft breakfast total, based on StayExtra.pricing_model (e.g., per_guest_per_night).

In the late checkout editor, toggling the extra on or off or adjusting the checkout date (if configurable) updates the lateCheckout draft configuration and recalculates that extra’s draft total, which is then reflected in the aggregated draft extras total.

Whenever any extra editor changes the draft configuration, the extras summary recomputes a draft extras_total and draft extras_per_adult using the current draft line totals and Booking.adults_count, and displays these alongside the saved extras_total and extras_per_adult values from the Booking.

In the budget helper component, typing a numeric value into the total extras budget field updates an in-memory budget value and an extrasBudget URL parameter; the component compares this budget with the draft extras_total to show whether the user is under, at, or over budget.
Optionally, in the budget helper, typing a value into a per-adult budget field updates an extrasBudgetPerAdult URL parameter and triggers a comparison against the draft extras_per_adult value.

Clicking a 'Reset changes' or 'Revert to saved extras' control resets all draft extra configurations to match the currently saved BookingExtra array returned by getBookingDetailsWithExtras or the latest updateBookingExtras call, then recalculates draft totals to match the saved totals.

Clicking the 'Update booking' button gathers current draft configurations for airportTransfer, dailyBreakfast, and lateCheckout, formats them into the parameter structure accepted by updateBookingExtras, and sends updateBookingExtras(bookingId, airportTransfer, dailyBreakfast, lateCheckout).
While updateBookingExtras is in progress, the 'Update booking' button is disabled to prevent duplicate submissions, and a loading indicator or status text is shown within the action bar.

If updateBookingExtras returns success=true, the page replaces the in-memory Booking and BookingExtra data with the returned booking and extras values, updates the summary totals, clears any error messages, resets the dirty/draft flags, and optionally shows a non-blocking confirmation message.

If updateBookingExtras returns success=false or an error occurs, the page shows an inline error message in the action bar, retains the current draft configuration so the user can correct inputs, and re-enables the 'Update booking' button.

If the user navigates away or reloads the page, only state encoded in the URL (bookingId, view, extrasBudget, extrasBudgetPerAdult) persists; unsaved changes that exist only in memory will be lost, so the page may optionally show a confirmation on unload when there are unsaved draft changes.
