page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is keyed by the bookingId URL parameter. On load, the page parses bookingId from the query string. If bookingId is valid, it calls getBookingDetailsWithExtras(bookingId) and getAvailableStayExtras(bookingId). The Booking, BookingExtra list, extras_total, extras_per_adult, and currency returned from getBookingDetailsWithExtras constitute the 'saved' state for extras and pricing. Separately, the page maintains an in-memory 'draft extras configuration' representing the user’s current selections for airportTransfer, dailyBreakfast, and lateCheckout. This draft configuration is initialized from the saved BookingExtra array after initial load or after a successful updateBookingExtras response. Any change in the extras editors updates only the draft configuration and derived draft line totals and aggregate totals. The URL may also contain optional parameters: view, extrasBudget, and extrasBudgetPerAdult. The view parameter determines which extras editor section is initially expanded; interacting with tabs or section headers can update view via history.replaceState, enabling deep-linking without full page reloads. extrasBudget and extrasBudgetPerAdult define user-entered budget targets; when present, the budget helper initializes its fields from these values and compares them against the draft extras_total and draft extras_per_adult. No extras state is stored in localStorage or other persistent client storage; all persistent context is derived from the server responses and the URL parameters. Calling updateBookingExtras(bookingId, airportTransfer, dailyBreakfast, lateCheckout) replaces the saved Booking and BookingExtra data when successful, effectively syncing the draft configuration back to the authoritative backend state and resetting the draft/saved comparison.

components.functionality: Reads bookingId from the URL, invokes getBookingDetailsWithExtras and getAvailableStayExtras, and initializes page-level state for Booking, existing BookingExtras, and available StayExtras. Provides loading and error states and re-fetches data if bookingId in the URL changes.

Displays high-level booking information including chalet_name, reference_code, check_in, check_out, nights, guest counts, booking status, and a link back to manage_booking.html. Also shows base pricing breakdown (room_charges_total, taxes, fees, current extras_total, grand_total) using the Booking data.

Shows a list of currently attached extras grouped by extra_type using the BookingExtra array, including unit_price, total_price, passengers_count, guests_count, directions, and dates. Presents both saved extras_total and extras_per_adult from Booking, as well as draft extras totals derived from the draft configuration in memory. Highlights differences between saved and draft totals and indicates which extras have unsaved changes.

Provides a form to add, edit, or remove an airport transfer extra for the booking. It locates the relevant StayExtra entry where extra_type=airport_transfer and uses its pricing_model and base_price to compute draft totals as the user adjusts fields such as enabled/disabled toggle, direction (one_way or round_trip), passengers_count, and optionally quantity. It initializes from any existing BookingExtra items of type airport_transfer and updates the draft airportTransfer configuration used when calling updateBookingExtras.

Allows configuration of daily breakfast extras. It filters StayExtra for extra_type=daily_breakfast and reads its pricing_model (for example, per_guest_per_night) and base_price. The form supports fields such as enabling/disabling the breakfast extra, guests_count (limited by the booking’s total guests if desired), and date range (date_from, date_to) constrained within the booking’s check_in to check_out window. It initializes from any existing BookingExtra entries of type daily_breakfast, recalculates a draft breakfast total as inputs change, and updates the draft dailyBreakfast configuration for use in updateBookingExtras.

Enables users to add or remove a late checkout extra tied to the booking’s departure day. It uses the StayExtra entry where extra_type=late_checkout and its pricing_model/base_price to compute draft totals. The form typically binds the date field to the booking’s check_out date (or allows selecting a date within the stay constraints if needed). It initializes from existing BookingExtra entries of type late_checkout, keeps a draft lateCheckout configuration in sync with user input, and recalculates the draft total whenever the enabled state or date changes.

Provides inputs for the user to define an extras budget (either as a total budget for the booking or as a per-adult amount). It reads Booking.adults_count and the current draft extras_total and draft extras_per_adult, compares them to the entered budgets, and displays whether the draft configuration is within or above budget. It also synchronizes the budget values with URL parameters (e.g., extrasBudget and extrasBudgetPerAdult) so that the budget preference is preserved across reloads and sharable links.

Centralizes client-side calculation of draft line totals and aggregated totals for extras. It consumes the draft configurations for airportTransfer, dailyBreakfast, and lateCheckout along with their respective StayExtra definitions (base_price, pricing_model) and the Booking context (nights, adults_count, date ranges). It outputs per-extra draft total_price values, a draft extras_total, and a draft extras_per_adult used by the extras summary and budget helper components.

Displays the primary actions for managing extras, including 'Update booking' to persist changes and 'Cancel changes' or 'Revert to saved' to discard current drafts. It shows a concise comparison of saved vs draft extras_total and grand_total, indicates when there are unsaved changes, and triggers the updateBookingExtras API call with normalized airportTransfer, dailyBreakfast, and lateCheckout parameter objects based on the draft configuration.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid (Bootstrap-style), with content constrained in a centered max-width container and nav elements arranged in columns (logo, nav links, cart) within the same grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, centered content column beneath the existing site chrome (blue contact strip + white nav bar). Within this column, content is stacked in a linear, document-like order: page title at the top, then the booking summary, followed by an extras summary + budget context, then the individual extras editor forms in sequence, and finally the update action bar at the bottom. Non-visual logic components (data loader, calculation engine) sit on top of or behind this column without altering its basic vertical flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, centered content column beneath the existing site chrome (blue contact strip + white nav bar). Within this column, content is stacked in a linear, document-like order: page title at the top, then the booking summary, followed by an extras summary + budget context, then the individual extras editor forms in sequence, and finally the update action bar at the bottom. Non-visual logic components (data loader, calculation engine) sit on top of or behind this column without altering its basic vertical flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top 10–15% of the viewport is reserved for the existing header (contact strip + nav). The main content area below is a centered column occupying about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, with 15–20% whitespace margins on each side. Each major section (summary panels, each extras editor group, budget helper, and action bar) is separated vertically by 24–32px. Internal padding within panels and forms is 16–24px. On tablet and mobile, the main column expands toward 100% width with 16–24px side padding, keeping the same vertical rhythm.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is moderately spacious and text-focused, similar to a legal or policy page. Headings and section titles have distinct spacing above and below, paragraphs and form fields have comfortable line height, and there are no competing sidebars. Panels appear as light, card-like sections with clear separation but not heavy decoration. Controls and totals are grouped clearly and given breathing room, avoiding visual clutter despite the amount of information.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye moves from the bright top contact bar to the nav, then down to the centered page title. From there, the flow becomes a strong vertical scan: first the high-level booking summary, then the extras cost summary and budget indicator, and then each configurable extra in numbered order (airport transfer, daily breakfast, late checkout). At the bottom, attention is drawn to the action bar to finalize changes. The layout uses bold section headings, subtle card boundaries, and consistent spacing to create clear visual anchors guiding the user step-by-step.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s fixed or stacked header (blue contact strip and white navigation bar), the main content sits in a single centered column, approximately 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, surrounded by generous white margins. At the top of this column, the page title “Booking Details & Extras” is centered and set with extra top and bottom spacing, visually anchoring the page. Directly beneath, a full-width booking summary panel provides key stay information in a clean, card-like section. Next, an extras summary panel displays the current extras and their combined cost; immediately below this, a slim budget helper strip shows the user-defined extras budget and how the current selection compares. Below these summaries, three extras editor panels—Airport Transfer, Daily Breakfast, and Late Checkout—are stacked vertically, each introduced with a bold, left-aligned heading and neatly arranged form controls. Throughout the column, sections are separated by consistent 24–32px vertical gaps, preserving a calm, document-like feel. Behind the scenes, a calculation engine updates totals as changes are made, while a lightweight data loader can temporarily overlay the column during load or save operations. At the bottom of the content column, a prominent but minimally styled action bar spans the full column width (becoming sticky near the viewport bottom), drawing the user’s eye to primary actions such as saving changes, while still respecting the clean, corporate aesthetic and ample white space of the overall design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader is an overlay layer that sits on top of the entire main content column, beginning just below the white navigation bar and extending to the bottom of the Booking Details & Extras page content. When active, it covers approximately the same width as the central content column (about 60–70% of the viewport on desktop), aligned horizontally with that column and centered on the screen. It uses a semi-transparent white background over the underlying panels and forms, with a small, unobtrusive loading indicator centered both vertically and horizontally within this column area. The surrounding page margins and the header remain visible but slightly dimmed, reinforcing the idea that only the booking content is in a loading state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Absolutely positioned overlay within the main content area; toggles between visible and hidden. When visible, it floats above all other page components in the content column but does not affect their underlying layout or spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking summary header panel appears as the first card-like section directly beneath the centered page title, occupying the full width of the main content column. On desktop, this means it spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, centered, with ample white space on the left and right. There is a vertical gap of about 24–32px between the bottom of the page title and the top edge of this panel, giving the summary space to stand out. Inside the panel, content (guest name, dates, chalet name, etc.) is left-aligned, with 16–24px padding on all sides. The panel’s bottom edge is followed by another 24–32px of whitespace before the next section (extras summary), clearly separating it as the high-level context for everything that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element in the vertical flow; it does not scroll independently or stick, and it simply expands vertically as needed with its content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The extras summary panel sits immediately below the booking summary header, separated by approximately 24px of vertical whitespace. It also spans the full width of the main content column, aligning flush with the left and right edges of the booking summary panel, maintaining a clean single-column rhythm. Inside, the layout favors a clear, tabular-like alignment: on desktop, labels such as “Current extras” and “Total extras cost” are left-aligned, while key amounts are aligned to the right edge of the panel, using the same 16–24px internal padding as the booking summary. This creates a strong horizontal line for monetary values. The panel itself reads as a concise, document-style summary, with 24–32px of whitespace below it before the budget helper, ensuring it feels like a distinct informational anchor before the user moves into editing individual extras.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element; it sits in the natural vertical stack and grows vertically with content while maintaining fixed horizontal alignment to the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The airport transfer editor is the first extras editor block and appears below the budget helper (or directly under the extras summary panel on small viewports), separated from the section above by around 24–32px of whitespace. It spans the full width of the content column, maintaining the same left and right alignment as the summary panels. At the top of this block, a bold, left-aligned heading such as “1. Airport Transfer” sits with 16–24px of space above and below, clearly marking this as the first configurable section. Below the heading, form elements (e.g., radio buttons for one-way/round-trip, pick-up time fields, number of passengers) are arranged in a vertical layout with 12–16px spacing between each field group. On wider screens, related fields may form a simple two-column layout within the block, but they retain generous horizontal padding from the block’s edge. The bottom of this editor block has roughly 24–32px of whitespace before the next extras editor begins, reinforcing a clear separation between the different extras.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width section within the column that expands vertically with its form content; it does not stick or float and scrolls naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The daily breakfast editor appears directly beneath the airport transfer editor, separated by about 24–32px of vertical whitespace. It mirrors the same full-width card-like structure within the central column, ensuring a consistent visual pattern. A bold, left-aligned heading such as “2. Daily Breakfast” anchors the top of the block, aligned to the same left text column as the previous heading, with comfortable spacing above and below. Below the heading, configuration options (e.g., number of days, number of guests, dietary preferences) are arranged in a vertical flow. If multiple fields are closely related (e.g., adults vs children), they can sit side‑by‑side on desktop within the block but collapse to a single column on mobile, always keeping 16–24px internal padding. The bottom of this block again has 24–32px of whitespace before the next extras editor, reinforcing the sequential, document-like flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block stacked in the main column, with responsive internal layout (fields may reflow to one or two columns) but fixed alignment and spacing relative to other sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The late checkout editor is positioned below the daily breakfast editor, separated by about 24–32px of whitespace, and serves as the last of the extras editor blocks. It continues the same full-width, single-column card pattern within the central content area. A bold, left-aligned heading such as “3. Late Checkout” appears at the top, aligned visually with the previous section headings and spaced 16–24px from both the top of the block and the first form field. The form content (e.g., late checkout time selection, fee display) is organized vertically with moderate spacing between fields. Because it is the final configuration section, its bottom edge transitions into a slightly larger vertical gap (up to 32px) before the budget helper or action bar below, giving a moment of visual pause before the user reviews totals and commits changes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width content block in the vertical flow, with simple responsive behavior for its internal fields but no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The extras budget helper is a compact, horizontally oriented strip that appears in close proximity to the extras cost information, to keep budget context visible without breaking the single-column flow. On desktop, it is positioned immediately below the extras summary panel, separated by about 16–24px of whitespace, and spans the full width of the main content column. It has a light, subtle background tint or a fine border to distinguish it from pure body text, with 12–16px of internal vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding. Within the strip, the budget label and indicator (e.g., a simple progress bar or difference indicator) are aligned left, while the numeric budget vs current extras total can be aligned to the right edge of the strip, maintaining the layout pattern of left labels and right amounts. There is a 24–32px gap beneath the budget helper before the first extras editor begins, ensuring it reads as a summary/meta element rather than part of a specific extra.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the vertical flow by default. On larger screens, it may adopt a subtle sticky behavior within the main content column, pinning near the top of the viewport after the user scrolls past it, so that the budget context remains visible while editing extras, without overlapping the header or leaving the central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The extras draft calculation engine is a non-visual logic component and does not render any visible UI in the page. Conceptually, it sits behind the extras editors and summary panels, continuously recalculating extras totals and budget deltas as the user changes form fields. It does not occupy any explicit vertical or horizontal space within the content column, and it does not interrupt the document-like flow of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No visual footprint; it operates behind the scenes without affecting layout. It only influences the live values shown in the extras summary panel, budget helper, and action bar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The extras update action bar anchors the bottom of the Booking Details & Extras content experience. In the natural document flow, it appears below the final extras editor (and below any inline budget helper positioned at the bottom, if used), separated by a slightly larger vertical gap of 32px to reinforce its role as the final step. It spans the full width of the main content column, aligning with the left and right edges of the panels above. Within the bar, primary actions (e.g., “Save changes”) are aligned to the right, with secondary actions (e.g., “Discard changes”, “Reset”) aligned to the left or just to the right but visually lighter. The bar has 12–16px of vertical padding and uses the brand blue or a slightly tinted variant as its background, with white or high-contrast text, making it visually distinct yet still clean and flat. Above it, a small 8–12px shadow or top border line visually separates it from the scrolling content, signaling that it is an action boundary.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky footer within the main content area on tablet and desktop: once the user scrolls near the bottom of the content column, the action bar pins to the bottom edge of the viewport, remaining visible as the user reviews or adjusts extras. On mobile, it can span nearly the full viewport width with minimal side margins and remain anchored at the bottom of the screen, sitting above the browser’s UI, ensuring the primary action is always easily accessible while preserving the single-column reading flow above.

name: About
title: About
description: Informational page that dynamically loads and displays the chalet brand story, mission, location highlights, and trust-related information using the getAboutContent interface, and provides navigation to contact.html.

core_features: Request and display About content (brand_story, mission, location_highlights, quality_and_safety, partnerships_and_certifications) via getAboutContent when the page loads.
Render distinct sections for brand story, mission, location highlights, quality and safety, and partnerships/certifications, based entirely on the data returned by getAboutContent.
Provide in-page section navigation driven by URL parameters (e.g., ?section=mission) to focus or scroll to a specific About section.
Allow users to focus on a specific location highlight using a URL parameter (e.g., ?highlightIndex=1) and by clicking on list items.
Offer a 'Contact us' call-to-action that navigates to contact.html, optionally including URL parameters to preserve context (e.g., which section the user came from).
Handle loading and error states for the getAboutContent request, controlling whether sections render or fallback messaging is shown.
user_workflows:
View full About content:
1. User opens about.html without URL parameters.
2. The about-page-container component triggers getAboutContent on page load.
3. While data is loading, a loading state is shown.
4. When data is returned, the page renders all available sections (brand story, mission, location highlights, quality and safety, partnerships/certifications) using the fields provided by getAboutContent.
5. User can scroll to read the content or use the in-page navigation to jump between sections.

Open About page at a specific section:
1. User navigates to about.html with a section URL parameter (e.g., about.html?section=mission).
2. The about-page-container loads and calls getAboutContent.
3. Once data is loaded, the about-section-navigation evaluates the section parameter and sets the corresponding section as active.
4. The page scrolls or focuses to the requested section (for example, the mission section) and highlights it as active.
5. If the user clicks another section link in the navigation, the section parameter in the URL is updated and focus changes accordingly.

Deep link to a specific location highlight:
1. User opens about.html with highlightIndex specified (e.g., about.html?section=location&highlightIndex=2).
2. The about-page-container loads data via getAboutContent.
3. The about-location-highlights-list reads highlightIndex from the URL, verifies that it is within the bounds of the location_highlights array, and sets the corresponding highlight as focused/expanded.
4. The page scrolls to the location highlights area, and the selected highlight is visually indicated as active in the list (functionally: marked as selected and its full description shown).
5. If the user clicks another highlight in the list, the highlightIndex parameter in the URL updates to match the clicked item’s index, and focus shifts to the newly selected highlight.

Navigate from About page to Contact page:
1. User reads the About content and decides to get in touch.
2. The user clicks the 'Contact us' CTA rendered by the about-contact-cta component.
3. The click handler constructs a navigation URL to contact.html, optionally appending query parameters that encode the current section and/or highlightIndex (e.g., contact.html?from=about&section=mission).
4. Browser navigates to contact.html using this URL; no additional data is stored in localStorage.

Refine view using in-page navigation (without leaving About):
1. User is on about.html with data already loaded.
2. User clicks on one of the in-page navigation items (e.g., 'Mission', 'Location', 'Quality & Safety', 'Partnerships').
3. The about-section-navigation component updates the section parameter in the URL via history API or full reload, depending on implementation requirements.
4. The page scrolls to the corresponding section and marks it active.
5. If the URL changes to a different section (e.g., via back/forward navigation), components react to the URL change and update which section is active.
interactions:
Click section navigation item:
- Trigger: User clicks a link/button for a specific section (brand, mission, location, quality, partnerships).
- Effect: section parameter in the URL is set to the selected section key; the corresponding section is focused/scrolls into view; the active section state is updated in the navigation component.

Click a location highlight item in the list:
- Trigger: User clicks on a location highlight row/item.
- Effect: highlightIndex parameter in the URL updates to the index of the selected highlight; the selected highlight’s description is expanded or focused; previously selected highlight is collapsed or de-emphasized.

Load About content on page load:
- Trigger: about.html is opened or reloaded.
- Effect: about-page-container calls getAboutContent; while waiting, it sets a loading state; on success, it stores the returned data in the page state and notifies child components; on failure, it stores error state and shows an error message instead of the detailed sections.

React to URL parameter changes:
- Trigger: URL changes to include or modify section or highlightIndex (via user clicks, manual URL edits, or browser navigation).
- Effect: Section and highlight components recompute which section and highlight are active; scroll/focus behavior is applied based on the new state.

Click 'Contact us' CTA:
- Trigger: User clicks the contact call-to-action element.
- Effect: A navigation URL to contact.html is built, optionally including about-context parameters (e.g., from=about and section=currentActiveSectionKey); browser navigates to contact.html.

Retry loading after error:
- Trigger: If getAboutContent fails, user clicks a retry action.
- Effect: about-page-container calls getAboutContent again and repeats the loading-to-success or loading-to-error flow.
page_functionality.state_logic: The About page uses URL parameters to control which sections are active and which location highlight is selected, without relying on localStorage. Optional URL parameters include: section (string) to indicate the currently focused section, and highlightIndex (integer) to specify which item in location_highlights is considered active. On initial load, about-page-container reads these parameters and, after getAboutContent resolves, forwards the current section and parsed highlightIndex values to child components. If section is missing or invalid, a default section is used (for example, the first available section based on loaded content). If highlightIndex is missing, out of range, or not a valid integer, no specific highlight is pre-selected. Whenever the user interacts with the in-page navigation or clicks a highlight item, the components update the URL’s section or highlightIndex parameters using the browser’s navigation capabilities. Components subscribe to URL changes: when the URL changes (via user interaction or browser back/forward), they re-evaluate and update their active states. All rendered content uses only the data returned by getAboutContent; no static domain content is hardcoded on the page.

components.functionality: Initializes the About page, calls getAboutContent, manages loading and error states, parses URL parameters (section, highlightIndex), and passes loaded data and derived state to child components.
event_handlers: onLoad: Triggered when about.html loads; calls getAboutContent and reads section and highlightIndex from the current URL.
onDataLoaded: Triggered when getAboutContent resolves successfully; stores the result in page state and notifies child components to render content.
onError: Triggered when getAboutContent fails; sets an error state and exposes it to child components so they can show an appropriate error message.
onUrlChange: Triggered when the URL changes (e.g., browser back/forward or internal navigation); re-parses section and highlightIndex and updates the derived state shared with child components.

components.functionality: Displays navigation controls for available About sections (brand story, mission, location, quality & safety, partnerships) and updates the URL to reflect the active section.
event_handlers: onClickSection(sectionKey): Updates the section parameter in the URL to the selected sectionKey, and triggers a scroll/focus to the corresponding section container.
onUrlChange: Reads the current section parameter to update which navigation item is marked as active.

components.functionality: Renders the brand story portion of the About content when brand_story is available, and responds to being the active section (for example, when section=brand).
event_handlers: onVisible: Invoked when the section becomes active based on URL.section; can be used to scroll the section into view.
onDataUpdate: Invoked when a new brand_story value is received from getAboutContent; re-renders the content.

components.functionality: Displays the mission text from the About content and integrates with the section-based navigation logic.
event_handlers: onVisible: Triggered when URL.section matches the mission section key; scrolls or focuses this section.
onDataUpdate: Triggered when mission data changes; re-renders the mission content.

components.functionality: Iterates over location_highlights returned by getAboutContent, rendering each highlight’s title and description, and managing which highlight is active based on user clicks and the highlightIndex URL parameter.
event_handlers: onClickHighlight(index): Sets the selected highlight’s index as active; updates the highlightIndex parameter in the URL; expands or focuses the corresponding highlight content.
onUrlChange: Reads the current highlightIndex parameter, validates it against the available location_highlights, and sets the active highlight accordingly; if invalid, clears the active selection.
onDataUpdate: When location_highlights changes, re-evaluates highlightIndex to ensure it is still in range and updates the active item.

components.functionality: Shows quality and safety information provided by getAboutContent and participates in section-based navigation.
event_handlers: onVisible: Called when URL.section matches the quality/safety section key; scrolls or focuses this section.
onDataUpdate: Called when quality_and_safety content is updated; re-renders the section.

components.functionality: Displays partnerships and certifications information returned by getAboutContent, providing trust-building context for the user.
event_handlers: onVisible: Triggered when URL.section indicates the partnerships section is active; scrolls or focuses this section.
onDataUpdate: Triggered when partnerships_and_certifications content changes; re-renders this section.

components.functionality: Provides a 'Contact us' call-to-action that navigates the user from about.html to contact.html, preserving About page context in the URL.
event_handlers: onClick: Reads the current section and highlightIndex parameters; constructs a URL to contact.html that includes context parameters (e.g., from=about and the current section/highlightIndex where applicable); triggers navigation to contact.html.
onUrlChange: Updates internal state so that subsequent clicks reflect the latest section/highlightIndex values.

The page ends on a visually stronger contact CTA that anchors the bottom and gently redirects the user toward the Contact page.

layout.overall_description: Below the existing header stack, the About page presents a single, centered column of content floating in generous white space. This column, roughly two-thirds the width of the screen on desktop, begins with the dynamically loaded About content container, which immediately reveals a prominent, centered page title with extra breathing room above and below. Just underneath, a slim, horizontal section navigation row spans most of the column width, giving quick access to Brand Story, Mission, Location Highlights, Quality & Safety, and Partnerships. As the user scrolls, each section appears as a bold, left-aligned heading anchored to the same vertical line, followed by comfortably spaced paragraphs and a clean list for location highlights. The rhythm is steady and document-like: clear blocks of text, 24–32px gaps between sections, and consistent alignment that encourages straightforward reading. At the bottom, a wider, more visually emphasized contact call-to-action bar or card spans nearly the full width of the content column, using the brand’s blue and white palette to stand out while still fitting the clean, corporate resort aesthetic. The entire page feels like a polished, readable document framed within a modern, minimal web layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-page-container sits directly beneath the global header stack, occupying the full available width of the page but constraining its inner content to a centered column about 60–70% of the viewport width (roughly an 8/12 grid span). It applies top padding of about 32px from the bottom edge of the navigation bar and bottom padding of about 32–48px before the footer begins. Horizontally, the container centers the content column within the viewport, leaving 15–20% whitespace on each side on desktop; on tablet and mobile, the column width increases relative to the viewport but always retains at least 16px side padding. All About sections, including the section navigation and CTA, are stacked vertically inside this container using the same left alignment for text and consistent spacing increments from the 8px scale.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static wrapper that defines the main content column and padding; scrolls normally with the rest of the page and does not have fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-section-navigation component is positioned near the top of the about-page-container, directly below the centered About page title and any brief introductory copy. It appears as a slim, horizontal strip of text-based navigation links (e.g., Brand Story, Mission, Location, Quality & Safety, Partnerships) arranged in a single row and centered within the content column. The navigation row spans about 80–90% of the content column width, leaving small left/right in-column margins so it doesn’t feel edge-to-edge. Each link has horizontal padding of roughly 16–24px and vertical padding of about 8–12px, following the navigation spacing pattern from the mockup. The row has about 16–24px margin above it (from the title) and 24px margin below it before the Brand Story section begins. The overall appearance is light and text-focused, with subtle bottom border or underline states in the brand blue when a section is active to support orientation without overpowering the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal navigation within the content flow; scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page. No sticky behavior, maintaining the clean document-like scrolling pattern.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-brand-story-section is the first major content block after the section navigation and acts as the narrative starting point for the About page. It occupies the full width of the centered content column, aligning all text to the left with generous line-height. The section begins with a bold, slightly larger heading (e.g., “Our Brand Story”) that sits on the same left vertical alignment as all subsequent headings. There is about 24–32px of vertical space above this heading (from the section nav) and about 16px below it before the body text starts. The body consists of one or more paragraphs that run the full column width, with 16px spacing between paragraphs. The total vertical height of this section is moderate, reflecting a primary information block but still allowing easy scanning downward to subsequent sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width content block within the column; scrolls in sequence with the rest of the document without any special behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-mission-section is stacked directly beneath the Brand Story section, separated by a clear section gap of about 24–32px to visually distinguish it as a new chapter in the narrative. It also spans the full width of the content column with left-aligned text. A bold heading (e.g., “Our Mission”) sits at the top of the section, aligned to the same left baseline as the other headings, with 0–8px margin above if the previous section’s bottom margin already provides adequate separation and about 16px margin below. Its paragraphs follow the same typographic rhythm: comfortable line-height, 16px spacing between paragraphs, and no additional columns or side elements. The section’s overall height is similar to or slightly shorter than the Brand Story, depending on text length, preserving a balanced vertical rhythm down the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block, following the linear document flow; no special positioning or interaction.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-location-highlights-list appears as the core content of the Location Highlights area and is placed directly beneath its corresponding heading, which follows the Mission section after a 24–32px vertical gap. The heading (e.g., “Location Highlights”) is left-aligned and given about 16px of space below before the list begins. The list itself occupies the full width of the content column but is formatted as a vertically stacked list of items (bullets, icons, or simple labels) with each item on its own line. Each list item has approximately 8–12px vertical spacing from the next, preserving the airy feeling of the page. If icons or markers are used, they are small and aligned to the left of the text, staying within the same text column without forming a separate visual column. The list can be one or two short paragraphs per item, but each remains within the single text column width, avoiding multi-column grids to maintain the legal-document-like reading flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static list component embedded in the section; scrolls naturally with the page and does not change position or layout on scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-quality-safety-section is placed beneath the Location Highlights section, again separated by about 24–32px of vertical whitespace, mirroring the spacing between other major sections. It aligns to the same left baseline, beginning with a bold heading (e.g., “Quality & Safety Standards”) that visually anchors the section. The heading has about 16px of space below before the paragraphs of text begin. Content is presented as one or more paragraphs, possibly including short subpoints, all constrained to the main text column width. Paragraphs have 16px spacing between them and a comfortable line height to support dense information (e.g., safety procedures, certifications) without appearing cramped. The section’s visual weight is similar to the Mission section, emphasizing trust and reliability while maintaining a calm, uncluttered appearance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically stacked section in the main flow; it does not float or stick and is read sequentially like a section in a legal document.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-partnerships-section sits below the Quality & Safety section, divided by a standard 24–32px vertical gap that marks it as its own block. It follows the same structural pattern: a left-aligned, bold heading (e.g., “Partnerships & Affiliations”) at the top, with about 16px of spacing before the copy. Content within this section may mention partner brands, associations, or service providers, and is organized as paragraphs or a short, inline list, all kept within the single column width. If partner names are emphasized, they use typographic emphasis (bold text) rather than logos or multi-column layouts, maintaining the restrained corporate aesthetic. The total height of this section is moderate, ensuring that the final bottom CTA remains visually close and easily reachable as the user scrolls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section within the main flow; no sticky or floating behavior, preserving the simple top-to-bottom reading experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-contact-cta is positioned as the final block within the about-page-container, directly below the Partnerships section with a slightly more generous top margin of about 32–48px to distinguish it as a concluding action area. It spans nearly the full width of the content column (close to 100% of that column) and is visually framed as a card or bar with a subtle background contrast—likely the brand blue or a light blue tint against the overall white page. Inside this CTA, text is left-aligned and concise, such as a short invitation to get in touch, accompanied by a clearly styled button or text link leading to contact.html. Internal padding is more generous than standard text blocks (approximately 16–24px on all sides), giving the element more presence. On desktop, the button may sit to the right of the text on the same row or below the text with 16px vertical spacing, while still respecting the single-column feel. This CTA anchors the bottom of the page visually and functionally, providing a strong but not overwhelming endpoint.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, bottom-anchored call-to-action within the container; it scrolls into view as the user reaches the end of the content and remains in the normal document flow without fixed positioning.

name: Contact
title: Contact
description: Page where users can view chalet/store contact details and send inquiries related to general questions, bookings, or store orders.

core_features: Fetch and display current contact details (email, phone numbers, address, response time expectation) using getContactInfo.
Provide a unified contact form that submits general, booking-related, or store-order-related inquiries via submitContactForm.
Preconfigure the contact form (topic and reference fields) based on URL parameters such as topic, bookingReference, and orderNumber.
Show response time expectations and highlight alternative contact options (e.g., emergency phone) based on getContactInfo.
Display submission result feedback (success/failure message and case_id) after submitContactForm completes.
Offer navigation back to the home page (index.html) via a dedicated link.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – General inquiry from footer link: User clicks the 'Contact' link in the footer on any page > Browser navigates to contact.html (no URL parameters) > On load, the page calls getContactInfo and displays email, phone, emergency phone, address, and response time > The contact form initializes with topic unset/default, bookingReference and orderNumber empty > User enters their name, email, selects 'General question' as topic (or equivalent option), writes a message, and submits the form > Page calls submitContactForm with the entered values and empty bookingReference/orderNumber > On success, the page displays a confirmation message using the returned message and case_id and may update the URL to include status=success and caseId=<returned_case_id> so that a reload shows the same confirmation state without resubmitting > User may click 'Back to home' to navigate to index.html.

Workflow 2 – Booking-related question with prefilled reference: A user from a booking-related area (e.g., a confirmation page) is sent to contact.html?topic=booking&bookingReference=<booking_reference_code> > On load, the page parses the URL parameters and sets the form topic to 'Booking question' (or equivalent) and pre-fills the booking reference field with the provided bookingReference value, while leaving orderNumber empty > The page calls getContactInfo and displays contact details and response-time expectations, including any emergency phone > User enters their name, email, and message (optionally editing the booking reference) and submits the form > Page calls submitContactForm with name, email, topic='booking', message, bookingReference (from the field), and an empty orderNumber > On success, the page shows a booking-context confirmation (based solely on submitContactForm response) and may update the URL to include status=success&caseId=<returned_case_id>&topic=booking&bookingReference=<submitted_reference> > User can use the 'Back to home' link to return to index.html.

Workflow 3 – Store order issue with prefilled order number: User clicks a help link from an order confirmation flow that navigates to contact.html?topic=order&orderNumber=<order_number> > On load, the page reads topic and orderNumber URL parameters, sets the form topic to 'Store order issue' (or equivalent), and pre-fills the order number field with the provided orderNumber while leaving bookingReference empty > The page calls getContactInfo and shows contact details and response-time expectations > User completes name, email, message, and optionally adjusts the order number, then submits > Page sends submitContactForm with name, email, topic='order', message, orderNumber from the field, and empty bookingReference > On success, the page displays the returned confirmation message and case_id and may reflect this state in the URL (status=success&caseId=<returned_case_id>&topic=order&orderNumber=<submitted_number>) > User can return to home via the 'Back to home' link.

Workflow 4 – Follow-up visit after form submission: After a successful submission, the page may redirect or update the URL to include status and caseId, e.g., contact.html?status=success&caseId=<returned_case_id>&topic=<topic_value>&bookingReference=<value_if_any>&orderNumber=<value_if_any> > When the user reloads or returns to this URL, the page parses the parameters > If status=success and caseId is present, the page shows a non-blocking confirmation banner with the status and encourages the user not to resubmit the same request, while keeping the form available for new messages (initialized from the remaining URL params) > If status indicates an error (e.g., status=error with an optional errorMessage parameter), the page shows an error banner and leaves the form populated based on URL parameters so that the user can try resubmitting.

Workflow 5 – Emergency or urgent stay issue: User reaches contact.html (with or without URL parameters) when they have an urgent stay issue > On load, the page calls getContactInfo and displays emergency_phone along with other contact details > If the topic URL parameter is emergency, the page sets the topic selector to an 'Urgent stay issue' value and may emphasize the emergency phone detail in the information panel (functionally, not visually defined here) > User decides whether to call the emergency_phone number directly (clicking the phone link triggers the device dialer) or still submit the contact form with topic set to an urgent value > If the form is submitted, submitContactForm is called with topic='emergency' (or equivalent), and the resulting confirmation is shown as in other workflows.
interactions: On page load, a controller component reads the current URL query parameters (e.g., topic, bookingReference, orderNumber, message, status, caseId, fromPage) and initializes the internal page state accordingly.

On page load, the same controller triggers a call to getContactInfo with no parameters; when the response arrives, it updates the contact-info display component with email, phone, emergency_phone, address fields, and response_time_expectation.

When the user interacts with the topic selector in the contact form, the selected topic value updates the internal form state and may control visibility/requirement of contextual fields (e.g., showing bookingReference when topic indicates a booking question, showing orderNumber when topic indicates a store order issue).

When the user types into the name, email, message, bookingReference, or orderNumber fields, the form state is updated in real time; validation can be performed before enabling submission (for example, ensuring required fields for a given topic are non-empty).

When the user clicks the primary 'Submit' button on the contact form, client-side validation runs; if validation passes, submitContactForm is called with the current form state (name, email, topic, message, bookingReference, orderNumber) and the submit button is temporarily disabled or marked as loading until a response is received.

When submitContactForm resolves successfully (success=true), the page updates the submission-status component with the returned case_id and message, and may update the URL using the History API to include status=success and caseId=<returned_case_id> (plus the current topic and reference/order values) without triggering a full reload.

When submitContactForm resolves with success=false or returns an error at network level, the page updates the submission-status component to show an error message and may update the URL with status=error and an optional errorMessage parameter, without clearing the current form values.

When the user clicks the main email address in the contact info panel, a mailto: link is triggered using getContactInfo.email, opening the default email client; this interaction does not call any additional interface.

When the user clicks the main phone or emergency_phone fields in the contact info panel, tel: links are triggered using the corresponding values from getContactInfo, allowing the device to initiate a call.

When the user clicks an address or 'View map' link derived from getContactInfo address fields, the browser navigates to a mapping service URL constructed from the dynamic address (if implemented), without hardcoded address data.
When the user clicks the 'Back to home' link, the browser navigates to index.html; optionally, a from=contact query parameter can be appended so the home page can adjust its behavior if needed.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Contact page uses URL query parameters to control initial state and preserve context across reloads. On every load, a page-level controller parses the location.search string and derives a state object. Supported parameters include: topic – a string used to preselect the topic within the contact form (e.g., values aligned with internal topic options such as 'general', 'booking', 'order', 'emergency'); bookingReference – if present, initializes the booking reference field and also forces the topic to a booking-related option if topic is not explicitly set; orderNumber – if present, initializes the order number field and also forces the topic to an order-related option if topic is not explicitly set; message – if present, initializes the message textarea with that content; fromPage – an opaque value indicating the source page (e.g., 'booking-confirmation' or 'order-summary'); this can be used only for analytics or UX hints and does not call any additional interfaces; status – used to control the submission-status component; for example, status=success or status=error informs whether to render a success or error banner without re-calling submitContactForm; caseId – if present and status=success, its value is shown in the confirmation banner as the user’s reference; errorMessage – if present and status=error, its value is displayed in the error banner. When the user successfully submits the form, the page may update the URL using the History API (e.g., history.replaceState) so that topic, bookingReference, and orderNumber reflect the submitted values, and status=success and caseId=<returned_case_id> are set. This avoids resubmitting on reload while preserving context and feedback. When submission fails, the URL may be similarly updated with status=error and an errorMessage, but the form’s internal state (name, email, message, bookingReference, orderNumber, and topic) remains unchanged. No form values are stored in localStorage or other persistent client storage; the only persisted state across visits is via URL parameters.

components.functionality: Initializes and coordinates the Contact page. On load, it parses URL query parameters to derive initial state (topic, bookingReference, orderNumber, message, status, caseId, fromPage, errorMessage), triggers getContactInfo, and distributes both the API response and derived state to child components. It also listens to events from the contact-form and submission-status components (e.g., formSubmitted, submissionResultUpdated) and may update the URL using the History API to reflect the latest submission status and context without causing a full page reload.

Displays the dynamic contact information returned by getContactInfo, including the primary email, phone, emergency phone, physical address lines, city, region, postal code, country, and the response_time_expectation text. Provides interactive links for email (mailto:), phone (tel:), emergency phone (tel:), and optionally a 'view map' link built from the address data. Also surfaces the response-time expectation as explanatory text to set user expectations before they use the form.

Highlights the response_time_expectation value obtained from getContactInfo in a concise banner near the contact form. This component is purely informational and updates automatically when getContactInfo returns new data or if the page controller refreshes contact information.

Manages the selection of the inquiry topic within the contact form. It initializes its selected value based on the topic URL parameter (or other inferred rules from bookingReference/orderNumber) and exposes the selected value to the parent contact-form component. Changes in selection influence which contextual fields (bookingReference or orderNumber) are visible and/or required.

Handles the bookingReference input field when the inquiry topic indicates a booking-related question. It initializes from the bookingReference URL parameter if present, updates the parent form state on every change, and participates in validation (e.g., can be required when the topic is booking-focused). This field’s visibility and requirement are controlled by the selected topic.

Handles the orderNumber input field when the inquiry topic indicates a store order issue. It initializes from the orderNumber URL parameter if present, updates the parent form state when edited, and is validated as needed when the topic is order-related. Its visibility and required status depend on the current topic.

Represents the main message textarea used for the user’s inquiry. It can be pre-populated from a message URL parameter if provided. The component keeps its value in sync with the parent contact-form state, and participates in required-field validation before submission.

Provides the primary interaction for sending inquiries. Contains fields for name, email, topic (via contact-topic-selector), message (via contact-message-field), and contextual references (booking-context-field and order-context-field). It initializes its values from URL parameters where possible, validates inputs based on the current topic, and on submit calls submitContactForm with parameters: name, email, topic, message, bookingReference, and orderNumber. It then triggers events to inform the page controller of submission progress and results, enabling status display and URL state updates.

Shows feedback about the result of the last form submission. When status and caseId URL parameters or submitContactForm results indicate success, it displays the API-provided message and case_id. When a failure occurs (as indicated by submitContactForm or URL status=error), it displays an error message (from the interface response or an errorMessage URL parameter). It does not itself call submitContactForm, but reacts to events and URL-derived state.

Renders an actionable link labeled functionally (e.g., 'Back to home') that navigates the user to index.html. Optionally, it can include a from=contact query parameter (and optionally topic or caseId) when generating the destination URL so that the home page can adjust its behavior if it is designed to do so.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid (Bootstrap-style), with content constrained in a centered max-width container and nav elements arranged in columns (logo, nav links, cart) within the same grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom, single-column document layout beneath the existing global header (blue contact strip + white navigation bar). All Contact page components sit inside a centered main content column. At the top of this column, the page controller presents the page title and introductory framing, followed by the contact info panel. Immediately beneath that, the response-time banner reassures users before they reach the contact form. The form itself is a vertically stacked sequence of fields (topic selector, contextual fields, message field) grouped visually as one coherent block. After the form, the submission-status banner appears, followed by a subtle back-to-home link aligned with the reading flow at the bottom.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom, single-column document layout beneath the existing global header (blue contact strip + white navigation bar). All Contact page components sit inside a centered main content column. At the top of this column, the page controller presents the page title and introductory framing, followed by the contact info panel. Immediately beneath that, the response-time banner reassures users before they reach the contact form. The form itself is a vertically stacked sequence of fields (topic selector, contextual fields, message field) grouped visually as one coherent block. After the form, the submission-status banner appears, followed by a subtle back-to-home link aligned with the reading flow at the bottom.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content column occupies roughly 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, centered, with 15–20% white margins on each side. Vertically, the header stack (contact strip and nav) use the top 10–15% of viewport height; the remaining space is dedicated to the Contact page content. Major blocks (page title/intro, contact info panel, response-time banner, form, status banner, back link) are separated with 24–32px vertical spacing, while internal spacing within the form and panels uses 16–24px padding. No sidebars or multi-column content compete for space; all components align to the same left edge within the content column.
layout_strategies.content_density: Text and form elements are spaced for comfortable reading and interaction. Headings and labels have clear breathing room above and below; paragraphs in the info panel follow a 16px vertical rhythm. Form fields are not tightly packed: each control has ~16–24px vertical gaps to avoid crowding. The layout emphasizes clarity over density, mirroring a legal/policy page: information-rich but visually calm, with ample white space around each functional block.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: After the site-wide header draws initial attention, the eye naturally drops to the centered Contact page title managed by the page controller. From there, the flow moves down the left-aligned content: first the concise contact info panel (how to reach the chalet/store), then the response-time banner as a reassuring highlight. The gaze continues to the prominent contact form, reading from top to bottom through the topic selector, contextual fields, and message area. Once the form is submitted, attention is drawn to the submission-status banner just below, before finally landing on the back-to-home link at the bottom left, which acts as a gentle exit route.

layout.overall_description: Below the global blue contact strip and white navigation bar, the Contact page content sits within a centered, white main column that spans about two-thirds of the viewport width on desktop, framed by generous margins. At the top of this column, the page controller presents a bold, centered “Contact” title with a brief left-aligned introductory line directly beneath, establishing a clear entry point. Just below, the contact info panel appears as a simple, text-first block giving email, phone, and location details, aligned to the left within the same column. A short distance below that, a horizontal response-time banner stretches across the full width of the content column, visually separated like a thin, subtle card that users encounter just before the form. The contact form itself dominates the central portion of the page: a vertically stacked set of full-width fields starting with a contact-topic selector, then context fields (booking or order details) that appear directly below the selector, followed by a larger multi-line message field. The entire form reads top-to-bottom without side-by-side fields, reinforcing the document-like flow. After the form, a submission-status banner appears in-line as the next block, occupying the same width and left alignment as the form, giving feedback without disrupting the layout. Finally, at the very bottom of the content column, a understated back-to-home link rests aligned to the left, separated by a bit of extra vertical space, acting as a quiet exit element that still conforms to the same single-column, text-focused design language.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-page-controller defines the main content region beneath the global header. It creates a centered content column that spans approximately 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, with equal white margins on both sides. At the top of this column, it places the page title — a large, bold, centered heading — at roughly 24–32px below the navigation bar. Directly under the title, it reserves space for a short, left-aligned introductory sentence or subheading, aligned to the same left edge as the body text and form fields. All subsequent components (contact-info-panel, response-time-banner, contact-form, submission-status-banner, back-to-home-link) are stacked vertically inside this column, each separated by about 24–32px of vertical space, maintaining a consistent single-column reading flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a static, full-height page container controlling the central column alignment and vertical stacking; it scrolls naturally with the page and does not have fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-info-panel sits directly below the page title and intro within the main content column, separated by roughly 24px of vertical space. It occupies the full width of the content column but maintains internal padding of about 16–24px on all sides if rendered as a card, or simply uses that spacing as margins if rendered as plain text. The content is left-aligned: heading-like text such as “Contact Details” appears first, followed by stacked lines for phone, email, and address with about 8–16px between each line, forming a compact, easy-to-scan block. The panel does not extend beyond the content column; its left edge lines up with the left edge of paragraphs and form labels, while its right edge aligns with the right edge of the column, preserving the clean, document-like appearance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element that sits in the vertical flow; no fixed or floating behavior. On smaller screens, it simply shrinks with the column and remains full-width within the safe content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The response-time-banner is positioned immediately below the contact-info-panel, with about 24px of vertical spacing separating them. It appears as a slim, horizontally oriented band that spans the full width of the main content column. The banner height is modest — roughly 2–3 lines of text plus padding — with internal vertical padding of about 8–12px and horizontal padding of 16–24px. Its background can use a light brand-tinted or subtle neutral shade to distinguish it from the white page but remain low-key. Text is left-aligned, starting at the same left edge as the contact info text, and concisely communicates typical response times. This banner serves as a visual prelude to the form below, creating a small visual break without feeling like a heavy card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline banner that sits within the scrollable column; it does not stick or float and shifts responsively with the column width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the contact-form, the contact-topic-selector is placed as the first form control at the top of the form block, roughly 24–32px below the response-time-banner. It spans the full width of the content column minus standard internal form padding (around 16–24px horizontally if the form is card-styled). The label is left-aligned and positioned directly above the selector field with about 4–8px spacing; the select input itself has a comfortable vertical height (standard input height plus some breathing room). There is approximately 16–24px of vertical space below this selector before the next context-specific field appears, ensuring a clean visual separation between controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard full-width form control within the form; its vertical placement does not change with scrolling. The field may conditionally reveal or hide the booking-context-field and order-context-field directly beneath it without affecting the single-column flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-context-field is positioned directly under the contact-topic-selector within the same form, appearing only when a booking-related topic is chosen. It aligns to the same left and right edges as the selector and other fields, occupying the full usable width of the form area. Its label is left-aligned above the input with 4–8px spacing, and the field itself is stacked vertically with about 16–24px of space below it before the next visible field in the form. When visible, it feels like an integral part of the linear form flow, adding at most a single additional block of height without breaking the straightforward vertical structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A conditional, static block that appears or disappears in-place without altering the overall column structure; it does not float or overlay other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-context-field occupies the same positional slot as the booking-context-field but is used when an order-related topic is selected. It is placed directly beneath the contact-topic-selector, aligning with the same left and right edges of the form fields. Like the booking field, its label sits above the input with 4–8px spacing, and a 16–24px gap separates it from the field below. Only one of the two context fields (booking or order) is visible at a time, so visually this area of the form behaves as a single contextual block that expands or contracts without disturbing the vertical reading rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A conditional static block; it toggles visibility within the flow but remains anchored in its position relative to the selector and message field.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-message-field appears below whichever context field is active (or directly below the topic selector if no context field is needed), separated by roughly 24px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the form area and is the tallest individual form control, with a multi-line textarea height that visually occupies about 2–3 times the height of a standard input. The label is left-aligned above the message box, with 4–8px spacing; inside the box, there is comfortable internal padding consistent with the rest of the form. A 24–32px vertical gap is maintained beneath this field before any form actions (e.g., submit button) and the end of the form, emphasizing its importance as the main content area for user input.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static full-width textarea within the form; it grows vertically within its block if resizable on some devices but does not alter its alignment or general placement.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form is the central interactive block on the page and sits about 24–32px below the response-time-banner. It is rendered as a single, vertical form card occupying the full width of the content column, with 16–24px internal padding on all sides. All form controls (contact-topic-selector, booking-context-field, order-context-field, contact-message-field, plus any submit button) are stacked top-to-bottom, aligned to the same left edge, and never placed side-by-side, preserving the single-column reading experience. Each control has 16–24px of vertical spacing from the next, and any call-to-action button is placed directly under the message field, aligned left or center within the form width. The top of the form likely includes a small section label like “Send us a message,” left-aligned, with 8–16px spacing above the first field. The form’s bottom aligns with the rest of the column, leaving 24–32px of space before the submission-status-banner below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level component that scrolls with the page. On smaller viewports, it takes nearly 100% of the screen width with consistent internal padding, remaining a single column without horizontal scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission-status-banner is positioned immediately below the contact-form, separated by about 24px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the main content column, mirroring the width and alignment of the form above. When active, it appears as a slim, horizontally oriented status strip with 8–12px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, using color (e.g., a soft green for success or a muted red for error) to visually differentiate it from the rest of the content. The text is left-aligned and concise, starting at the same left edge as the form labels. When inactive, the space collapses so that the banner does not leave an empty gap, preserving the clean document-like flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contextual, inline banner that appears or disappears within the vertical stack; it scrolls with the page and does not stick to the top or bottom of the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-home-link is placed at the very bottom of the content column, roughly 32px below the submission-status-banner (or directly below the form if the banner is not present). It is left-aligned with the text and form fields above, appearing as a simple text link or icon-plus-text treatment with no heavy background. The link does not span the full width; instead it occupies only as much horizontal space as the text needs, surrounded by generous white space above and below so it feels like a quiet, secondary action. On smaller screens, it remains aligned to the left margin of the main content area, staying consistent with the overall document flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline navigation element at the end of the scroll; no sticky or floating behavior. It moves naturally with the page content.

name: FAQ
title: FAQ
description: Page for viewing and searching frequently asked questions about chalet stays, store orders, gift vouchers, and managing bookings, using dynamic FAQ content from the backend.

core_features: Load all FAQ entries from the backend using getFaqEntries and keep them in page state for filtering and display.
Group and filter FAQ entries by their category_key (such as chalet-related, store-related, gift voucher-related, and manage-booking topics).
Provide keyword search across questions and answers, updating the visible FAQ list in real time.
Support deep-linking to specific FAQ items via URL parameters so users can land directly on a given question.
Allow expanding and collapsing individual FAQ answers, with state reflected in the URL when needed.
Expose contextual links in answers (such as 'Read full policies') that navigate to policies.html with relevant URL parameters.
Handle empty states when no FAQ entries match the current filters or search query, guiding the user to adjust filters.
user_workflows: Browse FAQs by category: 1) User opens faq.html without URL parameters. 2) The page controller calls getFaqEntries and stores the result. 3) The category filter component derives available categories from category_key values and highlights the default selection (e.g., all categories). 4) The FAQ list component shows all entries that match the selected category and no search query. 5) User clicks on another category in the category filter. 6) The page updates the URL parameter for category_key and re-filters the FAQ entries shown in the list.

Search within FAQs: 1) User opens faq.html with or without a category selected. 2) User types text into the search input in the FAQ search form. 3) On input change or form submit, the search query is written into a query URL parameter. 4) The page controller recomputes the filtered FAQ collection using both category_key (if present) and the query string, matching against question and answer fields from getFaqEntries. 5) The FAQ list updates to show only matching entries. 6) If no entries match, a no-results component is displayed.

Open and read a single FAQ answer: 1) User sees a list of questions in the FAQ list. 2) User clicks a question title (FAQ item header). 3) The FAQ item toggles its expanded state, revealing the answer text. 4) The page controller updates a faqId URL parameter (or an expanded list parameter) so the opened item can be restored if the user refreshes or shares the URL. 5) Clicking the same question again collapses it and updates the URL to remove or adjust the corresponding parameter.

Deep-link to a specific FAQ: 1) User arrives on faq.html with a faqId URL parameter (and optionally a category_key). 2) The page controller calls getFaqEntries. 3) Once data is loaded, the controller locates the FAQ entry with the matching id. 4) The category filter is updated to reflect the category_key for that FAQ (if category_key is present and valid). 5) The FAQ list scroll manager expands and scrolls to the matching FAQ item. 6) The item stays expanded so the user can immediately read the answer.

Open full policy page from an FAQ answer: 1) User expands an FAQ whose answer text includes a 'Read full policies' link. 2) User clicks this link within the FAQ item. 3) The link handler constructs a URL to policies.html, optionally including URL parameters such as source=faq and the current faqId. 4) Browser navigates to policies.html with those parameters, allowing the policies page to show the relevant section.

Adjust filters and share the FAQ view: 1) User selects a category and enters a search query, and optionally opens a specific FAQ item. 2) The page controller updates URL parameters (category_key, query, faqId or open ids) to reflect the current view. 3) User copies the page URL and shares it. 4) When another user opens the shared URL, the page controller reads the parameters, applies the same category and query filters, and expands any specified FAQ item so the shared context is preserved.
interactions: On page load, the FAQ page controller reads URL parameters (e.g., category_key, query, faqId) and calls getFaqEntries to fetch all FAQs.
User clicks on a category filter control to change the selected category, which updates the category_key URL parameter and triggers re-filtering of the FAQ list.
User types in the search input and either presses Enter or the search form auto-submits, updating a query URL parameter and re-filtering the FAQs by keyword.
User clicks a FAQ question header to toggle its expanded/collapsed state; the page updates URL parameters to represent the currently expanded FAQ(s).
User clicks a 'Read full policies' link within an FAQ answer, which navigates to policies.html with appropriate URL parameters for context.
User scrolls the page; the scroll manager may adjust which FAQ item is considered active for deep-linking when appropriate (for example, on explicit interaction with the item).
User refreshes the page or lands on the page from an external link; the controller re-applies state based on URL parameters and ensures the correct FAQ(s) are visible and expanded.
page_functionality.state_logic: The FAQ page uses URL parameters as the single source of truth for client-side state. Typical parameters include: 1) category_key: represents the currently selected FAQ category; when present, only FAQ entries whose category_key matches this value are shown (in combination with any active search query). If absent, the page can treat it as 'all categories' and show all FAQs. 2) query: represents the current search term typed by the user; when present, the page filters FAQ entries by matching the query against their question and answer fields (case-insensitively, implementation-defined). Clearing the search input removes the query parameter and restores the unfiltered view. 3) faqId: represents a primary FAQ entry to expand and focus. On load, after getFaqEntries resolves, the controller finds the FAQ with this id, ensures its category is selected, expands that item, and scrolls it into view. Changing the expanded item updates faqId accordingly. 4) open: optionally, a comma-separated list of FAQ ids that are currently expanded, allowing multiple items to be open at once. When the user expands or collapses an item, the open parameter is updated. If only one item should be open at a time, open can be kept synchronized with faqId. 5) source and other contextual parameters: when leaving the FAQ page via a link to policies.html, the link may append source=faq and faqId to the policies.html URL so the policies page can interpret that the user came from a specific FAQ. The FAQ page does not use localStorage; all state that needs to persist across refreshes or be shareable is encoded in the URL query string. The page controller listens for URL changes (e.g., browser navigation actions) and re-applies filters and expanded states accordingly without reloading FAQ data unless necessary.

components.functionality: Coordinates loading FAQ data via getFaqEntries, interprets URL parameters into internal state (selected category, search query, expanded items), computes filtered lists, and passes derived data and callbacks to child components.

Derives and displays the set of available FAQ categories from the category_key values returned by getFaqEntries. Allows the user to select a category, updates URL state, and notifies the controller to re-filter the list.
