Provides a text input for searching FAQ content. Reads initial value from the query URL parameter, updates the URL on change or submit, and triggers the controller to filter FAQs by keyword.

Displays the list of FAQ items computed by the controller, filtered by category_key and query. For each entry, renders a faq-item component with data from getFaqEntries and expansion state derived from URL parameters.

Represents a single FAQ entry with a clickable question header and a collapsible answer section. Uses the entry's id, question, answer, and category_key from getFaqEntries. Manages local expand/collapse behavior in response to clicks while delegating URL state updates to the controller.

Intercepts clicks on 'Read full policies' links within FAQ answers and constructs a navigation URL to policies.html, including contextual query parameters such as source=faq and the current FAQ entry id, then triggers client-side navigation.

Shows an informative message when the filtered FAQ collection is empty based on the current category_key and query parameters, and may offer a control to clear filters (which resets category_key and query in the URL).

After data is loaded and the initial filter is applied, checks URL parameters (faqId and open) and ensures that the corresponding FAQ item(s) are expanded and, for the primary faqId, scrolled into view. Also may update faqId when the user explicitly opens a different FAQ item.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid (Bootstrap-style), with content constrained in a centered max-width container and nav elements arranged in columns (logo, nav links, cart) within the same grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The FAQ page adopts the same stacked structure as the mockup: a slim full-width blue contact strip at the very top, a white navigation bar directly beneath it, and then a single main content column centered in the viewport. Inside this content column, the FAQ page title is centered at the top, followed by a vertically stacked sequence: search form, category filters, FAQ list (or no-results state), and inline policy links within answers. All components flow in a linear top-to-bottom order, with no heavy multi-column layouts or persistent sidebars intruding on the reading flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The FAQ page adopts the same stacked structure as the mockup: a slim full-width blue contact strip at the very top, a white navigation bar directly beneath it, and then a single main content column centered in the viewport. Inside this content column, the FAQ page title is centered at the top, followed by a vertically stacked sequence: search form, category filters, FAQ list (or no-results state), and inline policy links within answers. All components flow in a linear top-to-bottom order, with no heavy multi-column layouts or persistent sidebars intruding on the reading flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header stack (contact strip + nav bar) occupies about 10–15% of the viewport height. Below this, the main content area is a centered column that spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, leaving 15–20% neutral whitespace margins on each side. Within this column, the title, search, filters, and list each occupy the full column width; category filters behave like a light-weight, row-based control area rather than a bulky sidebar. Vertical spacing follows the 8px scale: 32–48px padding at the top and bottom of the content area, 24–32px gaps between major sections, and 16px between related elements (e.g., between questions).
layout_strategies.content_density: Text is presented in a moderately spacious, document-like format, mirroring the terms/policy page. Line height is comfortable for reading, with 16px spacing between paragraphs and 24–32px between FAQ items. FAQ questions and answers use ample padding inside each item so interactive elements don’t feel crowded. Overall, the density supports scanning questions quickly while still feeling calm and uncluttered—no visual noise, no advertising blocks, and no dense multi-column layouts.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts with the brand-driven header strip and navigation, then drops the eye directly onto the centered FAQ page title. From there, attention moves down to the search bar as the primary interactive control, then to the category filters immediately beneath it for a secondary method of narrowing content. Below that, the eye flows linearly down the left-aligned FAQ questions and their answers. Inline policy links within answers use brand blue to stand out subtly, and the scroll behavior ensures that when a specific FAQ is targeted, it appears clearly just below the fixed header, maintaining a strong vertical reading rhythm.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s consistent top contact strip and navigation bar, the FAQ page opens into a calm, centered content column against a white background. The column is relatively wide on desktop—around two-thirds of the viewport—framed by generous side margins that keep focus on the text. A bold, centered page title announces the FAQ, with open space above and below so it doesn’t compete with the navigation. Directly beneath, a single, prominent search bar is centered in the column, inviting visitors to type questions. Aligned under that, the category filter row anchors to the left edge of the content column, displaying a tidy line (or two lines) of pill-style category selectors. These controls sit on a clear band of whitespace that visually separates them from the FAQ content below. The main FAQ list then runs downward as a sequence of full-width question blocks, each left-aligned and evenly spaced, creating a simple reading path from top to bottom. Answers expand in place under their questions, with comfortable padding and line spacing for easy reading. When there are no results, a centered empty state message appears exactly where the list would normally be, using whitespace to emphasize the feedback. Throughout scrolling, the fixed top header remains, while the scroll manager ensures that any focused FAQ item aligns neatly below it, preserving an uninterrupted, document-like reading experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ page controller defines the main content region that begins just below the fixed header stack (blue contact strip + white navigation bar). It creates a centered content column with a max-width of approximately 960–1040px, occupying about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop and shrinking proportionally on smaller devices. This column is horizontally centered, leaving roughly 15–20% neutral whitespace on both left and right sides. Vertically, the controller applies about 32–48px top padding from the bottom edge of the navigation bar to the page title, and similar padding at the bottom before the page footer. Within this column, it stacks the page title, then the search form directly underneath, followed by the category filter area, and finally the FAQ list (or no-results state) in a single, uninterrupted vertical flow. All child components are constrained to this column’s width and alignment, ensuring a coherent, document-like layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static container that scrolls naturally with the page; its top offset accounts for the fixed header height so content never sits underneath the navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category filter sits inside the main content column, directly below the search form and above the FAQ list. It spans the full width of the content column but visually anchors to the left edge. The filter options are arranged in a horizontal row of pill-like buttons, wrapping to a second line if there are many categories. On wider screens, the row sits roughly 24px below the search bar and 24–32px above the first FAQ item, forming a clear, low-profile control band. Each pill has modest horizontal padding (about 12–16px) and vertical padding (8px), with 8–12px gaps between pills, maintaining a clean, breathable look. On smaller screens, the filter row can become horizontally scrollable while still hugging the left edge, keeping the appearance light and avoiding a heavy sidebar feel. The entire filter section reads as a subtle, secondary toolbar rather than a dominant panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page as a normal block; it is not sticky. On narrow viewports it may present as a horizontal scroll row while remaining in the same vertical position relative to the search form and FAQ list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search form is positioned near the top of the content column, immediately under the centered FAQ page title. It is horizontally centered within the column and spans about 60–70% of the column width, providing a long, single-line input that visually invites text entry. There is roughly 24–32px of vertical space between the bottom of the page title and the top of the search field, giving it room to breathe. The search input itself has comfortable internal padding (around 12–16px vertically and 16–24px horizontally), with a flat, minimal border style consistent with the corporate aesthetic. Any associated search button or icon is aligned to the right inside the input or directly adjacent to the right, but kept within the same 60–70% width zone. Beneath the form, about 16–24px of space separates it from the category filter section, keeping the top of the page visually structured: title, then search, then filters, in clear vertical steps.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element that scrolls normally; on smaller screens it stretches closer to 90–100% of the content column width while remaining centered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ list occupies the full width of the main content column and begins directly beneath the category filter section. It is left-aligned within the column and stacks FAQ items vertically, one after another, in a single column. A vertical gap of about 24–32px separates the filter area from the first FAQ item, reinforcing the transition from controls to content. Within the list, each FAQ item is separated by 16–24px of vertical space so individual questions are distinct but still feel part of a continuous document. The list extends downward as far as needed, with the column maintaining consistent left and right alignment all the way, preserving a straight reading line and preventing any side-by-side content competition.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A standard scrolling list that grows in height as more FAQs are loaded. It does not fix or virtualize items; items flow naturally with the page scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each FAQ item appears as a full-width block within the main content column. The question line sits at the top of the block, left-aligned, using a slightly bolder weight to differentiate it from the answer text. The block has internal padding on all sides—approximately 16–24px—to create a subtle card-like feel while still sitting on the same white background as the page. The question and any disclosure icon (such as a chevron) are aligned horizontally, with the icon on the far right of the block, leaving the question text to dominate the left two-thirds of the row. When expanded, the answer text appears directly beneath the question, indented slightly or aligned with the question text, and spans the full width of the block’s content area. The answer body uses comfortable line height and 16px spacing between paragraphs, maintaining the document-like readability. Each FAQ item’s top edge aligns with the left margin of the column, and there is 16–24px of vertical gap between the bottom of one item and the top of the next, creating a clear, rhythmic vertical progression down the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Collapsible/expandable within its own block; when opened via links or search results, it may be scrolled into view by the scroll manager so that its top sits just below the fixed header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policies link handler manifests within the body text of FAQ answers as inline text links. These links are placed inside the answer paragraphs of each FAQ item, left-aligned and following the normal text flow. They use the brand’s blue color (and optionally a subtle underline) to distinguish them from regular text, but remain consistent in font size and line height so they don’t break the document-like reading flow. Spatially, they occupy no extra vertical space; they simply appear in-line and may be followed by standard punctuation and line breaks, behaving like any other word in the paragraph. When activated, they trigger navigation to related policy or terms pages while preserving the underlying layout structure of the FAQ page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, non-block elements within answer text; they don’t affect layout dimensions and scroll naturally with their parent FAQ item.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The no-results state appears in the exact area reserved for the FAQ list, centered within the main content column when there are no matching FAQs for the current search or filters. It is a single block aligned to the center horizontally, with its content (an icon, headline, and optional supporting text) stacked vertically. There is generous vertical padding—approximately 32–48px—above and below this block to emphasize the empty state and prevent it from feeling cramped. The headline is centered and uses slightly larger type than regular body text, with 16px spacing before any additional explanatory text lines. This block starts at roughly the same vertical position where the first FAQ item would normally appear (24–32px below the filter area), ensuring continuity in the layout and minimizing visual jumps when results appear or disappear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Displays conditionally in place of the FAQ list; scrolls as a normal block element within the content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The scroll manager operates behind the scenes but has a spatial impact on how FAQ items appear when targeted. When a user navigates to a specific FAQ (e.g., via a direct link, search result, or filter interaction), the scroll manager adjusts the page scroll so that the selected FAQ item is positioned comfortably below the fixed header—typically leaving a buffer of about 16–24px between the top of the item and the bottom edge of the navigation bar. This ensures the item’s question line is fully visible and aligned with the left edge of the content column, maintaining the clean reading grid. Optionally, it may also control a subtle ‘Back to top’ affordance that appears near the bottom-right corner of the viewport within the main content area, offset about 24–32px from the edges; if present, this control is small and unobtrusive, floating over the white background without disturbing the core layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Controls scroll positioning to prevent content from being hidden under the fixed header; any optional floating controls it manages remain anchored to the viewport (sticky to the bottom-right) but small and visually unobtrusive.

name: Policies
title: Policies
description: Displays all policy documents (e.g., booking, privacy, store, gift voucher policies), allows users to navigate between them, deep-link to specific policies via URL parameters, search within policies, and navigate back to the FAQ page.

core_features: Load all available policy documents by calling the getPolicies interface on page initialization.
Display a selectable list of all policies (using their returned titles) for quick navigation.
Show the full content of a single active policy at a time, based on user selection or URL parameters.
Support deep-linking to a specific policy via a URL parameter that identifies the policy key.
Allow users to search policies by keyword, updating the visible list and content view according to the search query.
Generate in-page anchor navigation (when possible) based on headings within the active policy content.
Provide a 'Back to FAQs' navigation action from within the active policy section that routes to faq.html with relevant URL parameters.
user_workflows:
View all policies:
1. User navigates to policies.html.
2. On page load, the policies-data-loader component calls getPolicies.
3. The response array of policy objects is stored in page state and passed to other components.
4. policy-section-list displays all policies using their returned titles.
5. policy-content-viewer shows the first policy (or the one indicated by the URL parameter if present).

Open a specific policy from an external link (deep-link):
1. User navigates to policies.html with a URL parameter (e.g., policies.html?policyKey={someKey}).
2. policies-data-loader calls getPolicies.
3. Once policies are loaded, the loader searches for the policy whose key matches the policyKey URL parameter.
4. If a match is found, that policy becomes the active policy in page state; if not, the first policy is used as a fallback.
5. policy-section-list highlights the active policy; policy-content-viewer renders its content.
6. policy-anchor-navigation parses the active content (if possible) and generates in-page anchors for headings.

Switch between policies from the list:
1. User clicks a policy title in policy-section-list.
2. The click handler identifies the clicked policy's key and sets it as the active policy in page state.
3. The handler updates the URL query string (e.g., ?policyKey={clickedKey}&q={currentSearchQueryIfAny}) without reloading the page.
4. policy-content-viewer re-renders to show the newly active policy content.
5. policy-anchor-navigation re-parses the active content to update available anchors.

Search within policies:
1. User types a keyword into policy-search-form and submits the form (or uses live search on input change, depending on implementation).
2. The search handler updates the q URL parameter with the current search term.
3. policy-search-form updates its internal state and re-renders the input value from the URL-derived state.
4. policy-section-list filters or reorders the policy list based on whether the search term matches each policy's title or content.
5. If the currently active policy no longer matches the search, the first matching policy becomes active; if there is no match, the page explicitly indicates no matching policies (e.g., an empty list and/or a message) while keeping the last active policy content as-is or clearing the content view (implementation choice defined by functional spec).

Use in-page anchors for a long policy:
1. After policies are loaded and an active policy is selected, policy-anchor-navigation scans the active policy content for structural markers (e.g., heading elements in the HTML or markdown that can be mapped to anchors).
2. It generates a list of anchor links corresponding to sections inside the policy.
3. User clicks an anchor link in policy-anchor-navigation.
4. The click handler scrolls the policy-content-viewer to the corresponding section and may update the URL with a section parameter (e.g., &section={sectionId}).

Return to FAQs from a policy:
1. While viewing an active policy in policy-content-viewer, user clicks the Back to FAQs action (provided by back-to-faq-link component inside or adjacent to the content area).
2. The click handler reads the current active policy key and any existing search/section parameters.
3. The browser navigates to faq.html with query parameters that preserve context (e.g., faq.html?from=policies&policyKey={activeKey}).
4. The FAQ page can then interpret these parameters to show relevant FAQ entries (implementation on faq.html).
interactions: Initial data loading: On page initialization, policies-data-loader automatically invokes getPolicies with no parameters and stores the resulting policy array in a shared state accessible to child components.
Policy selection from list: Clicking a policy title in policy-section-list sets that policy as active and updates the policyKey URL parameter.
Policy selection via URL change: If the URL query string changes (e.g., browser back/forward navigation), a URL-change listener updates the active policy in state to match the new policyKey (if any).

Search input change: Typing into the search field in policy-search-form updates a local input state; on submit (or on change, if live search), the component writes the q parameter to the URL and triggers list/content filtering logic.

Search-based filtering: policy-section-list reads the current search term from shared state (derived from URL q parameter) and filters the list of policies; policy-content-viewer may switch active policy to the first matching result when appropriate.
Anchor navigation click: Clicking an item in policy-anchor-navigation triggers scroll-to-section behavior in policy-content-viewer and may update the section URL parameter.
Back to FAQs click: Clicking the Back to FAQs link triggers navigation to faq.html, including context parameters gathered from current page state (e.g., active policy key, search term, or section).
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is entirely derived from the getPolicies response and URL query parameters. No localStorage is used.

URL parameters:
- policyKey: String. Identifies which policy to display as active. After getPolicies resolves, the page selects the policy whose key field matches policyKey. If policyKey is absent or does not match any returned policy, the first policy in the array is used as the default active policy. Whenever the user selects a different policy from the list, this parameter is updated in the URL.
- q: String. Represents the current search term. On page load, if q is present, policy-search-form initializes its input value from q. policy-section-list uses q to filter or highlight policies based on title and/or content. When the user submits or changes the search, q is updated in the URL.
- section: String. Optionally identifies a subsection within the active policy for deep-linking to a heading or anchor. After the active policy is selected and rendered, policy-anchor-navigation (or the content viewer) scrolls to the specified section if it exists. When the user clicks an anchor link inside policy-anchor-navigation, section is updated accordingly.
- from: String. Used when arriving from or returning to other pages (e.g., 'faq'). It does not affect which policies are loaded, but may be used to conditionally show contextual information (e.g., showing or hiding the Back to FAQs link depending on whether from=faq was present). When back-to-faq-link is clicked, it sets from=policies in the outgoing URL to faq.html.

State derivation:
1. On initial load, the page parses the current URL query parameters (policyKey, q, section, from) into an internal state object.
2. policies-data-loader calls getPolicies and stores the result as policies[] in state.
3. Once policies[] is available, activePolicyKey is determined:
   - If a valid policyKey from URL matches a policies[i].key, activePolicyKey = that key.
   - Otherwise, activePolicyKey = policies[0].key (or null if the array is empty).
4. SearchTerm is set from the q URL parameter (or empty string if q is absent).
5. ActiveSectionId is set from the section URL parameter (or null if absent).
6. All components derive their behavior from this shared state; changes initiated by user interactions update both the internal state and the URL query parameters using client-side navigation (e.g., history API) without reloading the page.

components.functionality: On page initialization, fetches all policy documents using getPolicies, stores them in a shared page state (policies[]), and triggers computation of the active policy based on the URL policyKey parameter.
event_handlers: onInit: Called when the page is first loaded; invokes getPolicies and waits for the array of policy objects.
onPoliciesLoaded: After getPolicies resolves, updates shared state with policies[], determines activePolicyKey based on the current URL policyKey parameter, and notifies dependent components.
onUrlParamChange: Listens for changes to the URL query string (e.g., via browser back/forward); if policyKey changes, updates activePolicyKey in shared state to match the new value (if valid).

components.functionality: Displays a navigable list of all available policies, using their titles from the getPolicies result. Allows users to click a policy to make it active, updates the URL policyKey parameter, and visually indicates which policy is currently active. Optionally applies search-based filtering or highlighting based on the q URL parameter.
event_handlers: onPoliciesChanged: Re-renders the list when policies[] from shared state changes.
onActivePolicyChanged: Updates the selected/highlighted item when activePolicyKey changes in shared state.
onSearchTermChanged: Reads the search term derived from the q URL parameter and filters or highlights list items accordingly.

onPolicyClick: When a user clicks a policy item, sets activePolicyKey to the clicked item's key in shared state and updates the URL query string with policyKey={clickedKey} while preserving existing q and from parameters where appropriate.

components.functionality: Renders the full content of the currently active policy, using the content field returned by getPolicies. Supports HTML or markdown content and exposes hooks for anchor generation and in-page navigation.
event_handlers: onActivePolicyChanged: When activePolicyKey changes, looks up the corresponding policy object from policies[] and re-renders its content.

onContentRendered: After rendering the active policy content, notifies policy-anchor-navigation so it can parse headings and generate anchors; if a section URL parameter is present, scrolls to the corresponding section.
onUrlSectionChanged: When the section URL parameter changes, scrolls within the rendered content to the matching anchor or heading if found.

components.functionality: Analyzes the currently active policy content (e.g., by inspecting headings in the HTML/markdown) and generates an in-page navigation list of anchors. Clicking an anchor scrolls the policy-content-viewer to the relevant section and updates the section URL parameter.
event_handlers: onContentStructureUpdated: Triggered by policy-content-viewer after rendering; parses the content structure and builds a list of sections with associated IDs or anchor targets.

onAnchorClick: When a user clicks an anchor item, scrolls policy-content-viewer to the target section and updates the URL query string with section={targetId} while preserving existing policyKey, q, and from parameters as appropriate.

components.functionality: Provides a text input for users to search within policies. The search term updates the q URL parameter and influences which policies are shown or highlighted in policy-section-list, and optionally which policy is active in policy-content-viewer.
event_handlers: onInit: Reads the initial q parameter from the URL and sets the input field value accordingly.
onInputChange: Updates local input state as the user types; may optionally trigger live search by updating q in the URL immediately.

onSubmit: When the search form is submitted, writes the current input value to the q URL parameter and updates shared searchTerm state, then notifies policy-section-list and policy-content-viewer to adjust their behavior.
onClearSearch: When a clear action is triggered, removes q from the URL, resets searchTerm in shared state to an empty value, and causes policy-section-list to display all policies again.

components.functionality: Provides a Back to FAQs action from within or adjacent to the active policy content. When clicked, navigates to faq.html and passes context information via URL parameters so the FAQ page can react accordingly.
event_handlers: onRender: Determines whether to show the Back to FAQs link based on optional conditions, such as the presence or absence of from=faq in the incoming URL (implementation choice).

onClick: Reads the current active policy key (if available) and other context (e.g., searchTerm or section), then navigates the browser to faq.html with query parameters such as from=policies and policyKey set to the active policy key.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid (Bootstrap-style), with content constrained in a centered max-width container and nav elements arranged in columns (logo, nav links, cart) within the same grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing global header (blue contact strip + white navigation bar), the Policies page uses a single, centered content column that drives a strictly top-to-bottom reading flow. Within this column, the page title sits at the top, followed by a compact block of utility controls (back to FAQ, search form, and policy list navigation) that help the user choose what to read. Beneath these controls, an in-page anchor navigation introduces the currently selected policy, with the main policy content flowing linearly below it as a continuous, document-style text block. No sidebars or competing columns are introduced; all components are stacked vertically in one column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing global header (blue contact strip + white navigation bar), the Policies page uses a single, centered content column that drives a strictly top-to-bottom reading flow. Within this column, the page title sits at the top, followed by a compact block of utility controls (back to FAQ, search form, and policy list navigation) that help the user choose what to read. Beneath these controls, an in-page anchor navigation introduces the currently selected policy, with the main policy content flowing linearly below it as a continuous, document-style text block. No sidebars or competing columns are introduced; all components are stacked vertically in one column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header occupies about the top 10–15% of the viewport. The main content column sits centered in the remaining vertical space, constrained to roughly 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, leaving 15–20% whitespace margins on each side. Within this column, the title and main content viewer receive the most vertical space. Utility elements (back link, search form, policy section list, anchor navigation) occupy relatively shallow horizontal bands, separated by 16–32px vertical gaps. On smaller screens, the column expands to near full width while preserving vertical spacing and hierarchy.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains a moderately spacious, text-first density suited to legal and policy reading. Paragraphs in the content viewer have generous line height and approximately 16px spacing between them. Section breaks, headings, and component transitions (e.g., between search form and list navigation, or anchor navigation and content viewer) use 24–32px vertical spacing to visually segment functions without feeling sparse. Interactive elements like links and list items use 8–16px internal padding to remain comfortable and scannable without appearing bulky.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: After the eye is drawn across the existing header (contact strip, logo, main navigation, cart), attention drops to the centered page title. From there, the visual flow moves down to the left-aligned back-to-FAQ link and search form, then to the policy list navigation where the user chooses a document. Once a policy is selected, the eye naturally progresses to the anchor navigation that previews its internal structure, and finally into the main content viewer where the numbered section headings act as strong vertical anchors. When data is loading, a semi-opaque overlay and centered loader briefly interrupt this flow, then relinquish focus back to the content column once content is ready.

layout.overall_description: Below the fixed (or top-stacked) resort-branded header, the Policies page opens into a clean, white main area with a single centered content column floating in generous side margins. At the very top of this column, the word “Policies” appears as a bold, centered page title, visually anchoring the page. Directly beneath, content shifts to left alignment: a subtle “Back to FAQ” text link sits at the top-left edge of the column, followed by a wider, prominently styled search form that spans most of the column’s width. Below this, a vertically stacked list of policy titles allows users to choose which document to view, each item separated by consistent white space. Once a policy is selected, a compact anchor navigation area appears just above the main text, presenting section-level links in a lighter, understated style. The main policy content fills the remainder of the column as a legal-document-like scroll: numbered headings, bolded section titles, and well-spaced paragraphs flowing straight down the page. Throughout, the background remains largely white with occasional blue accents on interactive elements, preserving a modern, minimal corporate feel. When content is loading or switching, a translucent overlay with a centered loader temporarily sits above this column, dimming it slightly while keeping the header visually intact.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader is an unobtrusive overlay that appears above the main content column whenever policies are being fetched or switched. Visually, it covers the entire viewport or, at minimum, the area from the bottom of the main navigation bar down to the bottom of the screen, leaving the header still visible at the top. The overlay uses a semi-transparent white or light-tinted background that mutes the underlying content without fully hiding it. At the center of the viewport (horizontally and vertically), a small spinner or progress indicator appears, with a minimal label such as “Loading policies…” if needed. The loader does not alter the layout of other components; it simply floats above them, temporarily taking visual focus while maintaining the perception of a single, centered content column underneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Displayed only during data loading states as a fixed-position overlay; it sits on top of all content, pinned to the viewport, and scroll is typically disabled or discouraged while active. When hidden, it occupies no space in the normal document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy section list, which allows users to choose between different policy documents (e.g., booking, privacy, store, gift voucher), is positioned within the main content column beneath the search form. It is left-aligned and spans nearly the full width of the column (approximately 90–100% of the column width), with 24–32px of vertical space separating it from the search form above and about 24px separating it from the anchor navigation or main content below. On desktop, the list appears as a vertical stack of full-width clickable rows or pill-like buttons: each item has 16–20px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, with 8–12px vertical gaps between items. Text labels are left-aligned within each row, with optional subtle blue accent on hover/active states to echo the brand color. On tablets and mobile, the list maintains a single-column, full-width layout within the main column, simply expanding to match the narrower screen while preserving the same vertical rhythm and tap-friendly spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level component that participates in the normal vertical flow of the single content column. It does not stick or float; as the user scrolls down, it scrolls off-screen naturally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy content viewer is the primary reading area and occupies the lower portion of the main content column. It sits directly beneath the anchor navigation (if present) or directly below the policy section list if there is no anchor navigation, separated from the preceding component by approximately 32px of vertical space to signal a transition into the main document area. The viewer spans the full width of the content column, with its internal text constrained by a comfortable line length based on the column width (about 60–70% of viewport on desktop). Inside the viewer, the policy title or current policy name may appear as a bold heading at the top (left-aligned), followed by numbered subsections where each section heading is bold and separated from the preceding paragraph by 24–32px. Paragraphs are left-aligned, with generous line height and about 16px space between them, keeping the content readable and uncluttered. There are no sidebars or inline panels; all text flows in a single vertical thread. On smaller screens, the viewer expands to nearly full screen width while preserving vertical spacing, ensuring a consistent, document-like reading experience.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content area within the main column, scrolling normally with the page. It does not stick or float; the user scrolls through its content as through a standard long document.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy anchor navigation is a compact, in-page navigation block that appears within the main content column directly above the policy content viewer. It is left-aligned with the rest of the text, spans roughly 90–100% of the content column width, and is separated from the policy section list above by about 24px of vertical space. For a selected policy, it presents its top-level sections (e.g., numbered headings) as a series of concise links arranged horizontally where space allows, wrapping onto additional lines if needed, or as a short vertical list if that better fits the text volume. Each link has modest horizontal padding (8–12px) and vertical spacing (4–8px), with minimal styling—typically plain text or subtle pills with blue accents on hover/active—to keep it visually lighter than the main content. A thin bottom border or a slightly shaded background strip can be used to gently separate this navigation from the content viewer below without introducing heavy visual weight. On narrow screens, links stack naturally and remain within the same single column, maintaining an unobtrusive yet accessible table-of-contents feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Normally scrolls with the page as part of the document flow. Optionally, on larger screens it may become sticky within the main content area (sticking to the top of the viewport just below the main navigation bar) once scrolled into position, but this is subtle and confined to the content column, not the full viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy search form is placed near the top of the main content column, immediately below the page title and back-to-FAQ link cluster. Vertically, there is around 16–24px of space between the page title and the search form group. The search form itself consists of a single search input field (optionally with a search icon) that spans about 70–80% of the content column width on desktop. It can be centered within the column or left-aligned; in either case, it visually reads as a prominent but not overwhelming bar beneath the title. The input has 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, with a subtle border and light shadow or outline to distinguish it from the white background. On mobile and tablet, the search form expands to nearly full width of the column (close to 100% minus internal padding), and remains directly above the policy section list. A comfortable vertical gap (24–32px) separates the search form from the policy section list to maintain a clear functional grouping.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element participating in the normal vertical flow. It does not stick; as users scroll into the policy content, the search form scrolls out of view in a natural top-to-bottom progression.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-FAQ link is a subtle, tertiary navigation element located near the top of the main content column, just beneath the centered page title. It is left-aligned with the body text, positioned at the top-left edge of the column. There is a small vertical gap (8–12px) between the bottom of the title and the link, making it feel associated with the title but clearly distinct. The link itself is a simple text label (e.g., “Back to FAQ”) possibly accompanied by a left-pointing arrow icon, styled in the brand blue to signal interactivity while remaining light in weight compared to headings. It occupies minimal vertical space—essentially a single line of text—with 4–8px of padding or breathing room above and below. On all screen sizes, the link remains in this same relative position at the very start of the content column, preceding the search form and policy section list, and it flows off-screen naturally as the user scrolls down into the policies.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline/block-level link at the top of the content column, following normal document flow. It does not stick or float; it scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page.
